Alaa Abdelnaby was born on June 24, 1968, in Alexandria, Egypt — the ancient Mediterranean port three thousand miles and an entire civilization away from the ACC. His father was an engineer. His mother was a computer analyst. They were educated, ambitious, Muslim, and in 1971 they brought their two-year-old son to America in search of better careers. The family settled in northern New Jersey — first Nutley, then Bloomfield — and became American citizens. He is almost certainly the only native of Alexandria, Egypt, to ever play in the Atlantic Coast Conference.

Growing up in the 1970s, Abdelnaby was the only kid in the neighborhood named Alaa. In a 2024 NBC Philadelphia interview during Arab American Heritage Month, he described the quiet burden of difference: ‘When I was young, I used to tell people my name was Al, cause I just wanted to fit in. And Alaa in ’73 and ’74 was just a little too different for the youngsters.’ Basketball became his vehicle for belonging.

His parents saw him as a doctor. They didn’t come to his games. ‘They had two other kids to raise. I don’t think they even wanted to encourage me. If I came then I’m approving of this. So you go to do your thing. And at times, I wanted them there ’cause other parents were there.’ But as he grew to 6-foot-10, his parents slowly relented.

Moving from Nutley to Bloomfield was hard. ‘It was tough. Having to leave all your childhood friends and move to a new school was intimidating. I always felt like an outsider until basketball season started my junior year.’ Once basketball season started, everybody knew who he was. In the 1986 Essex County Tournament, he jumped from the foul line and threw down a thunderous dunk against Montclair — a moment Bloomfield fans still talk about decades later. Over 1,000 points in two varsity seasons. McDonald’s All-American. Parade All-American. His coach, Paul Palek, thought the sky was the limit.
bio.duke:
Abdelnaby arrived at Duke in 1986, and he was, by all accounts, a mess. Obviously talented — 6-10, soft hands, beautiful touch — but strikingly immature. Duke Basketball Report put it bluntly: ‘He was clearly immature and took a while to grow up.’ He partied heavily. He drove his car into a tree on campus. He joked about academics with a line that became Duke basketball legend: ‘The only way I can make five A’s is when I sign my name.’

Freshman through junior year: role player, single-digit scoring, more turnovers per game than assists, steals, and blocks combined. Duke made the Final Four in 1988 and 1989 with Abdelnaby as a supporting player, described as ‘a gifted but unpredictable 6-10 forward’ on Danny Ferry’s team.

Then senior year, 1989–90, everything changed. He has said that mentoring freshmen Christian Laettner and Bobby Hurley — teaching them how to deal with Coach K — transformed him into a better player and man. The numbers exploded: 15.1 PPG, 6.6 RPG, 62% FG, leading the ACC in true shooting percentage. Started 37 of 38 games. All-ACC Third Team.

The crowning moment: the 1990 East Regional Final at the Meadowlands — his New Jersey backyard — against top-seeded UConn. He called it the best day of his basketball life: ‘I played my best game in front of 86 of my friends and family! That’s a lot of ticket requests! We won, I played well, and I got to run into the stands and hug my parents.’ Game-high 27 points, 14 rebounds. Overtime. UConn’s Tate George airballed a three that fell into Abdelnaby’s hands. With 2.6 seconds left, Coach K called an audible — “Special” — and Laettner hit a buzzer-beater to send Duke to the Final Four.

Two weeks later: UNLV 103, Duke 73. The worst championship game loss in history. Abdelnaby had 14 points and 7 rebounds. But the freshmen he mentored — Laettner, Hurley, joined by Grant Hill — returned to beat UNLV in the 1991 rematch and win back-to-back championships. Abdelnaby’s class built the bridge. They were the last team to lose before the dynasty began.
bio.after:
Draft night was his toughest day. Told to expect picks 12–16, he fell to 25th (Portland). ‘To go 25th was disappointing! I did get picked by a great team that won a lot, so everything worked out.’

Portland was loaded: Drexler, Porter, Buck Williams. Abdelnaby was a deep reserve on a 63-win squad. His best Portland year, 1991–92: 71 games, 6.1 PPG. The Blazers reached the NBA Finals, losing to Jordan’s Bulls. Traded to Milwaukee for Tracy Murray’s rights. Traded again to Boston for Jon Barry. With the Celtics in 1992–93: 63 games, 52 starts, 8.2 PPG — his largest NBA role. Back surgery limited him to 13 games the next season. Sacramento, two 10-day contracts in Philadelphia, a Golden State signing that never produced a game. Five NBA seasons, five teams, ~256 games, 5.7 PPG.

But he wasn’t done. Greece (Papagou), CBA (Omaha Racers), France (Olympique Antibes), CBA again (Idaho Stampede, 2000) — a decade after leaving Duke, still chasing the game across continents.

The second career began while the first was sputtering. In 1995, the NBA asked Abdelnaby to broadcast the All-Star Game in Phoenix for Orbit Satellite Television. He was the league’s only Arabic-speaking former player. He called the game in Arabic — bringing the NBA to millions across the Middle East and North Africa. He continued with Orbit, then ART (2005–15) and NBA TV (2006–09). The kid who’d shortened his name to “Al” to fit in was now broadcasting basketball in Arabic to the Arab world.
bio.now:
Since October 2015, Alaa Abdelnaby has been the color analyst for the Philadelphia 76ers on NBC Sports Philadelphia — first alongside Marc Zumoff, now with Kate Scott. Also a CBS Sports Network studio analyst and Westwood One Radio color commentator. His Twitter bio: ‘Egyptian born, Jersey raised. Duke alum. Got paid to play. Now, I get paid to watch!’

The 2024–25 season marked his tenth year with the Sixers. He is one of the very few Arab and Muslim voices in NBA broadcasting. ‘I think it’s been my underlying desire all my life to fit in. I’m fortunate basketball has been a vehicle that has attracted, for the most part, people to me positively.’

Married Celeste Barnette, a fellow Duke graduate, in 2015. Two siblings. His parents — the engineer and the computer analyst who wanted their son to be a doctor, who didn’t come to his high school games because attending would mean approving — now attend his games.

The boy from Alexandria who told kids his name was Al. Who felt like an outsider until basketball season started. Who jumped from the foul line and dunked on Montclair. Who went to Duke and drove his car into a tree and made the famous quip about the five A’s. Who grew up by teaching Laettner and Hurley how to be Blue Devils. Who scored 27 and 14 at the Meadowlands and hugged his parents in the stands. Who got crushed by UNLV 103–73. Who broadcast the All-Star Game in Arabic because no one else could. Who found his voice — literally — courtside in Philadelphia. He is 57. He is still fitting in. The difference is that now, the world has changed enough to call him by his real name.

players.name: Phil Henderson
players.tagline: Three Final Fours. The dunk on Mourning. Co-MVP with Laettner. A good man with a gentle soul. Gone at 44.
players.now: Died February 17, 2013, in Manila, Philippines. Age 44. Cardiac arrest. Basketball Museum of Illinois HOF (2015).
players.stat: 4 Duke seasons • 1,397 pts • 115 games • 3 consecutive Final Fours • 1990 Championship Game • 22.3 PPG in tournament play

bio.road:
Phillip Terry Henderson was born April 17, 1968, in Chicago, Illinois, and grew up in University Park — a small, working-class village in the south suburbs, about forty miles from downtown.

At Crete-Monee High School in Crete, Illinois, Henderson became a sensation. Senior year: 21.8 PPG, 8.7 RPG, 5.5 APG, 3.0 SPG, 1.7 BPG. He led the Warriors to a 24-win season — the best in school history. First Team All-State. McDonald’s All-American. Parade All-American. The best player in the south suburbs of Chicago.

His rival and friend was Kendall Gill, who played at nearby Rich Central. They battled every week at the University Park YMCA. Gill, who would play fifteen NBA seasons, called Henderson his Joe Frazier — the opponent who made him better, the friend whose excellence demanded excellence in return. Gill chose Illinois. Henderson chose Duke.

In the summer of 1986, Henderson arrived at Duke for a six-week precollege program. There he met Robert Brickey, a fellow highly touted freshman from Fayetteville, North Carolina. They played pickup against David Henderson — no relation, a senior guard on Coach K’s team — and from the first day, the bond was forged. They would spend four years side by side and go to three Final Fours together. Brickey would later say Henderson was a fun-loving guy who loved life and loved basketball. That was the whole of it. That was enough.
bio.duke:
Phil Henderson played 115 games at Duke and appeared in three consecutive Final Fours — 1988, 1989, 1990. Few players in Duke history have spent as much time on basketball’s biggest stage. 1,397 career points, 330 rebounds, 217 assists, 128 three-pointers.

The defining moment: March 26, 1989, East Regional Final at the Meadowlands. Duke faced Georgetown — John Thompson, Alonzo Mourning, Dikembe Mutombo. The Hoyas were the dominant team. Duke had freshman Laettner, but he wasn’t yet the Laettner the world would know. Henderson took the ball down the lane. Mourning — 6-10, future Hall of Famer — rose to challenge him. Henderson did not hesitate. He dunked in Mourning’s face. The Meadowlands erupted. As much as any single play, that dunk broke Georgetown’s aura and announced that Duke belonged at the highest level. Coach K referenced it years later when Duke returned to the same building.

Senior year, 1989–90: 18.5 PPG regular season, 22.3 PPG in tournament play. All-Tournament at the ACC Tournament, East Regional, and Final Four. Second-Team All-ACC. Co-MVP with sophomore Christian Laettner — the senior who dunked on Mourning and the sophomore who would become the most famous player in college basketball history, sharing the award shoulder to shoulder. The passing of the torch.

The UNLV championship game: 103–73. The worst loss in Duke history. But the foundation Henderson helped build — the belief, the toughness forged in three consecutive Final Fours — was the ground on which the dynasty stood. The following season, Duke beat UNLV and won the first of back-to-back titles. Henderson had graduated, but the dynasty was built on what he helped create.
bio.after:
Dallas selected Henderson 49th overall in 1990. He never played an NBA game. He played in the CBA, averaging 12.7 PPG. The Global Basketball Association. Professionally in Belgium and Mexico. A basketball life lived in leagues most fans have never heard of, far from the Cameron Indoor roar.

Henderson returned to the south suburbs and founded the All-American Basketball Academy in Tinley Park, Illinois. He ran camps for kids in the community he’d come from. He coached the Crete-Monee boys’ basketball team in summer sessions. Athletic director Gene Cahan: ‘He wanted to get more involved with the community. He was one of them. He was from the neighborhood.’ Henderson taught footwork, pivoting, setting screens — the things nobody wants to work on but everybody needs.

In 2004, he met Shervonne while running coaching clinics at the University of the West Indies in Trinidad and Tobago. They married in 2005, had three children: Adonia, Athalia, and Azriel. He also had two older children, Genesis and Harrison. The marriage lasted four and a half years; they divorced in 2012. Henderson moved to Manila, Philippines, where he became director of the Filipino Basketball Academy, coaching and teaching the game to kids.
bio.now:
On February 17, 2013, Phil Henderson died at his home in Manila of cardiac arrest. He was 44 years old. His girlfriend and children were home. She found him unresponsive. He could not be revived.

Robert Brickey, his teammate since the summer of 1986, got the call and begged the caller to tell him they had the wrong name: ‘Please tell me you’ve got the name wrong, or you went to the wrong apartment, tell me you made a mistake.’ Kendall Gill, who called him his Joe Frazier, said he was grateful to have known him. Gene Cahan said kids looked up to Henderson as a role model. He was from the neighborhood.

Coach K: ‘Phil was a talented player and a good man with a gentle soul. We will miss him dearly.’

A good man with a gentle soul. That is the phrase Coach K chose. Not the dunk on Mourning. Not the 22.3 points in tournament play. Not the three Final Fours. A good man with a gentle soul.

He is survived by five children: Genesis, Harrison, Adonia, Athalia, and Azriel. Inducted into the Basketball Museum of Illinois Hall of Fame in 2015. He was born in Chicago and raised in University Park. He battled Kendall Gill at the YMCA. He was a McDonald’s All-American at Crete-Monee. He arrived at Duke in the summer of 1986 and met Robert Brickey on a pickup court. Three consecutive Final Fours. The dunk on Mourning. Leading scorer on a championship-game team. Co-MVP with Laettner. Drafted by Dallas, never played in the NBA. CBA. Belgium. Mexico. Trinidad. The Philippines. He taught kids to pivot and set screens in Tinley Park. He died at 44, in Manila, teaching basketball. He was a good man with a gentle soul. The Brotherhood lost him too soon.

players.name: Jeff Capel
players.tagline: Held the line when Duke was mortal.
players.stat: 1,601 pts • 433 ast • 220 3s • 4-year starter • The 40-footer vs UNC

bio.road:
Felton Jeffrey Capel III was born February 12, 1975, in Fayetteville, North Carolina, into a basketball family. His father, Jeff Capel II, was a college head coach who built programs at Fayetteville State, North Carolina A&T, and Old Dominion, compiling 201 victories and two NCAA Tournament appearances. Before coaching, the elder Capel had served in the United States Army from 1971 to 1975. Jeff III’s younger brother, Jason, would go on to play four years at Duke’s archrival North Carolina and later became head coach at Appalachian State.

Jeff III grew up immersed in the game. By age three he was shooting baskets. At ten, he attended a Wake Forest team camp with his father’s Pinecrest High squad. By fourteen, he was playing AAU basketball with the Charlotte Sonics, teaming with future NBA players Jerry Stackhouse and Jeff McInnis. In 1992, the Sonics reached the AAU national championship game in Winston-Salem, though an injury sidelined Capel for the final against a team featuring Allen Iverson and Joe Smith.

At South View High School in Hope Mills, North Carolina, Capel became the best player in the state. As a senior in 1993, he led South View to a 31–1 record and the state championship, scoring the game-winning layup with seconds remaining in a 53–52 victory over Charlotte South Mecklenburg. He was named the 1993 North Carolina High School Player of the Year and a Parade All-American, setting school career records in points (2,066), rebounds (668), and assists (663). He chose Duke.
bio.duke:
Capel arrived at Duke in the fall of 1993 and earned a starting position immediately as a freshman guard under Mike Krzyzewski. Senior Grant Hill led the Blue Devils to the 1994 national championship game, where they lost to Arkansas 76–72. Capel scored 14 points in that title game as a freshman. Over four years, he started all 129 games he played.

The defining moment came on February 2, 1995. Duke vs. North Carolina at Cameron Indoor Stadium. With Duke trailing 95–92 at the end of the first overtime, Capel caught the ball near halfcourt and launched a running 40-foot shot at the buzzer. It banked in, sending the game to double overtime. Duke eventually lost 102–100, but Capel’s shot was nominated for an ESPY Award for College Basketball Play of the Year and remains one of the most iconic moments in Cameron Indoor history.

He was a two-year team captain (1996 and 1997) and earned Third-Team All-ACC honors as a junior. He finished his career with 1,601 points, 433 assists, and 220 three-point field goals, ranking among Duke’s all-time top ten in minutes played, three-pointers made, three-point percentage, and assists. He graduated with a B.A. in history in 1997.
bio.after:
Capel went undrafted in 1997. He played two professional seasons: with the Grand Rapids Hoops of the CBA (named to the All-Rookie Team in 1998, averaging 11.9 points in 56 games) and briefly with Élan Chalon in France.

He began his coaching career as an assistant to his father at Old Dominion for the 2000–01 season. When Mack McCarthy resigned from VCU after 2001–02, Capel was promoted to head coach at age 27 — the youngest Division I head coach at the time. In four seasons at VCU, he compiled 79 wins, won the CAA titles in 2004, and led the Rams to their first NCAA Tournament since 1996.

Oklahoma hired Capel in April 2006. His second season produced a 30-win campaign, a No. 2 seed, and an Elite Eight run behind freshman Blake Griffin, who became the No. 1 overall pick. But Griffin’s departure gutted the roster. An NCAA investigation — Capel was not implicated — led to vacated wins. After two losing seasons, Oklahoma fired him in March 2011.

Capel returned to Duke as an assistant, was promoted to associate head coach in 2014, and served as acting head coach during Krzyzewski’s 2017 back surgery recovery (going 4–3). He spent seven seasons on Duke’s staff, helping land 15 McDonald’s All-Americans and a 2015 national championship. On January 25, 2017, Capel published a Players’ Tribune piece revealing his father had been diagnosed with ALS. Jeff Capel II died November 13, 2017, at age 64. Less than five months later, Pittsburgh hired Jeff III as head coach. He inherited a program that had gone 0–18 in ACC play.
bio.now:
Capel is in his eighth season at Pittsburgh. The 2022–23 breakthrough: 24–12, program-best 14–6 ACC record, NCAA Tournament wins over Mississippi State and Iowa State to reach the Round of 32. He won ACC Coach of the Year, USBWA and NABC Regional Coach of the Year. His contract has been extended through 2029–30. The 2025–26 season has been a struggle at 9–16 as of February 2026.

Overall head coaching record: 276–217 across VCU, Oklahoma, and Pittsburgh, with four NCAA Tournament appearances and an Elite Eight. He has been instrumental in the development of 19 first-round NBA Draft picks, including 11 lottery selections. In 2025, he was named to the USA Basketball Men’s Junior National Team Committee.

Capel lives in Pittsburgh with his wife Kanika (married 2003). They have two daughters, Cameron and Sydney, and a son, Elijah. His brother Jason’s career at North Carolina gave the Capel family one of the most unusual split loyalties in ACC history. Jeff Capel’s story is the coaching life in full: a basketball family that lost its patriarch to ALS, a player whose 40-foot buzzer-beater against Carolina is still replayed three decades later, and a coach who has rebuilt programs, developed NBA talent, and endured the cycles of firing and hiring that define the profession.

players.name: Steve Wojciechowski
players.tagline: Heart and hustle in human form.
players.stat: 128 games • 505 ast • 203 stl • NABC DPOY 1998 • McDonald’s All-American

bio.road:
Steven Michael Wojciechowski — pronounced woe-ju-HOW-skee, known to everyone as Wojo — was born August 11, 1976, in Severna Park, Maryland. He was a first-generation college student. He attended Cardinal Gibbons School in Baltimore, where he played under coach Ray Mullis and became one of the top high school point guards in the country. He was named to the 1994 McDonald’s All-American Team, playing against future Duke teammates Trajan Langdon and Ricky Price.

He chose Duke. At 5-foot-11, he was never the most physically gifted player on the floor. But he played with a ferocity that bordered on possession. Dean Smith once expressed his admiration and reminded UNC fans — who had mocked Wojciechowski — that there was a reason he had wanted Wojo in Carolina Blue.
bio.duke:
Wojciechowski spent four seasons at Duke from 1994 to 1998, appearing in 128 games and starting 88. He was the engine: relentless on defense, an orchestrator on offense, and the emotional heartbeat of every team he played on. He ranks eighth in Duke history in both career assists (505) and career steals (203). His 82 steals in 1997 is the second-highest single-season total in program history.

His senior season in 1997–98 was the culmination. He was team captain, leading Duke to a 32–4 record and a No. 1 ranking in the final AP regular season poll. The Blue Devils reached the South Regional final, losing to eventual champion Kentucky 86–84. Wojciechowski was named the 1998 NABC Defensive Player of the Year. He earned Second-Team All-ACC as a junior, Third-Team All-ACC as a senior, and was an Honorable Mention All-American. He graduated with a degree in sociology in 1998.
bio.after:
After Duke, Wojo played professional basketball in Poland for one year. He returned to Duke in 1999, initially in a business role and helping with radio broadcasts before Krzyzewski offered him a coaching position. He became an assistant and was promoted to associate head coach in 2008. Over 14 seasons on the Duke bench, his staff record was 441–92. He coached frontcourt players Shane Battier and Shelden Williams to NABC DPOY awards, and developed Carlos Boozer, Jabari Parker, Mason Plumlee, Miles Plumlee, Rodney Hood, and Lance Thomas. The Blue Devils won NCAA championships in 2001 and 2010.

He served as court coach for USA Basketball from 2006 to 2014, including Olympic gold medals in Beijing (2008) and London (2012), working alongside LeBron James, Kobe Bryant, and Dwyane Wade.

On April 1, 2014, Marquette hired Wojo as head coach. His best season was 2018–19 (24–10, NCAA Tournament). But in seven seasons he went 124–93 with two tournament appearances and zero victories. Marquette fired him after the 2020–21 season. A former Duke assistant, Chris Carrawell, once asked Wojo whether he wanted to win Friday’s practice or Saturday’s game — a comment about the intensity that was both his greatest strength and, perhaps, his limitation as a head coach.
bio.now:
After Marquette, Wojo and his wife Lindsay — a Utah native — moved to Park City. He took time off (he joked about considering teaching hot yoga). In June 2023, he was named head coach of the Salt Lake City Stars, the Jazz’s G League affiliate. He compiled a 41–17 record over two seasons, led the Stars to consecutive playoff appearances, and secured the franchise’s first-ever playoff victory on April 1, 2025.

On June 6, 2025, Jazz head coach Will Hardy announced Wojo would join the Jazz as a full-time assistant. Hardy called him ‘an incredible teacher.’ He is now an NBA assistant coach at age 49, following the same G-League-to-NBA pipeline that Quin Snyder — another Duke point guard — traveled before him.

Wojciechowski lives in Park City with Lindsay and their two sons, Jack and Charlie. His coaching record spans Duke, Marquette, the G League, and now the NBA — a career built on the same relentless intensity he brought as a 5-foot-11 point guard who Dean Smith wanted at North Carolina. The Brotherhood’s purest coaching arc: the player who never had the body but always had the fire.

players.name: Trajan Langdon
players.tagline: The Alaskan Assassin.
players.now: President of Basketball Operations, Detroit Pistons
players.stat: Duke all-time 3PT leader • 2x EuroLeague champ • Final Four MVP • also drafted by MLB Padres

bio.road:
During winter in Anchorage, Alaska, the sun can set by 3:45 in the afternoon. In high school, Trajan Langdon would go outside three hours before a 7 p.m. tipoff. In the dark, he would think about that night’s game. Who he was playing. How they would guard him. He tried to visualize how it would unfold — a technique his father had taught him. ‘Once you get in the game and it’s happening, it will feel like you’ve already played before and you won’t be as stressed, and you will react a lot quicker,’ Langdon said. ‘So why wouldn’t you do that to the mind as well?’

Trajan Shaka Langdon was born May 13, 1976, in Palo Alto, California, and moved to Anchorage as a young child. His mother, Gladys, is a social worker. His father, Dr. Steve Langdon, is a professor of anthropology at the University of Alaska Anchorage who earned his Ph.D. from Stanford in 1977. His life’s work has been the study of the Tlingit people of southeastern Alaska. He authored The Native People of Alaska. Trajan traveled with his father on anthropological trips through southeastern Alaska, into villages where basketball heritages go back to the early days of the sport. His father told Sports Illustrated: ‘The Tlingits are a historically aggressive people. They had armor and everything. Their villages have basketball heritages that go back to the early days of the sport.’ The anthropologist’s view was also the basketball player’s view: Steve Langdon had played among the Tlingits on the Craig town team in the 1970s.

His first name comes from a Roman emperor. His middle name, Shaka, from a Zulu king. His father entered him into track and field and free-throw shooting competitions. Steve prepared pregame meals — grilled chicken, mac and cheese, peas — served four hours before game time. ‘He had done his research,’ Langdon said.

At East Anchorage High School, Langdon set the state 4A record with 2,200 career points, was a three-time Alaska State Player of the Year (the first to win it more than twice), led East to three consecutive state championships, and earned McDonald’s All-American honors (winning the three-point contest). He received the Dial Award as the nation’s top male student-athlete. The Alaska Sports Hall of Fame: he ‘single-handedly raised the profile of Alaska high school basketball in the Lower 48 states.’

Before Langdon, no Alaskan male had ever played for a Top 20 college program. Sports Illustrated sent a reporter in January 1994 and captured the absurdity: an assistant coach from the Midwest had flown 4,000 miles to watch practice in silence. Bobby Knight, Krzyzewski, and Dean Smith were all spotted in Anchorage — SI reported top coaches had been found ‘in library carrels poring over the November 1991 issue of National Geographic.’

He was also a gifted baseball player. A scout discovered Langdon playing catch with his father in a parking lot during his Duke recruiting visit. The San Diego Padres drafted him in the sixth round of the 1994 MLB Draft — ahead of future All-Stars Carl Pavano and Plácido Polanco. He signed for $230,000 and spent three summers in the Padres’ minor league system while at Duke. In 12 high school baseball games as a senior, he hit .333 with four home runs and struck out eleven batters in the league championship.
bio.duke:
Langdon redshirted the disastrous 1994–95 season (Coach K’s back surgery, Duke’s 13–18 collapse) due to a major knee injury. He then played four seasons, becoming one of the purest shooters in program history. He left Duke as the school’s all-time leader in three-point field goals (broken by J.J. Redick in 2006). He shot 90% from the free-throw line. Two-time First-Team All-ACC (1998, 1999), consensus Second-Team All-American in 1999. Dual degrees in mathematics and history.

The 1998–99 season was the peak. Duke went 37–2, tying the school record. They swept the ACC at 16–0 — the first undefeated ACC campaign in league history. Alongside Elton Brand, William Avery, Chris Carrawell, and Shane Battier, Langdon led one of the most talented teams college basketball had ever seen to the national championship game.

Then the final two minutes against Connecticut. Langdon hit a long three to cut it to 73–72 with 1:40 left. He had already carried Duke with consecutive threes and a four-point play. UConn answered. It was 75–74. Coach K, who had a timeout to spend, declined. He put the ball in Langdon’s hands. ‘Absolutely. Positively. I want Trajan Langdon to take that shot,’ Krzyzewski said. ‘Win or lose with Trajan Langdon. I’ll walk down any road with Trajan Langdon, and I’m proud of Trajan Langdon.’

But Ricky Moore, the tournament’s best defender, was waiting. Moore funneled Langdon into three defenders. Langdon tried to drive — driving was never his forte. He traveled. 5.4 seconds left. ‘I knew it was him against me and my will to win was going to take over,’ Moore said. On the final possession, three UConn defenders converged. Langdon tried to get off a 17-footer, got tripped up, and Moore stripped the ball cleanly. Langdon fell as the buzzer sounded. UConn 77, Duke 74.

In the locker room, Langdon’s response was a masterclass in grace: ‘I brought the ball up and three people were there. I tried to get off a shot but I got tripped up a little. It was a clean strip. Everything about the game was clean. We got beat.’ No excuses. The kid who visualized games in the dark carried that loss for the rest of his career — and turned it into fuel.
bio.after:
Cleveland selected Langdon 11th overall in 1999 — the first Alaskan in the NBA. Three seasons, 119 games, 5.4 ppg. A pure shooter in a league that demanded more. Casual fans remembered him as another Duke lottery bust.

Then he went to Europe and became a star. Benetton Treviso in Italy (15.2 ppg his first year overseas), Efes Pilsen in Turkey, Dynamo Moscow, and then CSKA Moscow. With CSKA: two EuroLeague championships (2006, 2008), Final Four MVP 2008, three All-EuroLeague selections, All-Decade Team (2001–10). He shot 51% from three in his final season. A European scouting report: ‘Trajan Langdon proved again to be one of the premier shooters on the Old Continent. His stroke is a beauty.’ In a 2006 exhibition game, he scored 17 against former Duke teammate Elton Brand’s Clippers. He retired June 2011, two days after CSKA’s ninth consecutive Russian championship. He also won bronze with the U.S. at the 1998 FIBA World Championship.
bio.now:
After retiring, Langdon scouted for the San Antonio Spurs (2012–15), became assistant GM of the Brooklyn Nets (March 2016), was named GM of the New Orleans Pelicans (May 2019), and on May 31, 2024, the Detroit Pistons hired him as President of Basketball Operations.

At 49, Langdon runs the Pistons. He is married to Tatiana. Duke Athletics Hall of Fame (2015). Alaska Sports Hall of Fame. His father still teaches anthropology in Anchorage, still studies the Tlingit.

Langdon’s story is the most geographically improbable in the entire Brotherhood. Born in Palo Alto, raised in Anchorage, drafted by a baseball team, named after a Roman emperor by a Stanford anthropologist who studied indigenous Alaskan tribes. He played catch with his father in a parking lot and a baseball scout changed his life. He flew 4,000 miles from home to play for Coach K. He set Duke’s three-point record and then, in the biggest moment of his career, couldn’t get off a three-point shot. He was labeled an NBA bust and became a European champion. Every chapter has been a reinvention, and every reinvention built on the same foundation: the kid in the Anchorage darkness, three hours before tipoff, visualizing the game. His father taught him that. The Tlingit villages taught his father. See it first, then do it.

players.name: Chris Carrawell
players.tagline: From freshman unknown to ACC POY.
players.now: Associate Head Coach, Duke Blue Devils
players.stat: 136 games • 1,455 pts • ACC POY 2000 • consensus All-American • 66 ACC wins (2nd all-time Duke)

bio.road:
Chris Carrawell was born November 25, 1977, and grew up in a rough inner-city neighborhood in north St. Louis. He was the oldest of his mother Joanne’s four children. He never knew his father — he would not meet him until 1999, his senior year at Duke. Joanne raised all four kids alone. When Chris was thirteen, a mentor named Vollomar entered his life and became the father figure he’d never had. ‘I’ve called him my father ever since I was 13,’ Carrawell later said.

Basketball was the way out, but the choice of where to play mattered. If he stayed in the public school system, he was ticketed for Beaumont High School, plagued by inner-city problems. Cardinal Ritter College Prep, a Catholic school just blocks from his mother’s home, offered an alternative. ‘There was no way I was going to Beaumont,’ Carrawell said. At Cardinal Ritter, he found coach Preston Thomas, who had built the program from 8–18 into a powerhouse. Thomas would say: ‘Chris is a survivor. He’s always been willing to do what had to be done.’

What Carrawell did was become one of the most dominant high school players in Missouri history. In four varsity years, the team went 80–13 with a three-year undefeated home streak. He won two state Class AA championships. He set school career records for points (2,087), rebounds (1,076), and assists (555). Conference Player of the Year twice, Gatorade Central Region Player of the Year, fourth-team Parade All-American. Cardinal Ritter made USA Today’s top-25 teams list twice. His teammates included future NBA players Loren Woods and Jahidi White.

Then the shoulders betrayed him. During senior year, Carrawell suffered chronic dislocations of both shoulders. Coach Thomas: ‘He was trying to play with one arm strapped to his body. But he was willing to sacrifice some of his scoring to help bolster Loren’s fragile confidence so that the team could be successful.’ By the time he arrived at Duke, he’d had three shoulder surgeries. He was nineteen years old.

Many recruiters backed off. Krzyzewski never wavered. ‘The first time I saw Chris, I thought he could be a great player. After he started having his shoulder problems, I still thought he could be really good.’ His choice came down to Duke or Saint Louis University. Friends told him he was ‘damaged goods’ and couldn’t make it at Duke — in basketball or academically. ‘He’ll be on the first plane ride back. He’ll be back home soon enough.’ Carrawell said the naysayers ‘actually kind of helped.’

‘For whatever reason, I don’t know what reason, Coach K stuck with me and believed in me in high school,’ Carrawell said. ‘So from there, I thought whenever my time started with Duke, I was going to give 100,000 percent for him.’
bio.duke:
To understand Carrawell at Duke, start with a single image: February 5, 1997, at Wake Forest. Duke’s freshman forward, six-foot-six, lined up to guard Wake’s Tim Duncan — six-foot-eleven, the best center in the country, the eventual No. 1 overall pick. Dick Vitale, calling the game on ESPN, marveled: ‘Look at Carrawell, you’ve got to give that kid a lot of credit! There he is, a freshman inside, playing against Duncan, head-to-head.’ The St. Louis Post-Dispatch’s Benjamin Hochman later noted: ‘Except it wasn’t head-to-head. Head-to-neck, maybe.’

Krzyzewski decided the key to beating Wake was to smother Duncan and dare someone else to beat them. Duncan still went 11-of-13 for 26 points — unstoppable when he got the ball — but managed only 7 rebounds (he averaged 14.7 for the season). Then, with Duke up three and 1:18 left, the play that defined Carrawell’s career: he drifted from Duncan, helped on a driving guard, and pinned the layup to the backboard. ‘I just got over a second late. I just waited, came over and blocked it.’ Duke won 73–68, snapping an eight-game losing streak to Wake. Duncan won ACC Player of the Year that season. Three years later, Carrawell would win the same award.

That freshman year, on a perimeter-shooting team, Carrawell led Duke in dunks with 22. He shot 57.6% from the field, ranked third in offensive rebounds, and played with a controlled fury that belied his numbers. Anthony Bonner, an NBA player from SLU, offered the perfect scouting report: ‘He kills you with his unselfishness.’

Shoulder surgery before his sophomore year. Four games missed with a strained back. He still scored in double figures 22 times, including 19 and 18 in two UNC games, as Duke went 32–4 and reached the Elite Eight (falling to Kentucky 86–84). Junior year: started every game of the 37–2 season — Duke’s best record ever, first 16–0 ACC season, the Elton Brand/Trajan Langdon team that reached the national championship game. Carrawell contributed 9.9 points and 130 assists (second on the team). Third-Team All-ACC.

Then the senior season. Brand (No. 1 pick), Langdon (11th), Avery, Burgess, Maggette — all gone. Krzyzewski called on Carrawell to be leader, go-to scorer, and defensive stopper, all at once. After losing their first two games, Duke won 16 straight and climbed to No. 3. Carrawell: 16.9 points, 6.1 rebounds, 3.2 assists, 35.6 minutes per game. ACC Player of the Year. Consensus First-Team All-American. ACC All-Defense. Senior captain. 29–5, ACC Tournament championship, Sweet Sixteen.

He is one of only two players in ACC history to play on four consecutive regular-season championship teams (alongside Nate James). He was the only player in each of Duke’s ACC-record 30 straight league wins. His 66 career ACC victories are the second-most by any Duke player. Four-year record: 133–24 overall, 58–6 ACC. Career totals: 1,455 points, 608 rebounds, 309 assists, 114 blocks.
bio.after:
The San Antonio Spurs drafted Carrawell 41st overall in 2000. He was waived before the season began and never played an NBA minute. With his retirement in 2007, he became the first player of the new millennium drafted into the NBA who never appeared in a regular-season game.

Instead, Carrawell spent seven years chasing basketball around the world. Italy for a year. The inaugural NBA D-League season with the Asheville Altitude (56 games, 7.3 ppg). Lithuania with the storied Žalgiris Kaunas. Germany. Australia, where he played for the Sydney Kings and averaged 13.1 points and 7.1 rebounds in 18 NBL games, scoring 15 in his debut. The Philippines with the Alaska Aces. The ABA, where he won the 2006 league MVP, the championship, and the championship MVP — a triple crown in a league almost nobody had heard of. The Netherlands in 2007, where his team lost in the finals. He retired at 29, having played professional basketball on four continents without ever playing an NBA game.

Then the long road back to Duke. Not as a coach — he crawled. 2007–08: Athletics Outreach Coordinator. 2008–10: graduate assistant and head team manager. 2010–11: assistant video coordinator and assistant strength and conditioning coach. He briefly worked with the Duke women’s team. ‘That wasn’t in my dreams,’ Carrawell said. ‘I never dreamed of being a coach. But the love for the game, the passion for the game and wanting to pay it forward has led to me coaching at Duke?’

The Springfield Armor (Nets D-League affiliate, three years). Then Marquette under his Duke teammate Steve Wojciechowski (four years) — where Carrawell reportedly asked Wojo whether he wanted to ‘win Friday’s practice or Saturday’s game,’ a pointed question about calibrating the Krzyzewski intensity for a different program.
bio.now:
In March 2018, Carrawell returned to Duke as an assistant under Krzyzewski. He helped develop Zion Williamson and RJ Barrett — the first freshman teammates in NCAA history to each average 20+ points — and Tre Jones (ACC Player of the Year and Defensive Player of the Year, 2020). In 2021, Krzyzewski promoted him to associate head coach for Coach K’s final season. ‘This is incredible,’ Carrawell said. ‘For Coach to have that trust in me, especially in his last year, is an honor and privilege. It is the equivalent of being named a captain my senior year because Coach doesn’t just hand these things out — I’ve had to earn it and work for it.’

He now holds the Morris Williams Associate Head Coach endowed chair under Jon Scheyer. In January 2025, when Scheyer was ill, Carrawell served as acting head coach at SMU, guiding the No. 4 Blue Devils to victory. He came out talkative and active — exactly how the 2000 ACC Player of the Year played.

At 47, Carrawell lives in Mebane, North Carolina, with his wife Keisha. Their older son Caleb is a sixth-year forward at Loyola Marymount. Their younger son is Christian. His mother, sister, and brother still live in St. Louis. He returns to recruit, and he’ll argue with anyone that the early-to-mid-’90s were the best years for St. Louis basketball — in one phone call with the Post-Dispatch, he rattled off 19 names of local hoopers without pausing for breath.

The arc of Chris Carrawell’s life is the Brotherhood’s purest meritocracy story. He grew up without a father in north St. Louis. Three shoulder surgeries by age 19. Called ‘damaged goods.’ Chose Duke anyway and gave 100,000 percent. Defended Tim Duncan as a freshman. Won the same award Duncan won. Drafted by an NBA team, never played a second. Seven years on four continents. Came back to Duke and took every unglamorous job they offered — outreach coordinator, team manager, video assistant, strength coach — because being in the building was enough. Worked his way from the Asheville Altitude to the Duke bench. Promoted to associate head coach for the most legendary final season in college basketball history.

As Hochman wrote: ‘It’s an amazing and inspiring story of a St. Louis kid who found a coach who believed in him as much as the kid believed in himself.’ Coach K stuck with damaged goods. And damaged goods became indestructible.

players.name: Ricky Price
players.tagline: His jumper saved the 1996 season.
players.now: Founder, Game Ready Skills & Development; speaker; author
players.stat: McDonald’s AA dunk champ 1994 • 3rd-Team All-ACC • 1,000+ pts • 10 yrs pro overseas

bio.road:
When people who grew up playing basketball in Los Angeles in the early 1990s talk about the best hoopers to come out of the area, one name always surfaces: Ricky Price. Not sometimes. Always. The Not In My House podcast called him ‘without question one of the greatest high school basketball players to ever come out of the Los Angeles area.’ His mid-range game was automatic. At 6-foot-6, with explosive athleticism and a scorer’s instinct, he was the complete package.

Price was born February 4, 1976, in Carson, California — a working-class city in the South Bay, fifteen miles south of downtown LA. At Carson High School, he averaged 28 points and 11 rebounds and was unanimously named a USA Today/AP/Parade Top 5 player nationally. Gold medal representing the U.S. in the Junior Olympics in Argentina. And at the 1994 McDonald’s All-American Game, he won the slam dunk contest — the first Duke player ever to do so, a lineage that would later include Gerald Henderson (2006), Grayson Allen (2014), and Zion Williamson (2018). At that same McDonald’s Game, he played alongside future Duke teammate Trajan Langdon and against future Duke teammate Steve Wojciechowski.

His recruitment came down to Duke and Kansas. Roy Williams was his first choice for a while. But as Price spent more time with Coach K, the decision clarified. When he broke the news to Williams, something remarkable happened: the relationship didn’t end. ‘It’s amazing because, of course, I hate Carolina,’ Price said. ‘I think if you’re a Duke guy, naturally, that’s embedded in your DNA. But I love Roy Williams. He’s one of the few honest guys in the game. We still talk frequently. After every Duke-Carolina game, I always make it a point to go see him and say hello. He still asks about my mom. Here we are 20 years later and he’s still asking about my mom and dad. That just shows you the character of the type of guy he is.’
bio.duke:
What happened to Ricky Price at Duke cannot be separated from what happened to Duke in 1994–95. He arrived as one of three prized freshmen — Price, Langdon, and Wojciechowski — joining a program expected to contend. Price had never had consistent coaching in high school, and Duke was supposed to change that. Instead, it got worse.

Coach K had undergone back surgery and rushed back. Twelve games in, with Duke 9–3, the pain became unbearable. Krzyzewski left on medical leave. Pete Gaudet, making just over $300 a week as the ‘restricted earnings’ coach, took over. Duke went 4–15 under Gaudet, finishing 13–18 and 2–14 in the ACC — dead last. Tommy Amaker, who had helped build the program as a player a decade earlier, saw the confidence draining: ‘Once we lost a few games, we lost our confidence. That’s where these guys miss Coach K.’

For Price, 3,000 miles from home: ‘It was very difficult as a freshman. You are still trying to find your way. As a freshman you want to lean on your head coach.’ He didn’t have that opportunity. ‘It was embarrassing. You don’t want to be part of a team that goes down as one of the worst in recent memory. It was a very difficult year and shows how important leadership is.’

But from that disaster, Price took a lesson that shaped the rest of his life. When Krzyzewski returned for 1995–96, the coach was different. ‘Prior to going out he did everything,’ Price recalled. ‘He was taking on too much of a burden. Post back surgery he trusted other guys to do other things. He let other guys step up and do their part.’ The principle of delegation — that leadership means trusting others — became the foundation of Price’s speaking career decades later.

With Krzyzewski back, Price’s sophomore season was his best at Duke: 14.2 ppg, career-high 28 against Virginia, a buzzer-beater to beat Maryland that Duke Basketball Report later credited with putting Duke back in the NCAAs. Third-Team All-ACC. He reached 1,000 career points. Duke won the ACC regular-season title — ‘only five short years after Ricky Price put Duke in the NCAAs.’

As a junior, a broken finger wrecked his jump shot. For a player whose mid-range game was his signature, the injury was devastating. He slumped to just over 9 ppg. Then senior year: academic probation after submitting a paper that wasn’t his. Suspended for the first semester, lost his starting role. His own accountability advice years later: ‘Take accountability for what you did, correct it, apologize to the people you need to apologize to, and then you’ve got to do some self-improvement and make sure that never happens again.’ He didn’t quit. He came back, played off the bench, helped Duke win its second straight ACC regular-season title. He graduated with a degree in sociology.
bio.after:
Price went undrafted in 1998. Not long after, he was playing 5,000 miles from the NBA — in Slovenia. Also the Netherlands, Germany, France, Finland, Asia. NBA preseason stints with the Miami Heat, Detroit Pistons, Orlando Magic, and Charlotte Bobcats — close enough to taste it, never close enough to stay. For about a decade, he saw different arenas across Europe and Asia and played in American pro leagues like the IBL and the CBA.

As a player, he was known for his above-the-rim game. But of all his accomplishments, he has said that learning to become a solid defensive player was his biggest achievement — the thing that kept him employed for a decade overseas when the dunk contest highlights faded. After retiring, he worked as VP of Operations for Worldwide Hoops Sports Marketing (representing overseas and NBA players) and as a Raycom Sports correspondent covering ACC basketball.
bio.now:
In 2010, Price founded Game Ready Skills and Development LLC in North Carolina. His first summer camp in Charlotte drew 150 kids. Former Duke players Ryan Kelly, Seth Curry, Rodney Hood, Andre Dawkins, and Tyler Thornton have appeared. But Price’s focus is development, not exposure: ‘My focus is 100% skills development. My job is to get them ready to get to that level. Unfortunately, a lot of our kids don’t have the necessary essentials, the basics, the fundamentals on how to play the game. How to pivot off your left and right foot, how to pump fake and one dribble, how to shoot a one dribble pull up. You’d be amazed how many kids can’t do that.’

He commands thousands of dollars per speaking engagement, drawing parallels between basketball teamwork and corporate environments, leaning heavily on the lessons from that devastating freshman year. His book, Celebrate Your Wins, has a foreword by Coach K. He still volunteers coaching middle schoolers — spending back-to-back five-hour Sunday sessions with varsity and JV players at schools that might otherwise get overlooked.

Ricky Price’s story is the most candid in the Transition Era. The five-star recruit from the LA basketball machine. First Duke player to win the McDonald’s dunk contest. Chose Duke over Kansas and kept Roy Williams’ friendship for life. Arrived at Duke and immediately endured the worst season in Krzyzewski history. Peaked as a sophomore, lost his jumper to a broken finger, lost his starting job to academic probation, and never quit. Played ten years on three continents. Turned the worst season of his life into the foundation of a speaking career.

Duke Basketball Report observed: ‘If he had the choice to do things over again, he’d still choose Duke.’ That’s the line that matters. Not the dunk contest trophy. Not the All-ACC season. Not the broken finger or the probation. The choice. He’d make it again.

players.name: Shane Battier
players.tagline: The No-Stat All-Star. The soul of the 2001 champions.
players.now: Operating Partner, Garnett Station; minority owner, Tampa Bay Rays
players.stat: 146 games • Naismith POY • 3x DPOY • 2x NBA champ • Final Four MOP

bio.road:
Shane Battier grew up in Birmingham, Michigan, the only child of a Black father and a white mother. By seventh grade at Detroit Country Day School, he was already 6-foot-4. A year later, 6-foot-7. He was also the only biracial kid at Country Day, a prestigious private school in the Detroit suburbs. Michael Lewis, who profiled Battier in a landmark 2009 New York Times Magazine piece, captured the tension: the inner-city AAU kids treated Battier as a suburban kid with a white game, while his suburban teammates treated him like a visitor from another planet.

Battier turned that in-between status into fuel. He led Country Day to three consecutive Class B state championships from 1995 to 1997, earned Michigan Mr. Basketball as a senior, and graduated with a 3.96 GPA while being named the school’s outstanding student. He was the Naismith Prep Player of the Year in 1997 — the first signal of the award he’d bookend four years later.

Dean Smith recruited him for North Carolina. Battier chose Duke instead, beginning one of the most decorated four-year careers in the program’s history.
bio.duke:
Battier was the prototype of the modern do-everything forward before the term existed. At 6-foot-8, 220 pounds, he could guard every position on the floor, shoot threes at a 41.6% clip, post up smaller defenders, and anchor a defense. The Cameron Crazies honored his relentless charge-taking with a chant: ‘Who’s your daddy? Battier!’

His freshman year (1997–98) was solid but ended in heartbreak: Duke blew a 17-point lead to Kentucky in the regional finals, a loss to the eventual national champion. As a sophomore, the Blue Devils reached the Final Four before falling to Connecticut. The program was building toward something. It arrived in 2000–01.

That senior season was one of the greatest individual years in Duke history. Battier averaged 19.9 points, 7.3 rebounds, 2.3 blocks, and 2.1 steals while leading the Blue Devils to a 35–4 record, an undefeated ACC regular season (16–0), and the national championship. In the title game against Arizona, he scored 21 points with six rebounds and four assists. He was named Final Four MOP, consensus National Player of the Year (Naismith, Wooden, AP, Rupp), NABC Defensive Player of the Year for the third straight time, and Academic All-American of the Year.

He is the only player in history to win both the Naismith Prep Player of the Year and the Naismith College Player of the Year. Duke retired his No. 31, and he was inducted into the College Basketball Hall of Fame in 2019. Over 146 career games: 13.6 points, 6.1 rebounds, a 50/41/78 shooting line, and a career record of 131–15. He graduated with a degree in religion.
bio.after:
Memphis took Battier sixth overall in the 2001 draft. He spent five seasons with the Grizzlies (2001–06), becoming their defensive anchor and averaging 10.6 points, 4.8 rebounds, and 1.0 blocks. But his value transcended the box score — a theme that would define his entire professional life.

The Houston Rockets acquired Battier in 2006, and it was there that his statistical legend crystallized. The Rockets made him their only player with access to the team’s highly sophisticated analytical data on opposing players. Battier studied film and data obsessively, using it to contest shots from the exact angles where opponents were least comfortable. He earned NBA All-Defensive Second Team honors in both 2008 and 2009. Michael Lewis’ 2009 profile, ‘The No-Stat All-Star,’ demonstrated that Battier’s teams consistently performed better with him on the court despite his unremarkable counting stats. Harvard Business School turned his approach into a 2010 case study.

In 2011, Battier signed with the Miami Heat and stepped into the most important role of his career: the glue guy on a super-team. Playing alongside LeBron James, Dwyane Wade, and Chris Bosh, Battier provided the defense, the corner threes, and the veteran steadiness that championship teams need from their supporting cast. The Heat won back-to-back titles in 2012 and 2013. Battier retired after the 2013–14 season at age 35. In his final season, the NBA named him the inaugural Teammate of the Year. Career totals: 977 games, 8.6 points, 4.2 rebounds, 38.4% from three.
bio.now:
Battier briefly worked for ESPN as an analyst before the Heat hired him as their Vice President of Analytics and Basketball Development in February 2017. He served until 2021, when he transitioned to a consulting role with the organization.

Since then, Battier has built a diversified post-basketball portfolio. He became an operating partner at Garnett Station Partners, a private equity firm, in December 2023. He serves on the board of directors at Yext, a tech company. He speaks regularly at the MIT Sloan Sports Analytics Conference and corporate events. He has advised Intel on their sports technology division.

In October 2025, Battier joined the ownership group of the Tampa Bay Rays as a minority owner, part of the Patrick Zalupski-led consortium that purchased the franchise. It marked his first foray into professional sports ownership.

In 2010, Battier and his wife Heidi — his high school sweetheart from Country Day — founded the Battier Take Charge Foundation. The organization provides scholarships, mentorship, and leadership training for underserved youth in Miami, Houston, Detroit, and Camden, New Jersey. As of 2025, more than 84% of Battier Scholars are first-generation college students, and over 95% have completed a bachelor’s degree or higher. In 2024, the foundation received a $1.4 million grant from the KPMG US Foundation. After relocating to Charlotte, North Carolina, in mid-2024, Battier established a new outpost for the foundation there.

At 47, Battier lives in Charlotte with Heidi. He is, in many ways, the Brotherhood’s most complete post-playing success story: championships at every level, a pioneering role in basketball analytics, a thriving business career, a meaningful philanthropic legacy, and now a stake in a professional sports franchise.

The kid who navigated between two worlds in suburban Detroit — too Black for one, too white for the other — found a way to belong everywhere. His No. 31 hangs in the Cameron rafters, and his influence hangs over every team that values defense, data, and the things that don’t show up in the box score.

players.name: Elton Brand
players.tagline: NABC POY as a sophomore. Left early and never looked back.
players.stat: 16.2 ppg • 8.9 rpg • 37–2 • first 16–0 ACC season • Naismith POY

bio.road:
Elton Tyron Brand was born March 11, 1979, in Cortlandt Manor, New York, and grew up in Peekskill — a small, gritty city on the Hudson River about an hour north of Manhattan. At age thirteen, he enrolled at Peekskill High School and was immediately placed on the varsity roster. By the time he graduated, he was averaging 40 points and 20 rebounds per game — numbers so absurd they sounded invented. He played AAU basketball with future NBA players Lamar Odom and Ron Artest.

He was named New York State Mr. Basketball, a McDonald’s All-American, and Parade Magazine’s High School Player of the Year. He chose Duke, arriving in the fall of 1997 alongside Shane Battier and a freshman class that would define the next era of the program.
bio.duke:
Brand’s Duke career lasted only two seasons, but they were among the most productive in program history. As a freshman in 1997–98, he averaged 13.4 points and 7.3 rebounds and was named ACC Rookie of the Year. Duke reached the regional finals before blowing a 17-point lead to eventual national champion Kentucky.

His sophomore season was a masterpiece. Brand averaged 17.7 points, 9.8 rebounds, and 3.1 blocks per game while leading the Blue Devils to a 37–2 record and the first undefeated ACC regular season (16–0) in league history. He was named consensus National Player of the Year — winning the Naismith, Wooden, and Associated Press awards — ACC Player of the Year, and a consensus First-Team All-American. Duke reached the national championship game, where they fell to Connecticut 77–74.

Over two seasons: 72 games, 16.2 points, 8.9 rebounds, 50.0% shooting. He was the most physically dominant Duke player since Danny Ferry, perhaps since Laettner — but with a low-post power game neither of them possessed. He declared for the NBA Draft after his sophomore year.
bio.after:
The Chicago Bulls selected Brand with the first overall pick in the 1999 NBA Draft. He shared the NBA Rookie of the Year award with Steve Francis after averaging 20.1 points and 10.0 rebounds — the first player to average 20/10 as a rookie since Tim Duncan. He was traded to the Los Angeles Clippers in 2001 and became the franchise’s best player, earning two All-Star selections (2002, 2006) and an All-NBA Second Team nod in 2006, when he averaged 24.2 points per game.

In 2008, Brand signed a five-year, $82 million free-agent contract with the Philadelphia 76ers. An Achilles tendon injury lingered, and Brand was never the same explosive player. He was amnestied in 2012 with $18 million still owed. He spent time with the Dallas Mavericks and Atlanta Hawks before retiring in August 2015. In January 2016, he came out of retirement at Coach K’s urging, signing with the Sixers to mentor their young core during the Process era. He played 26 games at age 36, becoming the 51st player in NBA history to reach 9,000 career rebounds. Career totals: 17 seasons, 1,058 games, 15.9 points, 8.5 rebounds, 1.7 blocks, 50.0% FG. Approximately $172 million in NBA salary.
bio.now:
Brand transitioned to the front office immediately. In 2016, the Sixers hired him as a player development consultant. By 2017, he was GM of the Delaware Blue Coats (G League affiliate). On September 20, 2018, the Sixers named Brand their general manager — a meteoric rise from retirement to running a franchise in less than three years.

As GM, Brand traded for Jimmy Butler and Tobias Harris, pushing the eventual champion Toronto Raptors to a dramatic seven-game series decided by Kawhi Leonard’s buzzer-beater. But the offseason that followed haunted him: Harris re-signed for $180 million, Simmons extended for $170 million, Horford signed for $97 million — while Butler and JJ Redick left. Butler led Miami to the Finals twice. Horford was traded after one season. Simmons eventually refused to play.

In late 2020, the Sixers hired Daryl Morey as President of Basketball Operations. Brand’s contract was extended, but his role shifted behind the scenes. In the 2025 NBA Draft, he selected VJ Edgecombe 3rd overall.

At 46, Brand remains GM of the 76ers in his seventh year. He is one of only a handful of former No. 1 overall picks to transition into a senior front-office role. The kid from Peekskill who averaged 40 and 20 in high school and played AAU ball with Lamar Odom and Ron Artest now builds rosters, evaluates draft prospects, and navigates the salary cap.

players.name: Jay Williams
players.tagline: The motorcycle accident that changed everything.
players.now: ESPN NBA analyst; author; College Basketball Hall of Fame (2017); motivational speaker
players.stat: 109 games • 2,079 pts (6th all-time) • 2x NABC POY • Naismith + Wooden • 2001 NCAA Champion • Jersey #22 retired

bio.road:
Jason David Williams was born September 10, 1981, in Plainfield, New Jersey — a city of stark racial and economic divides. His father, David, worked in management information systems for American Express. His mother, Althea, was a guidance counselor who would earn a second master’s degree while working full-time as assistant principal at North Plainfield High. Jay was their only biological child. Strict household. Education first. You never quit something you committed to.

But the Williams family expanded unexpectedly. Althea was counseling a girl named Jackie Taylor, whose mother had died of cancer. David and Althea brought Jackie home. Never officially adopted her, but raised her, put her through school. ‘They’re mom and dad,’ Taylor said. Jay had a sister.

In fourth grade, David and Althea put up a hoop in the backyard. They had to make Jay come inside at night because of the snow. By fifth grade he’d taken the coach’s son’s job at point guard. He tried everything — karate, volleyball, soccer, baseball, tennis, swimming, chess. He was the state volleyball player of the year as a senior. But basketball consumed him.

At St. Joseph High School in Metuchen, he started all four varsity years. School records: 1,977 points, 407 steals. Senior stats: 19 PPG, 7.0 APG, 4.2 RPG, 3.7 SPG. New Jersey Player of the Year. Parade and USA Today All-American. McDonald’s All-American (20 points in the game). Morgan Wootten Award. 3.6 GPA. David and Althea rarely missed a game despite international travel schedules. Althea started the first girls’ cheerleading squad at the all-boys school. His coach, Mark Taylor: ‘They’re a special family.’
bio.duke:
Williams became one of the few Duke freshmen to average double figures in scoring (14.5 PPG, 6.5 APG). ACC Rookie of the Year. National Freshman of the Year.

Sophomore year was the championship. He started all 39 games alongside Shane Battier, the program’s moral compass. Averaged 21.6 PPG — the first Duke player since Danny Ferry (1989) to lead the ACC in scoring. Also 6.1 APG (2nd in ACC). NABC Player of the Year. Led all NCAA Tournament scorers at 25.7 PPG. Duke beat Arizona 82–72 in Minneapolis for Coach K’s third national title. Williams and Battier combined for over 1,500 points — one of only two Duke duos to each score 700+ in a single year.

Then the decision that defined his character. Projected lottery pick. Could have left for millions. He stayed. His father: ‘If you kept the larger picture in mind, the short-term things will work themselves out.’ His mother was simultaneously earning her second master’s. ‘It was quite a joy to be writing a paper and staying up all night while he was writing a paper and staying up all night. The problem came in that I had to go to work.’

Junior year was a coronation. 21.3 PPG, 5.7 APG, 3.5 RPG. His 841 points broke Dick Groat’s 49-year Duke scoring record. Set the NCAA Tournament record for three-pointers attempted. Won both the Naismith and Wooden Awards. Consensus first-team All-American for the second straight year. Two-time NABC POY. Graduated in three years with a sociology degree — the first Duke athlete to do so. Jersey #22 retired on Senior Day. Career: 2,079 points, 6th all-time in Duke history.

Coach K later described him as having ‘as fast a first step as any point guard in the history of basketball.’
bio.after:
The Chicago Bulls took Williams second overall in the 2002 draft, behind only Yao Ming. He was given Michael Jordan’s locker. He had a three-unit apartment on the 40th floor of the Park Hyatt on Michigan Avenue — floor-to-ceiling windows, Lake Michigan stretching out like an ocean. He was 20 years old.

Rookie season: 75 games, 9.5 PPG, 4.7 APG. Inconsistent but promising. Triple-double against the Nets. All-Rookie Second Team. Represented the US at the 2002 FIBA World Championship.

Then June 19, 2003. A perfect Chicago summer day. He took his black Yamaha R6 with red accents instead of the Corvette. No helmet. No motorcycle license. His contract forbade it. ‘I was that prototypical young hotshot who thought he had all the answers. Motorcycle license? What for? Money and arrogance were all I needed.’

He crashed into a streetlight at Belmont and Honore in Roscoe Village. Fractured pelvis. Severed main nerve in his leg. Three torn ligaments including ACL. Life-threatening internal bleeding. Doctors considered amputation. In an instant, the man with the fastest first step in basketball could no longer walk.

The Bulls drafted Kirk Hinrich a week later. They waived Williams but gave him $3 million for rehab despite having no contractual obligation. He became addicted to OxyContin and other painkillers. Depression. ‘For the longest time I felt like being alive was more of a consolation prize.’ The Nets signed him in 2006 — released after three weeks. Austin Toros D-League — three games, waived. Career over at 25.
bio.now:
Jay Williams rebuilt himself from the wreckage. Not quickly. Not cleanly. But completely. Coach K’s “next play” philosophy — don’t dwell on what happened, move forward — became the operating system for his second life. He went from NBA millions to $30,000 at ESPN.

He is now one of ESPN’s most prominent basketball voices: NBA Countdown, studio shows, game telecasts. He co-hosted the “Keyshawn, JWill & Max Show” on ESPN Radio. Launched “The Limits with Jay Williams” podcast on NPR. Motivational speaker. Co-founded Simatree consulting. Author of Life Is Not an Accident: A Memoir of Reinvention (2016). Grant Hill: ‘Jay’s story is about strength, resilience, and redemption.’ Charles Barkley: ‘A lot of athletes have been to the mountaintop and back to the bottom. But not Jay.’

Inducted into the College Basketball Hall of Fame in 2017. Married Nikki Bonacorsi in May 2018. Daughter Amelia Brooklyn-Rose (October 2018). Son Zane David. Lives in Plainfield, New Jersey — the same town where David and Althea put up a hoop in the backyard and had to make their boy come inside because of the snow.

He is 43 years old. He had everything — a national championship, the Wooden Award, the #2 pick, Jordan’s locker, the fastest first step in basketball — and threw it into a streetlight on a perfect June evening because he thought he was invincible. He wasn’t. But the crash that almost killed him at twenty-one was not the end of his story. It was the beginning of the one that mattered. He is convinced the accident was no accident. It was a tragedy that taught him how to live.

players.name: Carlos Boozer
players.tagline: Duke to the Dream Team.
players.now: Utah Jazz front office; ACC Network analyst; author; Naismith HOF (2008 Team USA)
players.stat: 109 college games • 2001 NCAA Champion • 13 NBA seasons • 16.2 PPG/9.5 RPG • 2x All-Star • Olympic Gold 2008

bio.road:
Carlos Austin Boozer Jr. was born November 20, 1981, at a U.S. military base in Aschaffenburg, West Germany. His father, Carlos Sr., was an Army sergeant. His mother Renee raised five children while both parents worked two jobs. The family settled in Washington, D.C., after Carlos Sr.’s Army service ended.

What happened next changed everything. Six-year-old Carlos was playing neighborhood basketball with his friend Chris. Chris made a basket and knocked another player down. An older kid — a gang member — pulled a gun from his backpack and shot Chris. Then rode away on his bicycle. Chris died in Carlos Boozer’s arms.

Carlos Sr. and Renee loaded the car and drove 2,800 miles to Bellingham, Washington. A ferry carried them to Juneau, Alaska. They were looking for the safest place in America.

Juneau was another planet. Population 30,000. Rainforest. Eagles. Glaciers. No roads in or out. ‘I was very different. My swag was different. I’m coming into school in that old ’85 Dodge van, bumping Tupac and Biggie, and nobody had ever heard of those rappers. They embraced me.’ The family lived in an apartment in Douglas, seven people, lower-middle class. No basketball hoop. So Carlos Sr. drove his son thirty minutes to Auke Bay Elementary, where there was a covered outdoor court. Even in snow: homework, wait for Dad, drive to Auke Bay, go at it for two hours, come home for dinner. Every single day for four years.

At Juneau-Douglas High: 95–12 record. Back-to-back state titles (1997, 1998). Three-time Alaska POY. Two-time Parade All-American. Senior: 28 PPG/14 RPG. Gatorade National POY. McDonald’s All-American (22 pts/11 reb). ‘Think about a kid at 12 years old, in Juneau, Alaska, telling his parents, “I want to go to the NBA.”’ His parents let him dream. Coach K came to Juneau. Boozer chose Duke.
bio.duke:
Boozer arrived in 1999 with Jay Williams. ACC All-Freshman Team. Sophomore year was the masterpiece: Williams, Boozer, Shane Battier, Mike Dunleavy Jr., Chris Duhon — one of the most talented Duke rosters ever. Boozer was the 6-9, 260-pound power forward with a soft touch and a competitive fire forged on that outdoor court in Auke Bay.

Duke went 35–4 and won the 2001 NCAA Championship, beating Arizona 82–72 in Minneapolis. Coach K’s third national title. Boozer averaged double figures alongside Williams (ACC scoring leader) and Battier (National POY).

Junior year, with Williams and Battier gone, Boozer stepped into the starring role: 18.2 PPG, 8.7 RPG. He, Williams, and Dunleavy became the only Duke trio to each score 600+ in a season. 2002 ACC Tournament MVP. NABC Third-Team All-American. Duke reached the Sweet Sixteen. After the season, Boozer declared for the draft.
bio.after:
Drafted 35th overall — massively undervalued. Rookie in Cleveland: 10.0 PPG, 7.5 RPG, All-Rookie Second Team. By year two: 15.5 PPG, 11.4 RPG alongside a rookie named LeBron James.

Then the controversy. The Cavaliers released Boozer from his $695K contract expecting him to re-sign for ~$39M. Instead, he signed a 6-year, $70M deal with Utah. Cleveland’s owner: ‘I decided to trust Carlos. He did not show that trust and respect in return.’ Boozer maintained NBA tampering rules forced his hand. The episode tagged him with a reputation he never fully shook.

In Utah he became a star: 2x All-Star (2007, 2008). Averaged 20+ PPG in 2006–07, leading the Jazz to the Western Conference Finals. Scored 41 against Houston in the playoffs. Six seasons in Utah: 19.3 PPG, 10.6 RPG.

Olympics: bronze in Athens 2004, then the legendary “Redeem Team” gold in Beijing 2008 alongside LeBron, Kobe, Wade, CP3, and Carmelo. The 2008 team was inducted into the Naismith HOF in 2025.

Chicago Bulls: 5-year, $80M contract (2010). Helped the Bulls win the East’s top seed in 2010–11 with Derrick Rose. Four seasons. Lakers 2014–15. Guangdong Southern Tigers (China) 2016–17. Retired. 861 NBA games, ~$130M career earnings.
bio.now:
The most important story of Carlos Boozer’s life has nothing to do with basketball.

On May 31, 2006, his firstborn son Carmani was diagnosed with sickle cell disease. Both Carlos and wife CeCe (Cindy Blackwell, whom he’d met when she was at NC State and he was at Duke) carried the gene. ‘The thought of me losing my son, what do I do? Do I put my kid through a life full of torture? Or do I roll the dice?’

They rolled the dice. Doctors at Emory told them about a bone marrow transplant using stem cells from a sibling’s umbilical cord. The Boozers conceived through IVF — screening embryos to be sickle-free and bone marrow matches. 34 eggs harvested. 10 viable embryos. Two perfect matches. Both implanted.

On July 18, 2007, Cameron and Cayden Boozer were born. Three weeks later, doctors transplanted their umbilical cord stem cells into Carmani. He spent 40 nights in Miami Children’s Hospital. It worked. Carmani was cured.

Those twins became the most dominant high school basketball duo in America. Cameron: 6-9, first player since LeBron to win Mr. Basketball USA (2023, 2025), projected top pick 2026 NBA Draft. Cayden: 6-4 guard, five-star recruit, USA Basketball gold medalist. Three straight Florida state championships at Christopher Columbus High. ESPN’s 2024 documentary Blood Brothers told their story.

Both committed to Duke on October 11, 2024. They are on the 2025–26 Blue Devils roster. The Brotherhood is now literal: a father’s legacy continued by sons who began their lives saving someone else’s.

Carlos joined the Utah Jazz front office in May 2025. ACC Network analyst. Published Every Shot Counts (2023). Still runs camps in Juneau every summer; proceeds go to sickle cell research. His parents moved back to Juneau. ‘We missed Juneau. We came back and decided to live here the rest of our lives.’

He is 43. Born on a military base in Germany. His childhood friend died in his arms. His parents drove 2,800 miles to Alaska. He shot baskets in the snow for four years. Won a championship at Duke, made $130 million, won Olympic gold. Then watched his firstborn diagnosed with a disease that could kill him — and conceived twins through IVF whose bone marrow cured their brother. Those twins are now at Duke, chasing the same championship their father won in 2001. And his parents are back in Juneau, where everybody knows their neighbor’s dog’s name.

players.name: Mike Dunleavy Jr.
players.tagline: The coach’s son who carved his own path.
players.stat: 3 Duke seasons • 2001 NCAA Champion (team-high 21 in title game) • Consensus All-American • 15 NBA seasons • 986 games

bio.road:
Michael Joseph Dunleavy Jr. was born September 15, 1980, in Fort Worth, Texas. His father, Mike Dunleavy Sr., was an NBA player whose career spanned 11 seasons before chronic back pain forced his retirement. His mother Emily raised three boys — Mike Jr., Baker, and James — in a household that never stayed in one city long. Basketball was the family business. It still is: Baker played at Villanova and coached at Quinnipiac, James played at USC and became an NBA agent.

When Mike Jr. was ten, his father took the head coaching job with the Los Angeles Lakers. The team featured Magic Johnson and James Worthy. The 1990–91 Lakers reached the NBA Finals before losing to Jordan’s Bulls. A ten-year-old boy had a front-row seat to the end of one era and the beginning of another. ‘Magic Johnson is one of the great athletes, leaders, and winners of all time. To be around somebody like that was great.’

The family moved when the jobs moved. Milwaukee (Bucks), where Mike Jr. attended University School of Milwaukee and Homestead High. Then Portland (Trail Blazers), where his father won NBA Coach of the Year in 1999. Mike transferred to Jesuit High School in Beaverton, Oregon — and did something almost no high schooler gets to do: he practiced against his father’s players. Steve Smith. Bonzi Wells. Rasheed Wallace. ‘Hey, I was a 16-year-old kid playing against pros. It definitely made me better.’ He led Jesuit to the 1999 Oregon 4A State Championship, 65–38 over North Salem.

The biggest lesson was what he saw, not what he was told. ‘Preparation was my dad’s biggest thing. I remember him being up late at night, all night, watching film. That’s something I’ve applied to my craft.’ He chose Duke. Coach K saw in him the most versatile forward since Grant Hill.
bio.duke:
Dunleavy arrived in 1999 alongside Jay Williams, Carlos Boozer, and Chris Duhon. All four became NBA players. Three were first-round picks.

Sophomore year was the championship. Duke went 35–4 with Williams leading the ACC in scoring, Battier winning National POY, Boozer providing muscle inside. Dunleavy was the Swiss Army knife — 6-9 with shooting touch, passing vision, and the basketball IQ of a kid raised in film sessions. In the title game against Arizona: team-high 21 points, including key three-pointers that stretched the lead. Duke won 82–72. That game was the announcement: the coach’s kid wasn’t just along for the ride.

Junior year, with Battier gone: 17.3 PPG, 7.2 RPG. He, Williams, and Boozer each scored 600+ points — a feat matched at Duke only by Scheyer/Singler/Nolan Smith. Consensus second-team All-American. First-team NABC All-American. Duke went 31–4, reached the Sweet Sixteen. He declared for the draft.
bio.after:
Golden State selected Dunleavy 3rd overall in 2002 — one spot behind Duke teammate Jay Williams. Five-year, $44M extension in 2005. But early-2000s Golden State was a wasteland. The #3 pick averaged 12 points on a team that couldn’t make the playoffs. He became an unfair symbol of franchise mediocrity. Traded to Indiana in January 2007.

With the Pacers, Dunleavy became the player Duke always knew he could be. First full season: all 82 starts, career-high 19.1 PPG. A legitimate three-level scoring wing. But injuries (wrist, then nagging issues) slowed him. By the time Indiana returned to the postseason in 2010–11, he’d waited 624 games and nine seasons without a playoff appearance — the longest drought in the Brotherhood.

Milwaukee (2011–13). Then Chicago, where everything clicked: alongside Derrick Rose, Joakim Noah, and Jimmy Butler. April 25, 2014: playoff career-high 35 points with 8 three-pointers against Washington — a Bulls franchise playoff record. Cleveland briefly (2016 championship roster). Atlanta (2017, final stop). Retired June 30, 2017.

Fifteen seasons. Six franchises. 986 games — second-most in the 2002 draft class. Career: 11.2 PPG, 4.5 RPG, 37.7% from three. ~$80M in earnings.
bio.now:
The son of a coach became something his father never was: a general manager of an NBA dynasty.

After retiring, Dunleavy joined the Golden State Warriors’ front office. The franchise that drafted him #3 in 2002 — when the Warriors were terrible and Dunleavy was the symbol of their futility — was now the NBA’s preeminent organization, built around Steph Curry, Klay Thompson, and Draymond Green. He rose through the ranks and was named General Manager.

During the 2025–26 trade deadline, he navigated rumors of a Giannis Antetokounmpo pursuit and managed a franchise in transition — aging champions, salary cap constraints, the pressure to compete while planning for the future. The preparation his father modeled at two in the morning is now his daily reality.

Married Sarah, whom he met at Duke. Three children. A residential sports camp in Eagle River, Wisconsin — Camp Menominee — named its basketball courts ‘The Mike Dunleavy Jr. Courts.’ The Dunleavy family has been in professional basketball, without interruption, for nearly fifty years.

He is 45. Born in Fort Worth. Never stayed anywhere long because his father’s career kept the family moving. Watched Magic Johnson at ten. Guarded Rasheed Wallace at sixteen. Scored a team-high 21 in a championship game at twenty. Was the #3 pick who spent nine years without making the playoffs. Hit 8 threes in a playoff game for the Bulls at thirty-three. Played 986 games and retired quietly at thirty-six. Now he runs the Warriors. The franchise that once represented everything that had gone wrong is the franchise he’s charged with keeping great. The coach’s kid became the coach’s boss.

players.name: Chris Duhon
players.tagline: The four-year floor general.
players.now: Head coach, Gaston Christian School (NC); Greater New Orleans Sports HOF (2024)
