players.stat: 133 starts • 123–21 record • Duke all-time: steals (300), minutes (4,813) • 2001 NCAA Champion • 3 ACC titles

bio.road:
Christopher Nicholas Duhon was born August 31, 1982, in Mamou, Louisiana — a Cajun prairie town of about 3,000 people in the heart of Evangeline Parish. He was raised primarily by his mother, Vivian Harper, a former high school point guard who introduced her son to basketball before he could spell the word. He has a younger brother, Thomas.

The family moved from Mamou to New Orleans, then to Slidell — a suburban community on the north shore of Lake Pontchartrain — when Chris was ten. There was a weathered rim in the family driveway. Duhon shot on it every day. His mother’s guidance was constant. She had been a point guard herself. She knew the position. She understood that the game was about making other people better.

At Salmen High School in Slidell, Duhon became the best player in Louisiana. Senior year: Louisiana Mr. Basketball. McDonald’s All-American. Parade All-American. McDonald’s National HS Player of the Year. Won the McDonald’s Three-Point Shootout. USA Basketball Male Athlete of the Year. Ranked #13 nationally by ESPN, #6 by RSCI.

‘Growing up in Louisiana, LSU was always the school you kind of always wanted to go to. I thought that was the only place I would be able to go.’ But when every program in America called, he chose Duke. ‘I thought Duke gave me the best of both worlds.’
bio.duke:
Duhon arrived in 2000 with Jay Williams, Carlos Boozer, and Mike Dunleavy Jr. He was the point guard — the table-setter. Freshman year: started all 39 games, initially backing up Williams, then moving into the starting lineup alongside him when Boozer was injured. Duke went 35–4 and won the 2001 NCAA Championship.

What made Duhon irreplaceable was durability, steadiness, and defense. 133 starts in four years. 4,813 minutes — most in Duke history. 300 steals — most in Duke history. 819 assists — second in Duke history. A 123–21 record — second-winningest player in ACC history behind teammate Shane Battier. Three ACC Championships. The only ACC player to amass 1,000+ points, 400+ rebounds, 700+ assists, and 200+ steals.

Senior year: 10 PPG, 6 APG, 2.2 SPG, 4.1 RPG. Duke reached the Final Four. Duhon was a finalist for the Wooden Award, Naismith Award, and Rupp Trophy. Not flashy enough to win. Too important to ignore.

What did Coach K teach him? ‘You just learned how to be a man. Everything he taught us was about taking responsibility, and going out and working hard for what you want.’ Regrets about staying four years? ‘No, no regrets at all. Being in college for four years helped me play at a high level. It came with maturity and understanding the game; becoming a man.’
bio.after:
Drafted 38th overall by Chicago in 2004. Rookie year: all 82 games, 5.9 PPG, 4.9 APG. Hit 8 of 9 threes in a comeback win over Atlanta — a Bulls franchise record. In 2006–07, the Bulls won their first playoff series since the Jordan era, sweeping Miami. Duhon was the sixth man who kept the machine running. Career-high 34 points with 9 assists against Golden State in February 2008.

New York Knicks: 2-year, $12M deal. November 29, 2008: 22 assists against Golden State — a Knicks franchise single-game record. The kid from Mamou owned the Garden for one night.

But the most important thing Duhon did had nothing to do with basketball. In August 2005, Hurricane Katrina devastated the Gulf Coast. Slidell was hammered. Duhon founded the Stand Tall Foundation and distributed over 3,000 boxes of supplies worth $450,000 to Slidell residents. Thanksgiving dinners. Free youth clinics. The kid who shot on a weathered driveway rim came back to feed the neighbors who watched him grow up.

Orlando (4-year, $15M). Lakers (2012–13, final season). Nine NBA seasons. 606 games. 6.5 PPG, 4.4 APG. ~$33 million in career earnings. Never an All-Star. Always the glue.
bio.now:
The transition wasn’t smooth. In November 2015, while coaching at Marshall, Duhon was arrested for aggravated DUI. In January 2017, arrested again for driving on a revoked license. He resigned from Marshall three days later. A painful public reckoning for a man who’d built his identity on steadiness.

He rebuilt. Illinois State coaching staff as an assistant (2018). In 2023, his wife Andrea — a mathematics professor — accepted a position at Davidson College. The family relocated to Gastonia, North Carolina. Duhon became head coach of the boys’ basketball team at Gaston Christian School. His eldest son Jeremy plays on the team. Five children: Jeremy, Eliana, Christopher, Faith, and Miya. The family chose the move to be together.

Inducted into the Greater New Orleans Sports Hall of Fame in 2024. His cousin is Jarrett Jack, the longtime NBA point guard. Point guard bloodlines run in the family.

He is 42. Born in Mamou, Louisiana, population 3,000. His mother was a point guard. He shot on a weathered rim in the driveway in Slidell. Chose Duke over LSU. Started 133 games, played more minutes and recorded more steals than anyone in Duke history. Won a championship as a freshman, reached the Final Four as a senior. Set the Knicks’ all-time single-game assist record. When Katrina destroyed his hometown, he drove 3,000 boxes of supplies back to Slidell and fed his neighbors. He stumbled after basketball — the DUI, the resignation — but he stood back up, which is what point guards do. Now he coaches his son’s high school team. His wife teaches math at Davidson. The foundation is still called Stand Tall.

players.name: Luol Deng
players.tagline: From Sudanese refugee to NBA All-Star.
players.now: President, South Sudan Basketball Federation; OBE; led South Sudan to 2024 Olympics
players.drafted: 1st Rd, 7th Overall — Phoenix (traded to Chicago Bulls)
players.stat: 1 Duke season • 15.1 PPG/6.9 RPG • Final Four • 15 NBA seasons • 2x All-Star • All-Defensive Team

bio.road:
Luol Ajou Deng was born April 16, 1985, in Wau, in what is now South Sudan. He is a member of the Dinka ethnic group. His father, Aldo Ajou Deng, was a member of the Sudanese parliament. His mother, Martha Leon John, raised nine children. When Luol was three, his father moved the entire family out of Sudan to escape the Second Sudanese Civil War. They settled in a tiny apartment in Cairo. They were refugees. They had nothing.

‘My parents were my greatest role models. What we went through, how they were always positive. They never lost their edge. My mom is very religious and we always just believed it was going to get better.’

In Egypt, the Deng family met Manute Bol — the former NBA center, 7-foot-7, a Dinka from Sudan who had played for the Bullets, Warriors, and 76ers. After his NBA career, Bol ran a basketball school in Cairo for Sudanese refugees. He taught Luol’s older brother Ajou how to play, and Ajou taught Luol. The game that would carry Deng out of refugee life was delivered by a 7-foot-7 legend in an Egyptian apartment. ‘Manute stood for the right things. He was always going back home and giving back. That’s who he was and basketball didn’t change him.’

The family was granted political asylum in the UK. They settled in Brixton, South London. Luol was about ten. He didn’t speak a word of English. Arabic was his first language. But he was good at football — every kid on the playground wanted him on their team. ‘I was able to learn English quickly because of sports.’ Coach Jimmy Rogers at the Brixton Topcats convinced him to play basketball instead of football.

At fourteen, Deng moved to Blair Academy in New Jersey alone. Another culture shock. By senior year: #2 recruit in America. The only player ranked above him was LeBron James.
bio.duke:
One season at Duke, 2003–04, but it was enough. He averaged 15.1 points and 6.9 rebounds as a freshman — the team’s leading scorer on a roster with Chris Duhon, Shelden Williams, Daniel Ewing, and J.J. Redick. Duke went 31–6 and reached the Final Four before falling to UConn.

Deng played with the maturity and composure of someone who had been through things that made college basketball seem manageable. A refugee from a civil war does not panic in a half-court set. He declared for the draft after one season.
bio.after:
Phoenix selected Deng 7th overall and immediately traded him to Chicago. He spent ten seasons with the Bulls (2004–14). Never the face of the franchise — that was Derrick Rose — but Dime magazine called him ‘the most underrated player in the NBA.’ Coach Thibodeau: ‘The glue that holds the Bulls together.’ GM John Paxson: ‘He really does epitomize everything I had hoped for as a person and a basketball player.’

All-Rookie First Team (2005). Two-time All-Star (2012, 2013). All-Defensive Second Team (2012). Career-high 40 points vs Portland. Six-year, $71M extension. Averaged 43 minutes per game in the 2011 playoffs, praised for defending LeBron in the Eastern Conference Finals.

Cleveland (traded 2014). Miami (two seasons). LA Lakers (4-year, $72M — a contract that became infamous when they benched him during their rebuild). Minnesota (final stop, 2018–19). Fifteen seasons. 900+ games. ~$150M in career earnings. NBA Sportsmanship Award. J. Walter Kennedy Citizenship Award. UN Refugee Agency Humanitarian of the Year (2008). ‘I love basketball, but if I don’t share my success, what’s the point.’
bio.now:
In 2005, one year into his NBA career, Deng founded the Luol Deng Foundation. In 2010, he returned to South Sudan for the first time since fleeing as a toddler. He didn’t recognize anything he saw. He built basketball courts, donated computers to refugee camps, opened the Manute Bol Court in Juba — dedicated to the man who taught him the game in Cairo.

In 2012, he captained Great Britain’s basketball team at the London Olympics. Became a British citizen in 2006. Appointed OBE in 2021. ‘A refugee is a survivor. A refugee is someone that has no choice but to make a difficult decision.’

In 2019, Deng became President of the South Sudan Basketball Federation. Using his own money, he built the national team from nothing into a global force. They qualified for the 2023 FIBA World Cup (their first ever) and the 2024 Paris Olympics — the first time South Sudan, a nation that has existed only since 2011, competed in Olympic basketball. Deng served as assistant coach. The team flew 18-hour flights to Rwanda for training camp. They squeezed big men into exit rows. In Paris, South Sudan defeated Puerto Rico in their Olympic debut.

He is 40. Born in Wau during a civil war. His father packed nine children into a Cairo apartment. Manute Bol taught his brother basketball there, and his brother taught him. A ten-year-old who didn’t speak English learned the language playing football in Brixton. Ranked #2 behind only LeBron. One season at Duke, Final Four. Drafted 7th, fifteen NBA years, two-time All-Star, OBE. Then he went home. He opened the Manute Bol Court. He became president of the basketball federation of a country that didn’t exist when he was born. He funded the national team from his own pocket. He took South Sudan to the Olympics. Because Manute Bol gave him hope in an Egyptian apartment, and Luol Deng decided the only way to repay that was to give it to everyone else.

players.name: Corey Maggette
players.tagline: One of Duke’s first one-and-dones.
players.now: Fox Sports analyst (Clippers/college basketball); Corey Cares Foundation; 2018 BIG3 MVP
players.stat: 1 Duke season • 10.6 PPG • 1999 NCAA Championship Game • 14 NBA seasons • ~$100M career earnings

bio.road:
Corey Antoine Maggette was born November 12, 1979, in Melrose Park, Illinois — a working-class suburb on Chicago’s western edge. His father was Jimmie Maggette. His mother Marguerite was one of five sisters in a family revolving around their father, Reverend Willie Dugan, a Baptist minister. The sisters lived within a short drive of each other — an extended clan bound by church, meals, and proximity.

There was a Christmas when money was tight. Corey’s brother Jimmie declined his own gifts so Corey could have what he needed. A small act of sacrifice in a modest home that crystallized the family’s values: you share what you have, you look out for the people beside you.

Corey found basketball at the Boys & Girls Club at twelve. The dream started there — not on a manicured AAU circuit, but in a community center where kids shot on bent rims. That origin never left him. When he made the NBA, he started Flight 50 Basketball Camp, initially inviting fifty kids.

At Fenwick High School in Oak Park: 23 PPG/10 RPG as a senior. State quarterfinals. Two-time All-State. McDonald’s All-American. Parade All-American. Also competed in the long jump.

The recruiting decision came down to Georgetown and Duke. John Thompson sold him like a father figure. But Coach K had a shared geography. ‘He’s from the South Side of Chicago, so right there you knew it was some type of connection being a fellow Chicagoan.’ The Chicago kid chose the Chicago coach.
bio.duke:
Maggette arrived in 1998 with Elton Brand (#1 recruit in America) and William Avery. Shane Battier was a sophomore. Trajan Langdon a senior. Duke went 37–2 — the best winning percentage in program history — and went undefeated in ACC play.

Maggette was the sixth man, the explosive freshman off the bench. 10.6 PPG, 3.9 RPG. ACC All-Rookie Team. His teammates nicknamed him “Maximus” for his jumping ability. His defining moment: a thunderous follow-up dunk against Temple in the East Regional Final. Julius Erving later compared his explosiveness to a young Vince Carter.

The championship game against UConn: Duke was favored by 9.5 — the biggest title-game spread in twenty years. TV networks showed Duke fans in Durham already celebrating. UConn saw it in their hotel. They won 77–74. Maggette scored 8 in 11 minutes. Langdon traveled in the final seconds. The best Duke team that never won a championship.

Then Maggette, Brand, and Avery made history: the first players to leave Duke early under Coach K. Before 1999, no Blue Devil had ever departed before his eligibility expired. ‘No one in Duke’s history had ever left, especially as a freshman. I felt it was a great opportunity to pursue this dream I started at the Boys & Girls Club.’
bio.after:
A year after leaving, the Myron Piggie shadow fell. Maggette admitted accepting ~$2,000 from his AAU coach while in high school — an NCAA violation. Piggie was convicted. Duke was cleared in 2004 (unaware of payments). But the episode haunted both Maggette and the program.

Drafted 13th by Seattle, traded to Orlando, then to the LA Clippers (June 2000). The Clippers became home for eight years. “Maximus” in Staples Center: a 6-6, 225-pound freight train who lived at the free-throw line, a perennial 15+ PPG scorer. 2001 Slam Dunk Contest. Career-high 39 vs the Lakers. In 2006, the Clippers won their first playoff series since moving from Buffalo.

Golden State: 5-year, $50M. Chris Mullin praised his work ethic as a model for young players. Averaged 19.3 PPG over two seasons as sixth man. Then Milwaukee, Charlotte, Detroit (2013). Fourteen NBA seasons. ~$100M in career earnings.

His family moved to Orlando when he was drafted — Marguerite leaving her sisters behind so her son wouldn’t be alone. Charles Barkley once complimented her parenting. Julius Erving became a mentor. Michael Finley and Juwan Howard, fellow Chicago guys, advised him on saving money.
bio.now:
In 2006, Maggette founded the Corey Cares Foundation — serving, mentoring, and inspiring underprivileged youth through basketball. Partners with Boys & Girls Clubs. Runs the Flight 50 camp (grown from 50 kids to hundreds). Raises scholarship money. Born from the same impulse as that Christmas when his brother gave away his gifts.

In 2017, he joined Ice Cube’s BIG3 league. Opening game: tore his Achilles. Most 37-year-olds would have retired. Maggette came back the next season, was named 2018 BIG3 MVP and Captain of the Year, scored 27 in the championship, and led Power to the title at age 38.

Now a Fox Sports analyst for the LA Clippers and college basketball. Married Milagro Martinez in 2006. Son Sergio is an emerging high school basketball talent in Orange County. Lives in Southern California.

He is 45. Born in Melrose Park. His brother gave up Christmas presents so Corey could have his. Found basketball at the Boys & Girls Club at twelve. Chose Duke because Coach K was from the South Side. Part of the best team that never won — 37-2, 9.5-point favorites, beaten by UConn. One of the first three to ever leave Duke early. The Piggie scandal cast a shadow. Fourteen NBA seasons, $100 million, called Maximus, Charles Barkley complimented his mother’s parenting. Tore his Achilles at 37, won BIG3 MVP at 38. The camp he started with fifty kids is still running. The foundation is called Corey Cares, because that’s the whole point — he does.

players.name: William Avery
players.tagline: Left early; the cautionary tale.

players.name: Dahntay Jones
players.tagline: The enforcer.

players.name: JJ Redick
players.tagline: The most hated player in America. Now he coaches the Lakers.
players.stat: 2,769 pts — Duke all-time scorer • 457 career 3s • Naismith POY

bio.road:
JJ Redick was born in Cookeville, Tennessee, and raised in Roanoke, Virginia, the second of five children in a family wired for basketball. His father, Ken, had played two seasons at Ohio Wesleyan University. His mother, Jeanie, was a nurse. His twin sisters, Catie and Alyssa, both played at Campbell University. His younger sister Abigail played at Virginia Tech and Drexel. His brother David was a football player at Marshall. In the Redick household, competition was the family language.

His nickname came from the twins. As a toddler, the family called him ‘J.’ The sisters kept repeating it — ‘J, J’ — and it stuck. His middle name, Clay, came from his father’s background as a stoneware potter. Jonathan Clay Redick became JJ to the world before he could walk.

At Cave Spring High School in Roanoke, Redick became the best player in Virginia. He averaged 28.3 points, 5 rebounds, and 4 assists as a senior. In the 2002 VHSL Class AAA state championship game, he scored 43 points — a championship-game record across all classifications that stood for 16 years, until Mac McClung broke it in 2018. He finished as Virginia’s all-time AAA leading scorer with 2,215 career points and shot over 44 percent from three. He played AAU ball for the Boo Williams All-Stars, whose national championship teams faced future NBA stars like Dwyane Wade.

He was the 2002 McDonald’s All-American Game MVP, scoring 26 points on 10-of-15 shooting and winning the three-point competition. Ranked the No. 2 shooting guard and No. 13 overall prospect in the country. Virginia Mr. Basketball. Parade All-American. Every major program wanted him. He chose Duke.
bio.duke:
JJ Redick left Duke as the most prolific scorer in school history and one of the most decorated — and most despised — players in the history of college basketball. He scored 2,769 career points, a Duke record. He made 457 career three-pointers, an NCAA record at the time of his graduation. He set the ACC single-season free throw percentage record at .953 during his sophomore year, including a streak of 54 consecutive free throws. He led Duke to a 116-23 record, three ACC Tournament championships, and a Final Four. He was a two-time National Player of the Year, two-time ACC Player of the Year, two-time ACC Tournament MVP, three-time All-American, and the 2006 Sullivan Award winner as the nation’s top amateur athlete. His jersey No. 4 was retired in February 2007.

Freshman (2002–03): The Arrival. Redick led the team with 30 points in the ACC Tournament championship game against NC State. He averaged 15.0 points per game — the fourth-highest freshman scoring average in Duke history behind only Johnny Dawkins, Gene Banks, and Mike Gminski. He set the ACC and Duke freshman record with a .919 free throw percentage. He made 95 three-pointers.

Sophomore (2003–04): The Final Four — and the Breaking Point. Duke reached the Final Four, where they fell to Connecticut. Redick was named to the All-Final Four team. But the season was defined by something darker: JJ Redick had become the most hated player in college basketball. Students from rival schools discovered his cell phone number; he estimated receiving 50 to 75 hate calls per day. Opposing crowds screamed vulgar things about his 12-year-old sister. Custom t-shirts with his photo that read ‘When I grow up, I want to name my kid JJ Redick’ on the front and ‘and beat him every day’ on the back. An NFL team owner talking trash to him courtside. An entire arena chanting an obscenity. He nearly quit. In December of his sophomore year, he had his twin sisters meet him on campus for dinner. ‘I don’t want to play anymore,’ he told them. ‘This is not for me. It’s not fun.’ He turned to writing poetry as an outlet. CBS Sports would later name him the ‘most hated current athlete in America.’

The Transformation: Summer Before Junior Year. Coach K sat him down in late May and told him the truth: ‘You’ve been good. You were All-ACC and a third-team All-American. We made the Final Four. So you’ve been good, but what you’re doing isn’t good enough.’ Krzyzewski kept him from playing USA Basketball that summer. Redick walked around campus carrying a jug of water. He lost 23 pounds, dropping from over 215 to 192. He won every preseason conditioning test.

Junior (2004–05): The transformed Redick averaged 21.8 points per game and won ACC Player of the Year and consensus National Player of the Year honors. Duke earned a No. 1 seed but lost in the Sweet Sixteen. He was playing over 37 minutes per game now, leaner, faster, more dynamic, able to create his own shot rather than just catch and shoot.

Senior (2005–06): The Record-Breaker. Redick averaged 26.8 points per game — a Duke single-season record. He broke Curtis Staples’ NCAA career three-point record against Wake Forest. He passed Dickie Hemric’s 51-year-old ACC career scoring record with a pair of free throws against Temple. He won every major national player of the year award: Naismith, Wooden, AP, NABC. He was a two-time team captain, a history major with a minor in cultural anthropology, and still the most hated player in America.

The final act was bittersweet. In the 2006 NCAA Tournament, LSU’s Garrett Temple — a 6’5 defensive specialist with a long wingspan — chased Redick relentlessly. The nation’s No. 2 scorer shot 3-for-18 and scored just 11 points. Duke lost.
bio.after:
The Orlando Magic selected Redick with the 11th pick in the 2006 draft. His early years were a test of patience. He competed for minutes, battled back spasms, and spent stretches as a third-string player. In January 2008, he posted on his blog that it had ‘been proven that even if I play well in the limited minutes I get, not much is going to change.’ He asked his agent about a trade. But he kept working. By the 2008-09 season, he was helping the Magic reach the NBA Finals. It was the first of 13 consecutive playoff appearances for Redick.

A three-team sign-and-trade sent Redick to the Clippers on a four-year, $27 million deal. He became a full-time starter for the first time in his career at age 29 — starting 218 of his first 219 games. He scored a career-high 40 points against Houston in January 2016 on 9-of-12 from three. He led the NBA in three-point shooting at 47.5% during the 2015-16 season. He made 200 or more three-pointers in four of five seasons from 2014 to 2019.

Redick signed a one-year, $23 million deal with Philadelphia in 2017 and posted his best overall statistical season in 2018-19: 18.1 points per game on 44% shooting and 39.7% from three. He moved to New Orleans in 2019, was traded to Dallas in 2021, and retired on September 21 of that year. His farewell was quintessentially Redick — announced on his own podcast: ‘I started playing basketball 30 years ago in my backyard on an uneven patch of dirt, gravel, and grass.’ Fifteen seasons. 940 games. 12,028 points. 1,950 career three-pointers — 15th most in NBA history.

In January 2016, while still an active player with the Clippers, Redick launched a podcast on Yahoo! Sports — becoming the first active NBA player to host one. The podcast evolved through multiple platforms. In August 2020, Redick and producer Tommy Alter co-founded ThreeFourTwo Productions — the name a reference to the 342 shots Redick took every Sunday during the offseason as part of his shooting regimen. Their flagship, ‘The Old Man and the Three,’ launched during the NBA’s COVID bubble with Damian Lillard as the first guest. It became one of the most popular sports podcasts in the world, amassing over 320 million YouTube views. The title was a play on Hemingway — Redick saw himself as the aging craftsman, still learning. After retirement, he joined ESPN as an analyst and quickly rose to the lead broadcast team by February 2024. LeBron James shared a clip and captioned it ‘JJ FOR PRESIDENT!!’ In March 2024, the two launched a joint podcast, ‘Mind the Game,’ focused on basketball strategy and analysis.
bio.now:
On June 26, 2024, the Los Angeles Lakers hired JJ Redick as their 29th head coach, signing him to a four-year contract. He had never coached at any level. The hire came after the Lakers’ pursuit of Dan Hurley was rebuffed (Hurley turned down a six-year, $70 million offer to stay at UConn). GM Rob Pelinka was sold on Redick’s basketball IQ, his ability to connect with players, and his relationship with LeBron James.

Redick’s first season exceeded expectations. He coached the Lakers to a 50-32 record, the third-best in the Western Conference, despite navigating a mid-season trade for Luka Doncic and the challenge of integrating ball-dominant superstars. The Lakers made the playoffs but were eliminated by the Minnesota Timberwolves in five games in the first round.

The season was also marked by personal devastation. On January 7, 2025, the Palisades Fire swept through Los Angeles. Redick’s rental home in Pacific Palisades burned to the ground. Chelsea evacuated their sons, Knox and Kai, while the team was on the road in Dallas. Everything they owned from nearly 20 years together was gone: his son’s charcoal painting of a lighthouse, the boys’ basketball card collections saved up from birthday money and chores, Chelsea’s engagement ring. Austin Reaves embraced him at the next practice.

In September 2025, the Lakers extended Redick’s contract through the 2029-30 season. He spent the offseason meeting with Rams coach Sean McVay to pick his brain on offensive concepts. He has set a remarkable NBA record: the Lakers are 57-4 when leading after three quarters under his watch, the best mark in league history.

He lives in Los Angeles with his wife, Chelsea Kilgore Redick, whom he married in 2010. They have two sons, Knox and Kai. His podcast empire continues without him — ‘The Old Man and the Three’ was rebranded as ‘The Young Man and the Three,’ with Cam Johnson of the Nuggets taking the host chair. Redick has sworn off social media to focus on coaching. ‘I am excommunicated from the content space,’ he said with a grin.

He has four tattoos of Bible verses on his body: Isaiah 40:31, Joshua 1:9, Psalm 40:1–3, and Philippians 4:13, plus a sleeve of the Virgin Mary. He graduated from Duke with a degree in history and a minor in cultural anthropology. He still holds Duke’s all-time scoring record. His net worth is estimated at approximately $50 million.

The transformation is the story. A kid from Roanoke who shot on an uneven patch of dirt, gravel, and grass. The most hated player in America who nearly quit because strangers were making signs about beating his future children. A poet who couldn’t find another way to process the pain. A role player who reinvented himself three times — as a starter, as a media mogul, and now as the coach of the Lakers.

players.name: Shelden Williams
players.tagline: The Landlord.
players.now: Coach/mentor; Shelden Williams Foundation; G League asst. coach
players.stat: 139 games • 13.9 ppg • 9.1 rpg • 3.0 bpg • 2x NABC DPOY • Duke career blocks/rebounds leader

bio.road:
Shelden DeMar Williams was born October 21, 1983, in Oklahoma City, Oklahoma, the son of educators Bob and Jeannette Williams. His father played basketball at Oklahoma Christian University and coached high school and junior high basketball. His brother Quincy played at North Texas. The value of learning was instilled early — Shelden was a member of the National Honor Society and the Fellowship of Christian Athletes.

At Midwest City High School, Williams became one of the most dominant big men in the country. As a senior, he averaged 20 points, 12 rebounds, three blocked shots, and three assists. He was named Gatorade Oklahoma Player of the Year in both 2001 and 2002, a Parade All-American, and rated the nation’s fifth-best prep player. His nickname — ‘The Landlord’ — stuck from high school, a nod to his ability to own the paint. He chose Duke, arriving in the fall of 2002 alongside J.J. Redick and Sean Dockery.
bio.duke:
Williams was the most dominant defensive player in Duke history. Over four seasons, he averaged 13.9 points, 9.1 rebounds, and 3.0 blocked shots while shooting 57.2% from the floor. He became the first player in NCAA history to register 1,750 points, 1,250 rebounds, 400 blocked shots, and 150 steals. He holds Duke’s career records in rebounds (1,217) and blocked shots (422), as well as the single-season blocks record (137 as a senior). He is the third player in ACC history to reach 1,500 points, 1,000 rebounds, 350 blocks, and 150 steals — joining Tim Duncan and Ralph Sampson.

He won the NABC Defensive Player of the Year in both 2005 and 2006, only the fifth player in history to win it consecutively. Three-time All-ACC, three-time All-ACC Tournament, two-time ACC Defensive Player of the Year, consensus All-American as a senior. His signature game: January 11, 2006, against Maryland — 19 points, 11 rebounds, and 10 blocked shots, only the third triple-double in Duke basketball history.

He was a tri-captain as a senior, helping the Blue Devils to a 116–23 four-year record, two ACC regular season titles, three ACC Tournament championships, four Sweet Sixteens, and the 2004 Final Four. Duke retired his No. 23 on January 28, 2007. Duke Athletics Hall of Fame, 2018. He graduated with a degree in sociology and a certificate in Markets and Management.
bio.after:
The Atlanta Hawks selected Williams 5th overall in the 2006 Draft. He showed flashes — 21 points and 10 rebounds against Indiana as a rookie, Eastern Conference Rookie of the Month for April 2007 — but his college dominance never fully translated. In February 2008, he was traded to the Sacramento Kings in the Mike Bibby deal. He bounced through seven NBA teams: Atlanta, Sacramento, Minnesota, Boston (part of the 2008 championship roster), Denver, New York Knicks, and New Jersey Nets.

NBA career totals: 4.5 points, 4.3 rebounds across 228 games. Approximately $12.3 million earned. After his last NBA season in 2011–12, he played overseas: Élan Chalon in France and Tianjin Ronggang in China for two seasons. He retired from playing around 2015.

In 2008, Williams married WNBA star Candace Parker. Their daughter, Lailaa Nicole Williams, was born in 2009. The couple divorced in 2016.
bio.now:
In 2019, Williams founded the Shelden Williams Foundation, dedicated to empowering student-athletes and their families through sports, education, and community involvement. The foundation operates in Oklahoma, Atlanta, and Durham — the three communities that shaped his life.

Williams has transitioned into coaching, serving as an assistant coach for the College Park Skyhawks in the NBA G League — the Atlanta Hawks’ affiliate, bringing his career full circle to the franchise that drafted him. He mentors young players on defense, positioning, and the mental discipline that made him The Landlord at Cameron Indoor.

At 42, his No. 23 hangs in the Cameron rafters. The records he set in blocks and rebounds may stand for decades. The son of educators who became one himself — the kid from Oklahoma City who owned every paint he stepped into is still teaching others how to claim theirs.

players.name: Gerald Henderson
players.tagline: The spark that reignited the engine.
players.stat: 103 games • First-Team All-ACC 2009 • McDonald’s AA dunk champion • 535 NBA games • $37.4M career earnings

bio.road:
Gerald Henderson Jr. was born into basketball royalty and raised in the shadow of one of the most famous steals in NBA history. His father, Gerald Henderson Sr., was a 13-year NBA veteran who won three championship rings — two with the Boston Celtics (1981, 1984) and one with the Detroit Pistons (1990). He played alongside Larry Bird, Robert Parish, Kevin McHale, Patrick Ewing, Charles Barkley, Isiah Thomas, and Joe Dumars. His signature moment: the 1984 NBA Finals against the Lakers, when he stole a James Worthy pass and scored a game-tying layup that forced overtime. The Celtics won the game and the series.

Gerald Jr. was born December 9, 1987, in Caldwell, New Jersey, while his father was playing for the 76ers. Youngest of three children — older brother Jermaine (basketball at Saint Rose) and sister Jade (graduated from Villanova). The father played his son one-on-one in the backyard and never let him win. ‘I beat him that last time when he was in ninth, tenth grade,’ Henderson Sr. said. ‘I played him until I got to the point where I said, I can’t take this punishment no more. He was getting bigger and stronger. That athleticism — that’s something you can’t teach.’

‘I’ve seen that steal hundreds of times,’ Gerald Jr. said of his father’s defining moment. When the 2009 NCAA East Regional was held at Madison Square Garden, the Boston Globe ran a feature: ‘Son is in Forecast.’ Gerald Jr. was asked what he knew about his dad’s era. ‘All he talks about is our shorts and how the guys didn’t used to dunk as much.’

At Episcopal Academy in Merion, Pennsylvania: five-sport phenom. 18.4 ppg, 8.6 rpg as a junior; 25–3 record; state champion. Scratch golfer with all-conference honors. Two-time league champion in high jump and triple jump. His teammate was Wayne Ellington, a future four-year UNC starter. McDonald’s All-American. Won the 2006 McDonald’s dunk contest — the second Duke player to win it (after Ricky Price in 1994). Also won the Naismith Sportsmanship Award. Led the East with 16 points.
bio.duke:
Freshman year (2006–07): 6.8 ppg off the bench. Raw and electric — 45 dunks as a sophomore, the most by a Duke wing since Grant Hill’s 48 in 1991–92. Sophomore year: starting lineup, 12.7 ppg, leading the team in scoring seven times and rebounding eight times. In the nine biggest games — two UNC, two Clemson, Davidson, Wisconsin, two NCAA Tournament games — he averaged 14.2 ppg, 5.7 rpg, 1.6 blocks, above his season averages across the board.

The Belmont game. First round, 2008 NCAA Tournament, Washington’s Verizon Center. No. 15 Belmont led Duke by one with two minutes left. Henderson had kept Duke alive with 21 points on 8-of-14. With 11.9 seconds left, Duke facing elimination, Henderson drove coast-to-coast and tipped in the game-winning shot. DeMarcus Nelson preserved the win with a steal. Duke 71, Belmont 70. DBR described it as one of the closest calls in Duke tournament history.

Junior year (2008–09): unquestioned leader. 16.5 ppg. First-Team All-ACC — first Duke player to earn it since Redick in 2006. ACC Tournament champion. No. 2 seed. He left for the draft after three seasons.
bio.after:
Charlotte Bobcats, 12th overall, 2009. Six seasons in Charlotte — the longest stretch of his career. During the catastrophic 2011–12 season (7–59, worst record in NBA history), Henderson was one of the lone bright spots, averaging a career-high 15.1 points. Traded to Portland (2015). Signed with Philadelphia (2016). Career: 535 games, 333 starts, 11.2 ppg, $37.4 million.

In August 2017, hip surgery. Missed the entire 2017–18 season. Tried to come back in 2018 — worked out for teams, then tore his Achilles in a pickup game. The comeback was over before it started. January 2019: joined the Charlotte Hornets broadcast team. April 30, 2019: officially retired.
bio.now:
Gerald Henderson Jr. is 37 years old. Hornets broadcast analyst in Charlotte, close to the franchise that drafted him. His father and mother run a real estate business in Blue Bell, Pennsylvania.

His Brotherhood story is the quietest of the Between Era — and the most shaped by inheritance. He grew up watching his father’s championship steal on replay. Played one-on-one against a three-time NBA champion and lost every time until high school. Won the McDonald’s dunk contest — the second Henderson to define a moment on national television. Tipped in a shot with 11.9 seconds left to save Duke from a 15-seed upset. Was the best player on the worst team in NBA history. Tried to come back from hip surgery and tore his Achilles in a pickup game.

His father stole the ball from James Worthy and changed a Finals. His son tipped in a shot against Belmont and changed a first-round game. The scale is different. The gene is the same. Gerald Jr. never won a championship. But he carried the name for eight NBA seasons, and when the body said no, he picked up a microphone. The Henderson basketball story spans two generations, three rings (all his father’s), one legendary steal, one legendary tip-in, and a family that understood: you play until it doesn’t let you anymore.

players.name: Josh McRoberts
players.tagline: Flair, flash, and an early exit.
players.now: Assistant coach, Carmel HS; Hamilton County Basketball HOF
players.stat: 69 games • 10.8 PPG • 6.5 RPG • McDonald’s AA Game MVP • ACC All-Freshman • 11 NBA seasons

bio.road:
Josh McRoberts grew up in Carmel, Indiana, in a basketball-centric family. His father Tim was a Carmel High School alumnus who had played on the basketball team. His mother Jennifer was a teacher. One of his most influential mentors was the late Eric Clark, a beloved Carmel administrator and coach for whom the school’s athletic facilities were named.

What separates McRoberts from most blue-chip recruits is the modesty of his original ambition. In a 2016 WTHR interview, he stood on the Carmel court and said: ‘I just love playing basketball. Honestly my biggest dream was probably to play here, to play at Carmel High School. That was my biggest aspiration. So everything after that was like a bonus to me.’ His picture hangs on the wall outside the gym.

At Carmel: 17.9 ppg, 11.4 rpg, 4.2 apg, 3.0 bpg on 59.3% shooting as a senior. Career: 1,264 points, 759 rebounds, 255 assists, 259 blocks. No. 1 power forward nationally (Rivals/Scout). Morgan Wootten Award (McDonald’s Player of the Year). McDonald’s All-American Game MVP (17 pts on 7-of-8, game-high 12 rebounds). First-team Parade and USA Today All-American. Indiana Mr. Basketball finalist. Also a scratch golfer and two-time league champion in high jump and triple jump.
bio.duke:
McRoberts arrived in 2005 as the headliner of what was supposed to be the nation’s top class. Duke Basketball Report would later call it ‘a major disappointment — at least in Duke terms.’ Boateng and Boykin transferred. Pocius never contributed. Paulus finished playing football at Syracuse. Only McRoberts delivered.

A Sports Illustrated feature captured J.J. Redick driving McRoberts home in his 2001 Toyota Corolla. McRoberts’s 6-foot-10 frame jammed in, knees near his chin. ‘It seems like J.J.’s always driving me around, and I’m always paying for his gas.’ Coach K compared McRoberts’s potential to Grant Hill’s.

Freshman: 8.7 ppg, 5.3 rpg, 60.5% FG. ACC All-Freshman. Duke went 32–4, ACC regular-season and tournament champs. Before sophomore year: lumbar discectomy for chronic back problems. Named co-captain with Paulus — first two sophomore captains in Duke history. December 19 vs Kent State: 19 pts, 6 blocks, 6 reb, 4 ast, 4 stl — the first Duke player to tally 4+ in all five categories. All-ACC and ACC All-Defense.

Then VCU. First round, 2007 NCAA Tournament. McRoberts played all 40 minutes: 22 pts, 12 reb, 5 blocks, 4 ast. Missed two of four free throws in the final 1:41. Eric Maynor hit a game-winner with 1.8 seconds left. Duke lost 79–77. Draft stock fell from lottery to 37th — the sharpest decline of any Duke player in the Brotherhood.
bio.after: Eleven NBA seasons, seven teams: Portland, Indiana (traded on draft night 2008 to his hometown Pacers), Lakers (part of the Dwight Howard blockbuster), Orlando, Charlotte, Miami ($23M contract), Dallas. Pau Gasol called him ‘scrappy and a hustler.’ Best season: 2013–14 Charlotte, all 78 games started, career-high 24 pts. Torn meniscus in Miami. Stress fracture. Waived by Dallas February 2018. ~$30M career earnings across ~450 games.
bio.now: Josh McRoberts came home. During the 2011 NBA lockout, his brother Zach was a Carmel sophomore. In 2020–21, during COVID, McRoberts officially joined the Carmel varsity staff as an assistant. ‘Having been part of a team for as long as I had, I felt the absence of that kind of inclusion and purpose.’ Hamilton County Basketball Hall of Fame. He is 38, coaching the next generation of Greyhounds in the gym named after his mentor Eric Clark. Everything after Carmel was a bonus. The bonus brought him back to where he started.

players.name: Greg Paulus
players.tagline: Then he tried to play quarterback at Syracuse.

players.name: DeMarcus Nelson
players.tagline: The unsung defender.

players.name: Jon Scheyer
players.tagline: From player to the throne.

players.name: Nolan Smith
players.tagline: His father’s legacy. His own name.

players.name: Mason Plumlee
players.tagline: Three Plumlees. This was the best one.
players.stat: 144 games • 10.5 ppg • 8.1 rpg • 57.4% FG • 2010 NCAA champ • FIBA gold 2014

bio.road:
Josh McRoberts grew up in Carmel, Indiana, a prosperous suburb north of Indianapolis, in a basketball-centric family. His father Tim was a Carmel High School alumnus who had played on the basketball team. His mother Jennifer was a teacher. One of his most influential mentors was the late Eric Clark, a beloved Carmel administrator and coach for whom the school’s athletic facilities were named.

What separates McRoberts from most blue-chip recruits is the modesty of his original ambition. In a 2016 WTHR interview, he stood on the Carmel court and said: ‘I just love playing basketball. Honestly my biggest dream was probably to play here, to play at Carmel High School. That was my biggest aspiration. So everything after that was like a bonus to me.’ His picture hangs on the wall outside the gym.

At Carmel: 17.9 ppg, 11.4 rpg, 4.2 apg, 3.0 bpg on 59.3% shooting as a senior. Career totals: 1,264 points, 759 rebounds, 255 assists, 259 blocks. No. 1 power forward nationally (Rivals/Scout). Morgan Wootten Award (McDonald’s Player of the Year). McDonald’s All-American Game MVP (17 pts on 7-of-8, game-high 12 rebounds). First-team Parade and USA Today All-American. Indiana Mr. Basketball finalist. Also a scratch golfer with all-conference honors and two-time league champion in high jump and triple jump.
bio.duke:
McRoberts arrived in 2005 as the headliner of what was supposed to be the nation’s top class. Duke Basketball Report would later call it ‘a major disappointment — at least in Duke terms.’ Boateng and Boykin transferred. Pocius never contributed. Paulus finished playing football at Syracuse. Only McRoberts delivered on his ranking — and even he fell short of the standard set for him.

But before the disappointment, there was the bonding. A Sports Illustrated feature captured J.J. Redick, the most famous player in college basketball, driving McRoberts home from practice in his 2001 Toyota Corolla. McRoberts’s 6-foot-10 frame jammed into the passenger seat, knees near his chin. ‘It seems like J.J.’s always driving me around, and I’m always paying for his gas.’ They played video games instead of going to clubs. Redick, asked what he thought during the NCAA president’s ‘message: graduate’ speech, cracked: ‘That there’s no way this dude’s going to graduate.’ Coach K compared McRoberts’s potential to Grant Hill’s.

Freshman year: 8.7 ppg, 5.3 rpg, 60.5% FG. ACC All-Freshman. Duke went 32–4, won ACC regular-season and tournament titles, reached the Sweet Sixteen. Before sophomore year: lumbar discectomy for chronic back problems. Named co-captain with Paulus — first two sophomore captains in Duke history. December 19 vs Kent State: 19 points, 6 blocks, 6 rebounds, 4 assists, 4 steals — the first Duke player to tally 4+ in all five categories. All-ACC. ACC All-Defense.

Then VCU. First round, 2007 NCAA Tournament. McRoberts played all 40 minutes: 22 points, 12 rebounds, 5 blocks, 4 assists. But he missed two of four free throws in the final 1:41. Eric Maynor hit a game-winning jumper with 1.8 seconds left. Duke lost 79–77. Krzyzewski: ‘Josh and Greg really carried us.’ It wasn’t enough. McRoberts declared for the draft. A year earlier he’d been a projected lottery pick. Portland took him 37th — second round. The sharpest draft-stock decline of any Duke player in the Brotherhood.
bio.after:
Eleven NBA seasons, seven teams: Portland, Indiana (traded on draft night 2008 to his hometown Pacers), Lakers (part of the Dwight Howard blockbuster), Orlando, Charlotte, Miami, Dallas. Not a star. Not an All-Star. Pau Gasol, his Lakers teammate, called him ‘scrappy and a hustler.’ A screen-setter with excellent passing skills for a big man whose scoring came off fast breaks, rebounds, and hustle plays.

Best season: 2013–14 Charlotte, all 78 games started, career-high 24 points vs Orlando. Biggest contract: Miami Heat, four years, $23 million (July 2014). But a torn meniscus 17 games in wiped out the first season. Hassan Whiteside took his spot. Bruised knee, stress fracture, traded to Dallas, waived February 2018. Approximately $30 million in career earnings across ~450 games.
bio.now:
Josh McRoberts came home. During the 2011 NBA lockout, his brother Zach was a sophomore at Carmel. Coach Heady let Josh hang around the team for a month. He loved it. In 2020–21, during COVID, McRoberts officially joined the Carmel varsity staff as an assistant under Ryan Osborn. ‘Having been part of a team for as long as I had, I felt the absence of that kind of inclusion and purpose,’ he said. ‘The help from my coaches in high school and having a support system that included my family and the Carmel community was a big thing for me.’

Hamilton County Basketball Hall of Fame. He is 38 years old. His picture still hangs on the wall outside the gym. He watches junior high kids chase the dream on the same court.

McRoberts’s story is the most honest in the Between Era about what happens when the hype doesn’t match the outcome. No. 1 power forward nationally. McDonald’s Game MVP. The player Coach K compared to Grant Hill. Headliner of Duke’s most disappointing class. Back surgery before sophomore year. Made the first five-category stat line in Duke history, then watched Maynor’s jumper end his college career with 1.8 seconds left. Draft stock fell from lottery to 37th. Eleven years, seven teams, scrappy and a hustler. Then he went home — back to Carmel, back to the court where his biggest dream was simply to play. Everything after that was a bonus. And the bonus brought him back to where he started.

players.name: Seth Curry
players.tagline: The other Curry.
players.now: Golden State Warriors (with brother Steph); 7th all-time NBA career 3PT%
players.stat: 99 Duke games • Sr: 17.5 PPG/43.8% 3PT • All-ACC 1st Team • 12+ NBA seasons • Led NBA 3PT% 2024–25

bio.road:
Seth Adham Curry was born August 23, 1990, in Charlotte, North Carolina. His father, Dell Curry, was in the middle of a 16-year NBA career — most of it with the Charlotte Hornets, where he became the franchise’s all-time leading scorer. His mother, Sonya Adams Curry, had played college volleyball at Virginia Tech. His older brother was Steph. His younger sister was Sydel, who would play volleyball at Elon. This was the first family of Charlotte sports.

As children, Dell took Seth and Steph to Hornets games. They shot around with the team during warm-ups — learning the geometry of the three-point line before they were old enough to reach it. They played on the backyard court constantly, competitive to the point of physical fights their mother had to break up. Both wore #30 in honor of their father. Both inherited the shooting gene.

But Steph was the older one, the one who broke out first, who made Davidson a national story. By the time Seth was a senior at Charlotte Christian (22.3 PPG, 5.0 APG, 105–24 team record, all-state, academic honor roll all four years), his brother was leading Davidson to the Elite Eight. Seth received two- and three-star recruiting ratings. Despite being Dell Curry’s son, the major programs didn’t come calling. He chose Liberty University.

At Liberty, he exploded: 20.2 PPG as a freshman — best among all freshmen nationally. Broke the Big South Conference freshman scoring record with 707 points. Scored 26 against Virginia. Big South Freshman of the Year. Dick Vitale’s ‘Diaper Dandy.’ After one season, he transferred to Duke.
bio.duke:
Curry sat out 2009–10 per transfer rules. He watched from the bench as Duke won the national championship. He wore #30. He waited.

His first playable season, Kyrie Irving — the most talented freshman in college basketball — ran the show. Irving averaged 17.4 PPG through eight games. Then a toe injury knocked him out for 26 games. Curry was named the starter. He never really gave it back. With Irving out, he averaged 9.6 PPG shooting 42.2% from three. Duke went 32–5, reached the Sweet Sixteen. Irving returned for the tournament, scored 28 against Arizona, and was drafted #1 overall. Curry had kept the ship afloat.

Junior year, alongside Austin Rivers: 13.2 PPG, led the ACC in free throw percentage (87.3%). All-ACC Third Team. Duke went 27–7.

Senior year was the coronation. 17.5 PPG, 43.8% from three. Career-high 31 against Santa Clara when Duke was ranked #1. All-ACC First Team. Second-team All-American (Sporting News). NCAA All-Region. Duke went 30–6, reached the Elite Eight.

He went undrafted. Not a single team used a pick on Dell Curry’s son and Steph Curry’s brother. The kid overlooked at every turn — by recruiting services, by major programs, by the draft — would have to find another way.
bio.after:
On his 23rd birthday, Curry signed a non-guaranteed contract with Golden State — Steph’s team. Six preseason games. Waived. Assigned to the Santa Cruz Warriors (D-League), where he played in the backcourt with Mychel Thompson while their brothers formed the ‘Splash Brothers’ upstairs.

The NBA journey was a masterclass in persistence. Memphis (waived after 1 game). Cleveland (10-day contract, 19 games). Phoenix. Erie BayHawks. Sacramento (first real chance: averaged 15.2 PPG over the final 11 games). Dallas (70 games, 12.8 PPG). Portland — where he was selected for the 2019 Three-Point Contest and reached the Western Conference Finals. Portland’s opponent: Steph and Golden State. First time brothers faced each other in an NBA playoff series. Golden State swept 4–0. Seth called it a dream come true.

Dallas again: career-high 37 points on 13-of-15 shooting, 8-of-9 from three against Miami — one of the most efficient scoring games in NBA history. Philadelphia under Doc Rivers (2020–22): career-best 15.0 PPG, 49/42/87 splits. Traded to Brooklyn in the James Harden deal. Dallas again. Charlotte — his hometown, his father’s franchise. Through all of it: career 43.3% from three, seventh all-time in NBA history. Led the entire NBA in 3PT% for 2024–25 at 45.6%.
bio.now:
On October 1, 2025, Seth Curry signed with the Golden State Warriors. His brother’s team. For the first time since those 2013 preseason games that ended with a waiver, the Curry brothers are NBA teammates. Steph is 37, the greatest shooter in basketball history. Seth is 35, seventh all-time in three-point percentage, a man who has played for ten-plus teams and never stopped shooting.

He married Callie Rivers on September 14, 2019. Callie is a former Florida Gators volleyball player — the daughter of Doc Rivers, the sister of Austin Rivers, Seth’s Duke teammate. The Duke-to-in-laws pipeline is real. Three children: Carter Lynn (2018), Cash (2021), Cruz (2023).

In January 2026, the Charlotte Hornets retired Dell Curry’s #30. The ceremony reunited the family in the city where it started — where Dell became a franchise icon, where Steph and Seth shot around during warm-ups as children, where the backyard court produced two NBA shooters. Seth said: ‘The coolest thing for me is that I would be the last person to wear number 30.’

Every family has the golden child and the other one. Seth Curry was always the other one. Two-star recruit. Liberty. Transfer. Sat on the bench while Duke won a championship. Stepped in when Kyrie went down. All-American. Undrafted. D-League. Waived by his brother’s team. Ten franchises. The slowest rise in Brotherhood history. Now he stands seventh all-time in NBA three-point percentage, playing alongside the greatest shooter who ever lived — who also happens to be the kid who used to beat him up on the backyard court in Charlotte. He just kept shooting until the world had no choice but to notice.

players.name: Quinn Cook
players.tagline: Four years. One ring. Two more in the NBA.
players.now: Playing overseas; 4th Quarter Sports Bar, Bowie MD
players.stat: 1,571 pts • 143 games • 2015 NCAA champ • 2x NBA champ (Warriors/Lakers)

bio.road:
Quinn Alexander Cook was born March 23, 1993, in Washington, D.C., the son of Ted and Janet Cook, both graduates of Howard University who met while attending the school. Ted Cook was a former star basketball player at Glenville High School in the late 1970s who became an entrepreneur in the restaurant business. Ted and Janet ran 4th and Goal, a sports bar on 11th Street and Pennsylvania Avenue in D.C. Quinn’s earliest childhood memories are of that bar — shooting on a miniature Fisher-Price hoop inside at age four while his parents worked.

Ted was a lifelong Los Angeles Lakers fan who instilled in his son a love for basketball from the beginning. Quinn had his own basketball as a toddler and was playing organized ball by age ten. But tragedy struck early: Ted Cook died of a heart attack in 2008, when Quinn was just fourteen and had only recently begun playing at DeMatha Catholic High School. The loss was devastating. Quinn missed games. It took time to escape the trauma. After Ted’s death, Janet supported the family by working two jobs in security and event staffing.

At DeMatha — the same school that produced Danny Ferry — Cook played three seasons. The Stags went 85–18 and were ranked No. 1 in Maryland during his junior year. He was named the Washington Post All-Met Player of the Year as a junior, the first junior to receive that honor in 30 years. His teammates included future NBA players Jerami Grant, Jerian Grant, and Victor Oladipo.

Cook transferred to Oak Hill Academy for his senior year, leading the Warriors to a 31–4 record while averaging 19.1 points, 10.9 assists, and 2.5 steals. McDonald’s All-American. He chose Duke over Villanova, UCLA, and North Carolina. When asked why: ‘The reason I chose Duke is because of Coach K.’
bio.duke:
Cook played all four years at Duke, amassing 1,571 career points (28th on Duke’s all-time list), becoming one of only seven Blue Devils to record 1,000 points and 500 assists, and finishing fourth in career free-throw percentage at .853.

As a freshman in 2011–12, he backed up Austin Rivers and Seth Curry, averaging 4.4 points in 11.7 minutes. As a sophomore, he moved into the starting lineup and led the team with 5.3 assists per game while averaging 11.7 points. Each year, Cook grew — not in dramatic fashion, but steadily, like a player building a house one brick at a time.

The payoff came as a senior in 2014–15. Cook averaged 15.3 points and 2.6 assists and was named All-ACC Third Team. He was the emotional engine of the team — the four-year player holding together a roster of talented freshmen (Jahlil Okafor, Justise Winslow, Tyus Jones) who would leave after one season. On April 6, 2015, Duke defeated Wisconsin 68–63 to win the national championship. Jones was named Final Four MOP. But Cook was the glue — the senior who knew the system, who had survived four years of development, who gave that team its backbone. He graduated with a degree in sociology.
bio.after:
Cook went undrafted in 2015. What followed was one of the most remarkable persistence stories in recent basketball: a grinding, multi-year odyssey through the G League, 10-day contracts, and overseas stints that produced two NBA championship rings.

He spent the 2015–16 season with the Canton Charge in the D-League, averaging 19.6 points and 5.4 assists to win D-League Rookie of the Year and the All-Star Game MVP. Then came 10-day contracts: Dallas in February 2017, New Orleans in March 2017. Each time he proved he could play; each time the opportunity evaporated.

In October 2017, Golden State signed Cook to a two-way contract. When Stephen Curry went down with an injury, Cook stepped into the starting lineup and delivered: 9.5 points per game as a starter, 44.2% from three. The Warriors won the 2018 championship. Cook had a ring — earned through the G League, through 10-day contracts, through sheer refusal to quit.

In July 2019, Cook signed with the Los Angeles Lakers. He won his second championship when the Lakers defeated the Miami Heat in the 2020 Finals in the COVID bubble. Two rings. Neither handed to him.

After the Lakers, the journey continued: brief stints with the Cavaliers, Portland training camp, Lokomotiv Kuban in Russia, Stockton Kings in the G League (averaging 23.5 ppg), Zhejiang Lions in China (where he scored a career-high 54 points in 29 minutes), time in Puerto Rico, Al-Ula in Saudi Arabia, and as of early 2025, the Fujian Sturgeons in the CBA. Career NBA totals: 188 games, 6.4 ppg — modest numbers that do not capture the journey.
bio.now:
Quinn Cook is 32 years old and still playing professional basketball overseas, chasing the game his father gave him. In 2024, he announced the opening of 4th Quarter Sports Bar in Bowie, Maryland — a tribute to his parents’ sports bar, 4th and Goal, where he first fell in love with sports. His mother Janet and sister Kelsey help run it while his playing career continues.

He has played on three continents, in seven countries, and won championships at every level: NCAA (2015), NBA (2018, 2020). Duke Basketball Report has noted he has an incredibly bright future in coaching if he wants it.

His story is the Brotherhood’s purest underdog narrative: the kid from D.C. who lost his father at fourteen, who played at DeMatha and Oak Hill and Duke, who went undrafted, who ground through the G League and 10-day contracts and overseas leagues, and who somehow ended up with two NBA rings and a sports bar named after the fourth quarter — because that’s when the game is decided, and Quinn Cook has always been a finisher.

players.name: Grayson Allen
players.tagline: Tripped, fell, and kept getting back up.
players.stat: 142 games • 1,996 pts (12th all-time) • 2015 NCAA champion • NBA champion (Bucks) • Led NBA in 3PT% 2024

bio.road:
Jim Martin, who coached basketball at the Providence School in Jacksonville, Florida, remembers the first time he shook hands with Grayson Allen. The kid was in fifth grade. ‘He had the biggest hands for a kid,’ Martin said. Then Allen told his coach what he wanted: ‘Coach, I want to play at Duke.’ Martin stared at him. A fifth grader. In Jacksonville. ‘Can you imagine the look on my face when he said that? But you know what my answer was? Let’s go to work.’

Between fifth and sixth grade, Allen put in eight to ten hours a day all summer. His mother would call Martin asking where Grayson was. He was in the gym. ‘People wouldn’t even believe,’ Martin said. That relentlessness became the defining feature of Allen’s career — the thing that made him great and the thing that, at his worst, made him dangerous.

At Providence School: 90–10 record, one state championship, two state semifinal appearances. Junior: 19.6 ppg, 35–1 record, Florida 3A state title, 31 points in the championship game. Senior: 22.8 ppg, 6.7 rpg. Graduated cum laude. 2014 McDonald’s All-American. Won the dunk contest jumping over future Duke teammate Jahlil Okafor — the third Duke signee to win it (after Ricky Price, Gerald Henderson). Jordan Brand Classic.

Florida actually out-recruited Duke. Donovan’s staff was relentless. ‘It was just a case that Duke was his dream school,’ Martin said. ‘Had he wavered at all, he would have ended up at Florida.’ He didn’t waver.
bio.duke:
Act I: The Unknown (2014–15). Allen averaged 4.4 ppg off the bench on a team loaded with Okafor, Winslow, and Jones. In the championship game against Wisconsin, Duke trailing by nine, Coach K turned to his freshman. Allen scored 16 on 5-of-8 with a crucial steal. Krzyzewski: ‘We were kind of dead in the water. We were nine points down and Grayson just put us on his back.’ All-Tournament team.

Act II: The Star (2015–16). Sophomore breakout: 21.6 ppg, 41% from three. All-America and Academic All-American in the same season — only the sixth Duke player to do both.

Act III: The Villain (2016–17). February 2016: tripped Louisville’s Ray Spalding. Weeks later: tripped a Florida State player. December 21, 2016: tripped Elon’s Steven Santa Ana. Third time in twelve months. Allen broke down on the bench. Teammate Matt Jones: ‘I love Grayson. He’s my brother. I just gave him a really big hug, told him, I love you. And let the silence fill the void.’ Krzyzewski: ‘I handle things the way I handle them. I don’t need to satisfy what other people think I should do.’ The next morning: indefinite suspension. Then stripped of his captaincy. Every collision scrutinized ‘like the Zapruder film.’

Act IV: The Redemption (2017–18). Came back when he could have left for the NBA. Opened with 37 points against Michigan State. Named senior captain — the same title stripped a year earlier. USA Today: ‘A flipped script.’ Finished with 1,996 career points (12th all-time). Four-time All-ACC Academic.

On J.J. Redick’s podcast: ‘Obviously if I could go back in time, I wouldn’t trip anybody. But it doesn’t bother me. I realize it was part of my journey where I had to mess up. I had to make some mistakes to learn.’
bio.after:
Jazz, 21st overall, 2018. Career-high 40 points in his final Jazz game. Traded to Memphis (2019, Conley deal). Two Grizzlies seasons. Traded to Milwaukee (2021) — won an NBA championship ring. The freshman who won a title at Duke now had one as a pro.

Traded to Phoenix (September 2023). First Suns season: 13.5 ppg on 50/46/88 splits, leading the NBA in three-point percentage at 46.1%. Four-year, $70M extension (April 2024). November 2025: franchise-record 10 threes, career-high 42 points vs New Orleans. February 2025: two threes in the final 4.4 seconds including a buzzer-beater to force OT against the Jazz.
bio.now:
Grayson Allen is 30 years old. Eighth NBA season with the Suns. Married Morgan Reid, a former Duke soccer player. Career earnings will exceed $85 million.

His high school coach’s message: ‘Give him a chance. What has his body of work been for the last three years? I guarantee you every organization loves the fact that he’s such a great competitor.’

Yahoo’s Jeff Eisenberg captured the arc: ‘He evolved from seldom-used benchwarmer to improbable national title game hero, from breakout star to serial tripper, from polarizing villain to understated veteran.’ The fifth grader told his coach he wanted to play at Duke. He trained eight to ten hours a day. He jumped over the No. 1 recruit in the dunk contest. He put a championship team on his back. He became the best scorer in the ACC. Then he tripped three people and became the most hated player in college basketball. He came back when he could have left. He was stripped of his captaincy and earned it back. He won an NBA title. He led the league in three-point shooting. He got a $70 million contract. The Brotherhood doesn’t get to pick which parts of a man’s story it keeps. It keeps all of them.

players.name: Jahlil Okafor
players.tagline: One year. One ring. Then the NBA broke him.
players.now: Levanga Hokkaido (Japan B.League); represented Nigeria 2020 Olympics
players.stat: 38 games • 17.3 PPG • 66.4% FG • ACC POY (1st freshman) • unanimous All-American • 2015 NCAA champion

bio.road:
There are two Jahlil Okafors. The one you see on the court: all power dunks, spin moves, stare-downs. And the one you don’t see. The one who plays chess and the saxophone and the tuba. Who worked stage crew for a musical in junior high. Whose morning alarm, for years after his mother died, was a recording of her voice: ‘Jahlil, Jahlil.’

Born December 15, 1995, in Fort Smith, Arkansas. His father, Chukwudi — known as Chuck — is a second-generation Nigerian American of Igbo descent. His mother, Dacresha Lanett Benton, was a 6-foot-2 basketball force at Carl Albert State College. They met there on basketball scholarships. As a child, Jahlil split time between his mother’s home in Moffett, Oklahoma — population 127 — and his father’s home in Chicago.

When Jahlil was nine, he was watching BET with his sister Jalen when their mother started breathing hard. They thought she was joking. Jahlil teased her — said he was going to steal her Oreos. Then he realized it wasn’t a joke. He called 911. She had contracted bronchitis and suffered a collapsed lung. She died.

Jahlil called his father. Chuck heard it in his son’s voice — somewhere between a cry and a scream. The only words: ‘Mama. Mama.’ At the gravesite, the wind lifted, and Chuck spread out his arms: ‘Every time you feel the wind, that is your mom with you.’

Chuck’s own mother died of breast cancer when he was eighteen months old. He’d been a ‘roughneck’ — stolen car at thirteen, drugs, five high schools, a group home. But when Dacresha died: ‘Without Jah, I’d probably be dead or in jail.’

At Whitney Young Magnet High School — Michelle Obama’s alma mater — Okafor became the most dominant high school center in the country. National POY (McDonald’s, USA Today, Parade). Illinois Mr. Basketball. Three USA Basketball golds. He committed to Duke in a package with AAU friend Tyus Jones. ‘My deepest fear is losing someone else close to me. That’s something I think about way more than I should.’
bio.duke: One season. 38 games. 17.3 ppg, 8.5 rpg, 66.4% FG — second in the nation. Shot 70% or better twenty times. First freshman in Duke history to win ACC Player of the Year. Unanimous First-Team All-American. In the 2015 championship game against Wisconsin, Okafor scored 10 key points as Duke won 68–63 alongside Tyus Jones (tournament MOP), Justise Winslow, Quinn Cook, and a freshman named Grayson Allen who scored 16 off the bench.
bio.after:
Philadelphia, 3rd overall, 2015. Debuted with 26 points. Averaged 17.5 as a rookie on a 10–72 team — the architecture of The Process. All-Rookie First Team.

Then the implosion. A gun pulled on him at a Philadelphia club. A Boston street fight on TMZ. 108 mph on a bridge. Suspended two games. He was nineteen. Brett Brown: ‘I’m looking at a 19-year-old kid. I do not feel betrayed.’ Joel Embiid arrived and Okafor became expendable. Traded to Brooklyn for Trevor Booker. Brown: ‘Human disappointment.’ Embiid texted: ‘Love you. New beginnings.’

Reinvented his body in New Orleans — semi-vegan diet, 8.2 ppg in 59 Pelicans games. Detroit 2020–21. February 2025: Indiana 10-day contract, one game. July 2025: signed with Levanga Hokkaido in Japan. Represented Nigeria at the 2020 Tokyo Olympics.
bio.now:
Jahlil Okafor is 30 years old, playing basketball in Hokkaido, Japan — 6,200 miles from Cameron Indoor. His Twitter profile still reads: ‘R.I.P. I love you mom.’ A picture of her headstone is the backdrop on his phone.

He lost his mother at nine and called 911 while she was dying. His father told him the wind was her. He set his alarm to her voice. He became the No. 1 recruit, won a championship, was drafted third. Then street fights and 108 mph and traded for Trevor Booker. He reinvented himself in New Orleans and played for Nigeria and signed a 10-day contract nine years after being a top-three pick. The kid who played chess and saxophone and tuba is still playing basketball at thirty, on a different continent, because the game saved him when his mother died and nearly destroyed him when the money came. Every time he feels the wind, that is his mom with him.

players.name: Tyus Jones
players.tagline: Hit the biggest shot of 2015.
players.now: NBA point guard (Phoenix Suns); 6x NBA assist-to-turnover ratio leader; Write Your Own Story Foundation
players.stat: 1 Duke season • 11.8 PPG/5.6 APG • 2015 NCAA Champion • Final Four MOP • NBA record A/TO ratio (7.35)

bio.road:
The name came from a basketball game his mother was watching on television. In 1995, Debbie Jones watched UCLA’s Tyus Edney race the length of the court and hit a buzzer-beater against Missouri. A year later, she named her second son Tyus. She was a point guard herself — she’d led Devils Lake High School to the North Dakota state championship in 1981. She knew what she was watching. She knew what she wanted her boy to become.

The basketball bloodline runs deep. Father Rob Jones, 6-6, played at Division III Wisconsin-Parkside. Aunt Darcy Cascaes won two state titles at Devils Lake. Uncle Al Nuness captained the Minnesota Gophers in the 1960s. Older half-brother Jadee played at Furman and Mankato State. Younger brother Tre would follow Tyus to Duke and now plays for the Chicago Bulls. ‘It’s kind of in the blood, I guess you could say.’ Grandma eventually got tired of hearing about nothing but basketball at family gatherings.

Rob and Debbie divorced. Debbie raised the boys as a single mother in Apple Valley, Minnesota — a Twin Cities suburb of 50,000 with no basketball tradition. She worked as a paralegal. She used her vacation days to travel with Tyus and Tre to tournaments across the country. ‘All the time we’ve spent, all the money, all the days off work, all the early mornings … it’s just so worth it.’

In eighth grade — not yet enrolled at Apple Valley High — Tyus was named the varsity starting point guard. ‘He was the best point guard at our school, and he wasn’t even at our school.’ Over five years: first-ever state championship, Minnesota Mr. Basketball, 3x AP Player of the Year, 3x Gatorade POY, McDonald’s All-American. Before Tyus, if you typed ‘Apple Valley’ into Google, it showed California. After Tyus, it showed Minnesota.

He’d been friends with Jahlil Okafor since age eight through AAU. They committed to Duke together as a package deal. Something his family told him again and again became his mantra: ‘Tyus, you have to write your own story.’
bio.duke:
The 2014–15 roster was stacked with freshmen: Okafor, Jones, Justise Winslow, Grayson Allen. Senior Quinn Cook ran the offense. Jones averaged 11.8 PPG, 5.6 APG. All-ACC Third Team. All-ACC Freshman First Team (first trio ever unanimously selected, with Okafor and Winslow). AP honorable mention All-American.

Jeff Capel: ‘The one thing the best point guards have in common is that they win. Whatever it takes to win, Tyus does.’

The championship game against Wisconsin proved it. Frank Kaminsky, Sam Dekker, an experienced Badgers team. Jones scored 23 points with 5 rebounds. He hit a deep three in the final minutes that gave Duke a lead it never relinquished. Duke 68–63. Final Four Most Outstanding Player. Nineteen years old. True freshman. The photo of Jones cutting down the net at Lucas Oil Stadium graced the cover of Sports Illustrated.

April 22, 2015: the mayor of Apple Valley proclaimed “Tyus Jones Day.” Key to the city. First pitch at a Twins game. Number retired at Apple Valley High after a parade through town. President Obama referenced his nickname, “Tyus Stones.” He declared for the draft.
bio.after:
Drafted 24th by Cleveland, immediately traded to his hometown Timberwolves. Four seasons as a backup (2015–19) behind Ricky Rubio and Jeff Teague — reliable, steady, always efficient.

Memphis (2019–23) is where the quiet excellence became historic. Jones set the NBA single-season record for assist-to-turnover ratio: 6.96 in 2020–21. Broke his own record: 7.04 in 2021–22. Broke it again: 7.35 in 2022–23. Six consecutive seasons leading the NBA. No player in league history has ever been as precise with the basketball. His mother was a point guard who taught him the position is about making others better and never giving the ball away. He internalized it at a cellular level.

With Ja Morant suspended, Jones stepped into the starting lineup and proved he could run a team at the highest level — just as he had as an eighth-grader, just as he had in the championship game. Traded to Washington (2023–24). Signed with Phoenix (2024). Career: ~8.5 PPG, ~4.5 APG, historically unprecedented A/TO ratio.
bio.now:
In 2019, Debbie was diagnosed with breast cancer. For the first time in over two decades, basketball took a backseat. ‘Without my mom, I wouldn’t be where I am today. This was a wakeup call. She came first. Family was the only thing I focused on.’

In Minnesota, his former teammate Karl-Anthony Towns had custom sneakers created for the entire Timberwolves squad to wear in a game honoring breast cancer awareness. Towns’s own mother had battled COVID-19. The gesture moved the Jones family deeply.

Debbie underwent two rounds of chemotherapy and five weeks of radiation. ‘She’s Superwoman. She’s the toughest, strongest person I know.’ After nine months: cancer-free. The single mother who used her vacation days to drive to AAU tournaments had beaten the disease that tried to take her from the family she’d built.

Tyus is 29. Lives in Minnesota. Dog named Duke Stone Jones. Runs “Write Your Own Story” — refurbishing technology labs for kids, hosting camps, giving speeches. Brother Tre plays for the Bulls. Brother Jadee trains players. The family that got tired of hearing nothing but basketball at holiday dinners produced three college players and two NBA pros from a North Dakota point guard who named her son after a buzzer-beater on television.

He was named after Tyus Edney because his mother saw a point guard do something extraordinary. He started varsity as an eighth-grader. Led Apple Valley to its first state title and got a day named after him. Committed to Duke with a friend he’d known since age eight. Hit the biggest shot of the 2015 tournament. Set the NBA A/TO record six times. And when his mother was diagnosed with cancer, he put the game aside — because she had always come first. The point guard who taught him how to be a point guard. The toughest person he’s ever known. ‘Tyus, you have to write your own story.’ He did. The best chapter is the one where his mom is still here to read it.

players.name: Justise Winslow
players.tagline: The glue of the 2015 title team.
players.now: Free agent; Robin’s House Family Foundation; pursuing media/entertainment career
players.stat: 1 Duke season • 12.6 PPG/6.5 RPG • NCAA Tourney: 14.3 PPG/9.3 RPG • 2015 NCAA Champion • 8 NBA seasons

bio.road:
Justise Jon Winslow was born March 26, 1996, in Houston, Texas, the youngest of five children. His father, Rickie Winslow, played basketball at Houston from 1983 to 1987 as part of the legendary Phi Slama Jama teams alongside Hakeem Olajuwon and Clyde Drexler. Rickie was drafted 28th by the Bulls in 1987 and played professionally in Europe until 2000. Sister Bianca played basketball at Houston. Brother Josh played football at Dartmouth.

But Justise didn’t cling to basketball because of his father. Rickie and his mother Robin Davis divorced when Justise was in grade school. Robin got full custody and raised all five children with an African proverb as her philosophy: It takes a village to raise a child.

Beverly Mosby was part of that village. Miss Bev was the unofficial team mom for the Houston Jaguars, Justise’s third-grade traveling team. She drove kids to practice, gave money to players who couldn’t afford food on road trips, and told Justise there were no limits. She died in 2012. When Winslow announced his Duke commitment, he choked back tears: ‘She really just found this dream inside of me. I dedicate my life in basketball to her.’

Steve and Leticia Trauber became his godparents in sixth grade. ‘If anything ever happened to me, they would be in control.’ They designated a room in their home for Justise. Steve once drove him to the movies and laughed from the back row when the gangly teenager got rejected trying to put his arm around his date.

John Lucas — former Rockets player, longtime NBA coach, Houston basketball godfather — became another mentor. At St. John’s School: four-year starter. Senior: 27.5 PPG/13.6 RPG/3.5 APG/2.1 BPG. Gatorade Texas POY. McDonald’s All-American. Jersey #42 retired.

The college decision: Robin pulled Justise from class the day before the signing period. They sat at a picnic table for three hours. On his first Durham visit at fifteen, they stopped for potato chips and onion dip at the Washington Duke Inn at 11 p.m. ‘If you want to commit now, you can,’ Robin said. Justise smiled: ‘Mom, we’ve only been here for three hours. The visit hasn’t even started yet.’ He took two more years. Then chose Duke.
bio.duke:
Winslow arrived as the “forgotten freshman.” Okafor was the #1 recruit. Tyus Jones was the #1 point guard. They committed as a package deal. Winslow committed a week later, almost an afterthought. But Coach K compared his game to Tommy Amaker and Grant Hill.

Jeff Capel saw something deeper: ‘He sees the world in a very mature way. He has an old soul. It’s almost like he’s been here before.’ At seventeen, playing for USA Basketball in Prague, Winslow spent his downtime visiting museums and photographing the city. He became interested in art through documentaries.

12.6 PPG, 6.5 RPG, 2.1 APG, 1.3 SPG. The do-everything wing who could guard four positions. Scored in double digits in Duke’s final five tournament games. Against Utah in the Sweet Sixteen: 21 points, 10 rebounds. Played through rib and shoulder injuries. In the championship game vs Wisconsin: 11 points, but his defense and rebounding let Tyus Jones and Okafor do their damage. Duke won 68–63.

During a late-game moment, Jon Scheyer’s mother tapped Robin Davis on the shoulder in the stands: ‘Look. Justise is smiling.’ The afterthought had become the championship’s most indispensable player.
bio.after:
Miami Heat, 10th overall, 2015. Five seasons in Miami. Raw rookie (6.4 PPG, All-Rookie Second Team). Career-high 28 points vs Lakers (December 2018). Reimagined as a 6-6 point guard when Dragić went down — 24 points, 11 rebounds, 7 assists in his best game.

But injuries devastated his trajectory. Torn labrum (2017). Knee strains. Back problems. Only 11 games in 2019–20. Traded to Memphis. 26 games over two seasons. Clippers. Portland — 11 games before another season-ending injury. Eight seasons, six teams, ~$45M career earnings. The body that was so durable in college kept breaking.

After Hurricane Harvey destroyed Houston in 2017, Winslow donated $100,000 through Robin’s House Foundation. $50,000 to Small Steps Nurturing Center for at-risk children.
