Organized a charity event at Cameron Indoor with Duke. Personally donated cars to two families who lost vehicles. Lanesha Dabney, a 24-year-old single mother of two, received a Chevy Equinox: ‘It feels so good to know I have a safe car to drive to work.’ Winslow: ‘These families were hurt hard. This is just a small token of my appreciation for the city and what it’s done for me.’
bio.now:
Justise Winslow is 29. He last played for the Wisconsin Herd (G League) in 2024, still chasing a return. Has a son, Niko Tosoani Winslow (born 2021, with partner Kristina Escobar). Pursuing media and entertainment — drawn to storytelling and sports documentaries, the same impulse that sent him into Prague’s museums at seventeen. Favorite athletes growing up: Jackie Robinson and Muhammad Ali.

The Robin’s House Foundation continues — free annual basketball camps, the D.R.E.A.M. Showcase talent show for under-resourced children in Miami. Named after his mother.

He was the youngest of five from a broken home in Houston. His father played on Phi Slama Jama but wasn’t around much. His mother raised him with a village — Miss Bev, who paid for his meals and died in 2012; the godparents who designated a room in their home; John Lucas, who opened the door; Coach K, who compared him to Grant Hill. He sat at a picnic table with his mother for three hours and chose Duke. He was the forgotten freshman who became the championship’s most versatile player — the one Scheyer’s mother noticed was smiling during the biggest game. Drafted 10th. Injuries stole his prime. Donated $100,000 to Hurricane Harvey relief because Houston raised him. Visited museums in Prague at seventeen because he has an old soul. Named his foundation after his mother.

It takes a village. Robin Davis built one. And Justise Winslow has spent his whole life trying to build it for everyone else.

players.name: Jabari Parker
players.tagline: #2 pick. Two torn ACLs. A what-if story.

players.name: Rodney Hood
players.tagline: Transferred in from Mississippi State.

players.name: Kyle Singler
players.tagline: The four-year warrior of the 2010 title.

players.name: Marshall Plumlee
players.tagline: He served his country after serving Duke.

players.name: Brandon Ingram
players.tagline: #2 pick. All-Star. The quiet superstar.
players.stat: 17.3 ppg at Duke • ACC ROY • MIP 2020 • 2x All-Star • ~$182M career earnings

bio.road:
There are two Jahlil Okafors. There is the one you see on the court: all power dunks, spin moves, stare-downs. And there is the one you don’t see. The one who plays chess and the saxophone and the tuba. Who worked stage crew for a musical in junior high. Whose morning alarm, for years after his mother died, was a recording of her voice: ‘Jahlil, Jahlil.’

He was born December 15, 1995, in Fort Smith, Arkansas. His father, Chukwudi — known as Chuck — is a second-generation Nigerian American of Igbo descent. His mother, Dacresha Lanett Benton, was a 6-foot-2 basketball force at Carl Albert State College before a knee injury ended her career. They met there on basketball scholarships. As a young child, Jahlil split time between his mother’s home in Moffett, Oklahoma — population 127 — and his father’s home in Chicago.

When Jahlil was nine, he was watching music videos on BET with his sister Jalen when their mother started breathing hard. They thought she was joking. Jahlil teased her — said he was going to steal her Oreos. Then he realized it wasn’t a joke. He called 911. She had contracted bronchitis two weeks earlier and suffered a collapsed lung. She died.

Jahlil called his father. Chuck heard it in his son’s voice — somewhere between a cry and a scream. The only words: ‘Mama. Mama.’ Chuck flew to Fort Smith. At the funeral, Jahlil read a poem he’d written. At the gravesite, the wind lifted, and Chuck spread out his arms: ‘Every time you feel the wind, that is your mom with you.’

Chuck’s own story was a mirror of grief. His mother died of breast cancer when he was eighteen months old. His father, a Nigerian immigrant, raised six children alone. Chuck had been a ‘roughneck’ — stolen car at thirteen, drugs, five high schools in three years, a group home. But when Dacresha died: ‘Without Jah, I’d probably be dead or in jail.’

At Whitney Young Magnet High School — Michelle Obama’s alma mater — Okafor became the most dominant high school center in the country. National Player of the Year (McDonald’s, USA Today, Parade). Illinois Mr. Basketball. Three USA Basketball golds (U16, U17, U19). DePaul offered him a scholarship before he played a high school game. He committed to Duke in a package deal with his AAU friend Tyus Jones. ‘My deepest fear is losing someone else close to me,’ he told Chicago magazine. ‘That’s something I think about way more than I should.’
bio.duke:
One season. 38 games. 17.3 ppg, 8.5 rpg, 66.4% FG — second in the nation. Shot 70% or better twenty times. First freshman in Duke history to win ACC Player of the Year. USBWA National Freshman of the Year. Unanimous First-Team All-American.

In the 2015 championship game against Wisconsin, Okafor scored 10 key points as Duke won 68–63. He played alongside Tyus Jones (tournament MOP), Justise Winslow, Quinn Cook, and a freshman bench player named Grayson Allen who scored 16 off the bench. Duke went 35–4.
bio.after:
Philadelphia, 3rd overall, 2015. Debuted with 26 points against the Celtics. Averaged 17.5 as a rookie — leading the Sixers and all first-year players. All-Rookie First Team. But the team was 10–72 — the architecture of The Process.

Then the implosion. A heckler pulled a gun on him at a Philadelphia club. A Boston street fight filmed by TMZ. A second fight the same night. 108 mph on a Philadelphia bridge. A fake ID. Suspended two games. A personal security guard assigned. He was nineteen. Brett Brown: ‘I’m looking at a 19-year-old kid that all of a sudden has come into money and has been used to winning. I do not feel betrayed.’

Joel Embiid arrived and Okafor became expendable. Traded to Brooklyn for Trevor Booker. Brown: ‘I wouldn’t say there’s regrets. I’d say there’s human disappointment.’ Embiid texted him: ‘Love you. New beginnings.’

Okafor reinvented his body in New Orleans — semi-vegan diet, resculpted his frame, averaged 8.2 points in 59 Pelicans games. Detroit in 2020–21. Then unsigned. In February 2025, Indiana signed him to a 10-day contract; he appeared in one game. In July 2025, he signed with Levanga Hokkaido in Japan’s B.League. He represented Nigeria at the 2020 Tokyo Olympics, honoring his grandfather’s homeland.
bio.now:
Jahlil Okafor is 30 years old, playing basketball in Hokkaido, Japan — 6,200 miles from Cameron Indoor and 6,800 from the South Side of Chicago where his father reinvented himself. His Twitter profile still reads: ‘R.I.P. I love you mom.’ A picture of her headstone is the backdrop on his phone.

His Brotherhood story is the most heartbreaking in the Superteam Era. He lost his mother at nine and called 911 while she was dying on the couch. His father told him the wind was her. He set his alarm to a recording of her voice. He became the No. 1 recruit, won a national championship, and was drafted third overall. Then he got into street fights and drove 108 mph and was traded for Trevor Booker. He reinvented his body with the Pelicans and played for Nigeria in the Olympics and signed a 10-day contract nine years after being a top-three pick.

The kid who played chess and saxophone and tuba, whose deepest fear was losing someone else close to him — he is still playing basketball at thirty, on a different continent, because the game is the thing that saved him when his mother died and the thing that nearly destroyed him when the money came. Every time he feels the wind, that is his mom with him.

players.name: Jayson Tatum
players.tagline: One year at Duke. NBA champion at 26.
players.now: Boston Celtics — rehabbing Achilles; Duke Chief Basketball Officer
players.stat: 16.8 ppg at Duke • 6x All-Star • 5x All-NBA • 2024 NBA champ • 2x Olympic gold

bio.road:
Jayson Tatum’s first basketball court was a bathtub. His mother, Brandy Cole, hung a suction-cup hoop on the wall, and her son would shoot for hours. He was born March 3, 1998, in St. Louis, Missouri. Brandy was eighteen, a high school graduate who’d been offered a volleyball scholarship to Tennessee. She passed on it. She enrolled at the University of Missouri–St. Louis, worked gift-wrapping at Cingular Wireless, and raised Jayson alone. Four hours was a quality night’s sleep. She moved out of her mother’s house when Jayson was six months old. She brought her toddler to class. She missed his first crawl because she was at work. ‘I just broke down and cried,’ she said.

When Jayson was eleven, the bank threatened to foreclose. He understood what was happening. Cole saved the house at the last minute. ‘I’m not going to become a statistic.’ She eventually earned degrees in communications and political science, a law degree from Saint Louis University, and an MBA. Every lesson Jayson learned about work ethic, he learned watching her.

His father, Justin Tatum, played at Saint Louis University and in the Netherlands. He came back into Jayson’s life around age seven or eight. Justin’s best friend, NBA player Larry Hughes, is Jayson’s godfather. Through Hughes, eight-year-old Jayson met LeBron James and rebounded for him at practice. He’s also a cousin of NBA head coach Tyronn Lue.

The moment Justin knew: Jayson was eleven, playing in a men’s league. Justin’s team was short a player. He told his son, ‘Just shoot the ball.’ Jayson scored 22 points against grown men. But he also craved his father’s approval in ways that haunted him: ‘I could score 40 and he would never clap. He would just sit there. He would leave. I would go to the gym thinking if I score 50, then maybe that would be good enough.’

At Chaminade Prep, his coach Frank Bennett marveled: ‘I get to school about 6:30 every day, and he was here at 5:45. The only day he took off was the day after we won state. He just worked and worked and worked.’ 250 shots every morning. His mother prepared him for fame by pausing his NBA2K, shoving a hairbrush in his face, and grilling him with postgame questions like Craig Sager.

Senior year: 29.6 points, 9.1 rebounds, six 40-point games. State champion (40 in the final). Beat Malik Monk, Miles Bridges, and Markelle Fultz — three future lottery picks in three showcase games. McDonald’s All-American. Gatorade National Player of the Year. In gym class at Chaminade, he became friends with Matthew Tkachuk. Both would win championships in the same week in 2024.
bio.duke:
Tatum missed the first eight games with a foot injury. He debuted in December 2016 and immediately became Duke’s best player. In the ACC Tournament: 22.0 ppg across four games in four days as Duke won the title. Season: 16.8 ppg, 7.3 rpg in 29 games. In the NCAA Tournament, South Carolina pulled the upset in the second round. One season. One ACC title. Then gone.

Danny Ainge traded the No. 1 overall pick to Philadelphia for No. 3 and a future first. Philly took Markelle Fultz — the same kid Tatum had beaten by 40 at the HoopHall Classic. Ainge took Tatum. Fultz’s shot vanished. Tatum became a superstar. Widely regarded as the steal of the decade.
bio.after:
351 playoff points as a rookie — most in Celtics first-year history. 60-point game in 2021 (tying Larry Bird, 21 in the fourth quarter to erase a 32-point deficit). Six-time All-Star. Five-time All-NBA. Two Olympic golds (2020 Tokyo, 2024 Paris). In the 2024 NBA Finals: Tatum and Jaylen Brown led Boston to its 18th championship, beating Dallas in five games after a 64-win season. Supermax extension July 2024. Career earnings approaching $400 million. Jordan Brand signature shoe (Jordan Tatum 1).

Then May 12, 2025: Achilles rupture, Game 4 vs the Knicks at Madison Square Garden. Surgery. No timeline. Four months later, he posted video of himself shooting again: ‘It’s been a long journey but I’m working my tail off to get back.’ In October 2025, Duke named him Chief Basketball Officer — a first-of-its-kind volunteer role mentoring current players on development, professionalism, career management, and personal branding. Scheyer: ‘Jayson is the ultimate professional.’ Tatum: ‘This program means so much to me.’

By February 2026, full five-on-five scrimmages. Shams Charania reported he was cleared physically but the decision was his. He told ESPN he ‘wants to get it right the first time.’ Bill Simmons reported in early March that all signs pointed to a return against Dallas on March 7 — a rematch of the 2024 Finals.
bio.now:
Jayson Tatum is 27 years old. He runs the Jayson Tatum Foundation, helping low-income St. Louis families build generational wealth (SoFi donated $1M to the foundation in 2024). Son Jayson Jr. (‘Deuce’), born 2017, is a courtside celebrity. In 2024, Tatum and singer Ella Mai had their first child together.

His mother was eighteen when she had him. She missed his first crawl. She saved their house from foreclosure. She became an attorney. His father never clapped when he scored 40. His high school coach watched him arrive at 5:45 every morning — 250 shots before class, only day off the day after winning state. His mother practiced media interviews with a hairbrush.

He went third instead of first, and the team that passed on him got Markelle Fultz. He scored 60 in a game and won an NBA championship and two Olympic golds. Then his Achilles ruptured at Madison Square Garden and he spent the fall back at Duke mentoring the next generation while his tendon healed. The kid who shot hoops in the bathtub — ten months from a ruptured Achilles and scrimmaging five-on-five. The Brotherhood’s most dramatic story isn’t finished yet. It might not even be at halftime.

players.name: Zion Williamson
players.tagline: The shoe exploded. The legend was born.
players.now: New Orleans Pelicans; $197M contract; career-high 35-game streak 2025–26
players.stat: 22.6 ppg/68% FG at Duke • Unanimous POY • 2x NBA All-Star • 259 games/296 missed

bio.road:
Zion Lateef Williamson was born July 6, 2000, in Salisbury, North Carolina. His mother, Sharonda Sampson, ran track at Livingstone College. His stepfather, Lee Anderson, played basketball at Clemson. Starting at age nine, Williamson woke at 5 AM daily for basketball training. He was built differently — a combination of size, speed, and explosive leaping ability that bordered on the supernatural.

He attended Spartanburg Day School in South Carolina, leading the team to three consecutive state championships and earning South Carolina Mr. Basketball. He became a viral sensation before college — his dunk highlights accumulated millions of YouTube views, making him the most famous high school player since LeBron James. Consensus five-star, top-5 nationally, McDonald’s All-American, runner-up Mr. Basketball USA. He chose Duke.
bio.duke:
Williamson’s single season at Duke was one of the most electrifying in college basketball history. In 33 games: 22.6 points, 8.9 rebounds, 2.1 assists, 1.8 steals, 1.8 blocks on 68.0% shooting — staggering efficiency at that volume. He was unanimous consensus National Player of the Year (AP, Naismith, Wooden, and every other major award).

The defining moment: February 20, 2019, Duke vs. North Carolina. Thirty-three seconds in, Williamson planted his foot and his Nike PG 2.5 shoe literally exploded — the sole ripping away from the upper. Grade 1 knee sprain. Nike’s stock dropped $1.1 billion the next day. The shoe heard round the world. Williamson returned three weeks later for the ACC Tournament and led Duke to the title.

He played alongside RJ Barrett and Cam Reddish — all three selected in the top 10 of the 2019 Draft. Duke was eliminated in the Elite Eight by Michigan State.
bio.after:
The Pelicans selected Williamson first overall on June 20, 2019. Torn meniscus in preseason delayed his debut until January 22, 2020, when he scored 17 consecutive points in 3:08 vs. the Spurs. He became the first teenager in NBA history to score 20+ in 10 consecutive games. Highest scoring average through 24 career games since Michael Jordan.

In 2021, at age 20, he became the fourth-youngest NBA All-Star: 27.0 points, 7.2 rebounds, 61.1% shooting. Then the injuries: missed all of 2021–22 (broken foot), 29 games in 2022–23 (hamstring), career-high 70 games in 2023–24 (but hamstring strain in play-in game), just 30 games in 2024–25 (hamstring, suspension for late team flight, back contusion).

Through seven NBA seasons: 259 games played, 296 missed — unavailable more often than available. He signed a five-year, $197.2 million max extension in 2022, but the deal includes non-guaranteed years tied to weight checkpoints and games-played thresholds.
bio.now:
The 2025–26 season has offered the most sustained hope. Williamson set a career record by playing 35 consecutive games before spraining his ankle on March 1, 2026. Through 45 games this season (third-highest total), he is averaging approximately 21.5 points and 5.8 rebounds. He has publicly committed to New Orleans: ‘If you can win a championship in New Orleans, you’re forever. And who doesn’t want to be forever?’

The Pelicans sit at 17–42. Williamson, now 25, remains under contract through 2027–28 (if he meets his guarantees). He is still, when healthy, one of the most physically dominant players in basketball — a 6-foot-6, 284-pound force shooting over 57% from the field for his career.

Zion’s Brotherhood story is the most complicated of the modern era. Most hyped since LeBron, the shoe that exploded on national TV, No. 1 pick whose body has betrayed his talent more games than it has served it. Two-time All-Star and a player who has missed more games than he’s played. A $197 million investment with a clause that says the money disappears if he can’t stay on the court. The story is unresolved — and that is the most honest thing anyone can say in March 2026.

players.name: RJ Barrett
players.tagline: #3 pick. Canadian. Still proving himself.
players.now: Toronto Raptors; 2023 FIBA World Cup Bronze (Canada’s first in 87 years); $120M contract
players.stat: 1 Duke season • 22.6 PPG/7.6 RPG • ACC Freshman of Year • #1 recruit (1st since LeBron to sweep all HS awards)

bio.road:
Rowan Alexander Barrett Jr. was born June 14, 2000, in Toronto, Ontario. His father, Rowan Sr., was born in Scarborough to Jamaican parents. The elder Rowan hadn’t even played basketball as a child — he ran track, racing friends at recess every day. Basketball came later. He played at St. John’s, turned pro in Europe and South America, and captained the Canadian national team at the 2000 Sydney Olympics. His closest friend on that team was Steve Nash.

When RJ was born, Nash bought him his first crib. He became the baby’s godfather. Two-time NBA MVP. Hall of Famer. ‘Dad, what’s Uncle Steve gonna do against Kobe?’ RJ once asked his father while watching Nash on television.

RJ’s mother, Kesha Duhaney, was a nationally ranked sprinter and long jumper at St. John’s — where she met Rowan. Brooklyn-born, Jamaican descent. Her aunt Dahlia won a 4×100m gold at the 1991 World Championships for Jamaica. ‘My mom is crazy competitive. She always wants to beat my dad. But my dad never wants us to beat him. Ever.’

The family lived in France for five years — Dijon, Chalon, Lyon — where RJ played youth basketball in the JDA Dijon Basket club, wearing the same uniform and number as his father. He attended French schools. His mother taught him English at home. He grew up bilingual.

The family returned to Mississauga in 2008. At twelve, after struggling in a game and coming home crying, RJ decided to focus entirely on basketball. At fifteen, he left Canada alone for Montverde Academy in Florida. ‘The first year was definitely the worst. I was not used to being away from my parents.’ He saw them every couple months for three years.

At Montverde: #1 recruit in America. Undefeated season. National championship. Naismith Prep POY. Gatorade National POY. McDonald’s All-American (26 pts). First since LeBron to sweep all major HS awards and win a national title. Summer 2017: led Canada to FIBA U19 World Cup gold, scoring 38/13/5 vs Team USA to end America’s six-year winning streak. Tournament MVP at seventeen.
bio.duke:
The 2018–19 freshman class was the most hyped in college basketball history: Barrett, Zion Williamson, Cam Reddish, Tre Jones. All top-fifteen recruits. Barrett and Williamson were roommates, friends, the #1 and #2 players in America.

Barrett averaged 22.6 PPG, 7.6 RPG — 860 points, the most by a Duke freshman in history. All-ACC First Team. ACC Freshman of the Year. 33 points against North Carolina. Back-to-back 30-point games. Relentless, physical, a 6-6 wing who attacked the basket with force.

But the season will always be remembered through Zion. Williamson was a phenomenon — the shoe blowout against UNC, dunks that crashed the internet. Barrett was the #1 recruit who became the #2 story on his own team. He handled it with the quiet professionalism of a kid raised in professional locker rooms across three continents. Duke went 32–6, reached the Elite Eight, fell to Michigan State.
bio.after:
New York selected Barrett 3rd overall in 2019, behind Zion (#1) and Ja Morant (#2). Madison Square Garden was supposed to be his stage. Rookie year: 14.3 PPG, youngest player in Knicks history to reach 1,000 points. Improved every year: 17.6 PPG (2020–21), 20.0 PPG (2021–22). Signed a 4-year, $120M extension in 2022.

In January 2024, traded to the Toronto Raptors for OG Anunoby. He was going home. The kid from Mississauga who left Canada at fifteen was now playing professional basketball in the city where his father captained the national team.

But the international stage delivered his most meaningful basketball. Summer 2023: wearing the maple leaf, Barrett helped Canada win bronze at the FIBA World Cup — Canada’s first medal at a major global tournament since the 1936 Olympics. In the bronze medal game against the United States, Barrett scored 23 points, outplaying his Knicks teammates Brunson and Hart on the opposing side. He combined with Shai Gilgeous-Alexander and Dillon Brooks for 93 points. His father was in the stands — the man who captained Canada at the 2000 Olympics and spent two decades building Canadian basketball. Represented Canada again at the 2024 Paris Olympics.
bio.now:
RJ Barrett is 25. He plays for the Toronto Raptors — in the city where his father grew up, where Steve Nash changed the perception of Canadian basketball, where Vince Carter’s dunks in 2000 lit a fire still burning. Barrett is the inheritor of all of it: son of the man who runs Canada Basketball, godson of the man who proved Canadians could be MVPs.

His brother Nathan also attended Montverde. He speaks fluent French. He played piano as a child. He lived in Dijon wearing his father’s number before he was old enough for school. He came home crying at twelve and decided to dedicate his life to basketball. Left his country at fifteen. Became the #1 recruit. Beat Team USA at seventeen. Averaged 22.6 at Duke alongside the most hyped player in history. Drafted third. Signed $120 million. Got traded home. Helped his country win its first basketball medal in 87 years.

Steve Nash bought him his first crib. His mother taught him English in a French apartment. His aunt won gold in the relay for Jamaica. His father captained Canada at the Olympics. And now RJ Barrett plays for the Raptors, carrying the inheritance of a basketball family that stretches from Jamaica to Scarborough to St. John’s to Sydney to Dijon to Mississauga to Durham to Madison Square Garden and finally, home.

players.name: Tre Jones
players.tagline: Tyus’s brother. Stayed two years.
players.now: Chicago Bulls; 3-year/$24M contract; Susan G. Komen Foundation advocate
players.stat: 2 Duke seasons • 65 starts • 2020 ACC POY + Defensive POY (first to win both) • Duke career A/TO record (2.88)

bio.road:
Tre Isiah Jones was born January 8, 2000, in Apple Valley, Minnesota — four years after Tyus, into the same basketball family, the same single-mother household. Debbie Jones had won a North Dakota state championship as a point guard. Rob Jones played at Division III Wisconsin-Parkside. Older brother Jadee played at Furman. Uncle Al Nuness captained the Gophers in 1969. Debbie and Rob divorced when Tre was five. Debbie, a paralegal, raised the boys on her own.

In 2010, Uncle Al took both boys to the Final Four in Indianapolis. While visiting Durham, Al told Coach K to remember the name Tyus Jones. Then he mentioned Tre: ‘Tre Jones is a Duke fanatic.’ Al got Krzyzewski to sign memorabilia and gave ten-year-old Tre a Blue Devils watch from Jostens. Tre created a Duke shrine in his bedroom — posters, signed memorabilia, the watch. Years later, when Coach K visited the Jones home on a recruiting trip and saw Tre’s room, the decision was already made. ‘It was pretty obvious where Tre had his mind set. He always had an infatuation with Duke and Coach K.’

But Tre had to earn it on his own. In Apple Valley, Tyus was a legend — a day named after him, a parade through the streets. Every time Tre played: ‘How does your game compare to your brother’s?’ He answered by being different. Tyus was the scorer. Tre was the defender.

Varsity as an eighth-grader. Two state championships (2015, 2017). In the 2017 final: 24 pts/18 reb/5 ast. Senior: 23.5 PPG/10.4 RPG/7.5 APG. Two-time Gatorade POY. Minnesota Mr. Basketball. McDonald’s All-American. #1 PG in the 2018 class. USA Basketball U-16 gold (American record: 19 steals). The shrine told you where he was going.
bio.duke:
Tre arrived in 2018 alongside Zion Williamson, RJ Barrett, and Cam Reddish. The most hyped freshman class in history. The spotlight burned on Zion and RJ. Tre was the one who made it work.

Freshman: started all 36 games. 9.4 PPG, 5.3 APG, 1.9 SPG. Set the Duke single-season record for A/TO ratio (3.62) — also an ACC freshman record. Duke went 32–6, reached the Elite Eight. Coach K: ‘They are big-time moment players.’ Tre was the steadier, the one who held superstars together while everyone else got the highlights.

Then the decision that separated him from his brother’s path. Tyus left after one year. Tre came back. He returned for his sophomore season determined to lead his own team — not Zion’s team, not RJ’s team, but Tre’s team.

Sophomore: 16.2 PPG, 6.4 APG, 4.2 RPG, 1.8 SPG. Career-high 31 vs Georgia State. On February 8, 2020, at Chapel Hill: hit a buzzer-beating jumper to tie the game against North Carolina, sending it to overtime. Duke won 98–96. Named ACC Player of the Year AND ACC Defensive Player of the Year — the first player to win both. Duke career record holder in A/TO ratio (2.88). 5.8 APG (3rd in Duke history). 1.8 SPG (5th).

Then COVID cancelled March Madness. No tournament. No championship chase. The season ended in silence. Tre declared for the draft.
bio.after:
Drafted 41st overall by San Antonio in 2020 — a second-round pick, lower than his talent warranted. G League first: named to the 2020–21 All-G League Team. Earned minutes gradually as Dejounte Murray’s backup, then as a starter when Murray was traded in 2022.

As a starter: 13.5 PPG on 48.8% shooting, 7.5 APG, just 1.1 turnovers — the family DNA. April 2, 2023: first career triple-double (17/11/10) in overtime vs Sacramento. It came sixteen days after Tyus recorded his own first triple-double — against Tre’s Spurs.

Traded to Chicago in February 2025. Signed a 3-year, $24M contract in June 2025. Five years in, Tre Jones is a reliable starting-caliber point guard — not a star, but a professional, the kind of player teams trust with the ball.
bio.now:
Tre is 25. Plays for the Chicago Bulls. Married Maddy Torres in 2023. Three daughters. Plans to study kinesiology, inspired by brother Jadee, who studied it in college and now trains players and coaches at Apple Valley High and Minnehaha Academy.

When their mother was diagnosed with breast cancer in 2019, Tre was a sophomore at Duke. He and Tyus came together around her fight. After she beat the disease, they turned it into a cause. Tre partnered with the Susan G. Komen Foundation. Donated proceeds from pink sneakers. Promoted the More Than Pink Walk in San Antonio. He and Tyus collaborated on family advocacy videos. The brothers raised by a single mother found a way to fight the disease that tried to take her.

He was born four years after Tyus, into the same family, the same town, the same gymnasium. He built a Duke shrine in his bedroom at ten. Heard ‘How does your game compare?’ at every turn and became the first player in ACC history to win Player of the Year and Defensive Player of the Year in the same season. Came back for a second year when his brother left after one — not because he wasn’t good enough to go, but because he wanted to lead his own team. Hit a buzzer-beater at Chapel Hill. COVID stole his March. Second-round pick who earned a starting job and a $24M contract. Got his first triple-double sixteen days after his brother got his.

The Jones family produced two NBA point guards from a North Dakota point guard who raised them alone. Tyus was the trailblazer. Tre was the one who had to walk the trail his brother blazed and make it his own. He did. The shrine told you where he was going. The ACC trophies told you he’d arrived. And the pink sneakers told you what mattered most.

players.name: Paolo Banchero
players.tagline: #1 pick. Coach K’s last lottery star.
players.now: Franchise player, Orlando Magic; 2x All-Star; NBA ROY 2023; Italian & U.S. dual citizen
players.stat: 1 Duke season • 17.2 PPG/7.8 RPG/3.2 APG • ACC Freshman of Year • Final Four • NBA ROY

bio.road:
There was a photograph hanging in the basement of the Banchero family home in Seattle. A USA Basketball team photo from the 1990s. One of the players was Paolo’s mother. His bedroom was in the basement. He saw it every day. ‘That was literally one of my biggest dreams as a kid, to play for USA.’

Paolo Banchero was born November 12, 2002, in Seattle. His mother, Rhonda Smith-Banchero, played college basketball at Washington, where she left as the all-time leading scorer with 2,948 points. She was the first Washington player drafted by the WNBA (3rd round, Sacramento Monarchs, 2000). Played professionally in the ABL and overseas. Husky Hall of Fame 2004. Became the basketball coach at Holy Names Academy in Seattle.

His father, Mario, played tight end at Washington. Mario’s Italian ancestors immigrated from northern Italy in the early 1900s and settled in Black Diamond, Washington — a small mining town in the Cascade foothills — working as farmers and coal miners. Rhonda and Mario met at Washington, married July 31, 1999. Three children: Paolo, daughter Mia (soccer player), youngest son Giulio (signed for Eastern Washington football). With a father named Mario, an uncle named Angelo, and a brother named Giulio: ‘I like having a unique name. When you think of Paolo, you think of me.’

Rhonda made him practice with the girls’ team at Holy Names to keep him humble. ‘She’s always been the one to humble me. Never too high, never too low.’ He never saw her play — she’d retired three years before he was born — but watched enough tape to recognize her game in his own. They watched WNBA games together.

In February 2020, he received Italian citizenship. Italy offered him a spot on their senior national team before college. He’d been cut from USA Basketball youth teams, which left him frustrated. But the photo in the basement kept pulling him back. He visited Italy for the first time in 2023: ‘You know how you get butterflies? It was like that. I was so proud.’

At O’Dea High School (his father’s school): backup QB on the state championship football team as a freshman; led basketball to the 3A state title as a sophomore. Washington Gatorade POY. Most expected him to stay home at Washington. Instead he committed to Duke, 2,341 miles away.
bio.duke:
Banchero arrived for Coach K’s final season, 2021–22 — the farewell tour. He was its centerpiece. College debut: 22 points vs Kentucky. Then 28 vs The Citadel, 21 vs Gonzaga, 23 vs Virginia Tech. Averaged 17.2 PPG, 7.8 RPG, 3.2 APG. ACC Freshman of the Year. Third-Team All-American. Physical, skilled, poised — a 6-10 forward with point-guard passing and a scorer’s mentality.

Duke went 32–7 and reached the Final Four. The semifinal against North Carolina — Duke’s greatest rival — was supposed to be the storybook ending for Krzyzewski. It wasn’t. Carolina won 81–77. Banchero had 20 points and 10 rebounds in his final college game. Coach K’s career ended one round short of the championship.
bio.after:
Draft night, June 23, 2022: every mock predicted the Magic would take Jabari Smith Jr. Instead they called Banchero’s name. The surprise was visible on his face. He made doubters look foolish immediately: 27 points, 9 rebounds, 5 assists in his debut vs Detroit — the first player since LeBron to post 25/5/5 in an NBA debut. Scored 20+ forty times as a rookie. NBA Rookie of the Year 2022–23 — the first Magic player to win since Shaquille O’Neal in 1993.

Summer 2023: chose the USA photo over the Italian passport. Represented Team USA at the FIBA World Cup. Led the team with 21 points in 19 minutes vs New Zealand. Americans lost to Germany in the semifinals by two points.

By his second season: All-Star. By his third: the franchise. The Magic built around him alongside Franz Wagner and Jalen Suggs. Averaged 28+ early in 2024–25 before an oblique injury. Everything in Orlando runs through Paolo.
bio.now:
Paolo Banchero is 22. Franchise player of the Orlando Magic. In November 2025, he joined the ownership group of the Seattle Seawolves rugby team — a hometown investment. He also holds a stake in AC Milan, bridging his two identities across an ocean. He carries an Italian passport and wears the American flag. He told his father Italy needs to be a regular family trip.

Rhonda still coaches girls’ basketball at Holy Names Academy and works at a nonprofit serving homeless people. Mario has served on Seattle district councils. Mia plays soccer. Giulio signed for Eastern Washington football. The Banchero family is what happens when Italian immigrants who mined coal in Black Diamond marry into a lineage of African American athletes from Franklin High School — and their children grow up bilingual in competition, humble enough to practice with the girls’ team and proud enough to carry two passports.

He was born in Seattle. His mother was the greatest scorer in Huskies women’s history. His great-grandparents dug coal out of the Cascades. He was three feet tall at fifteen months. His mother made him practice with the girls’ team. He got Italian citizenship at seventeen and almost played for Italy because USA Basketball had cut him. But there was a photograph in the basement where he slept, and it became the biggest dream of his childhood.

He chose Duke because Coach K was the greatest. He was the centerpiece of Krzyzewski’s final season. Lost to Carolina in the Final Four. Surprise #1 pick. Scored 27/9/5 in his debut. Rookie of the Year. Chose the red, white, and blue over the green, white, and red because of a photograph in a basement. He is 22 years old and he is the future of the Orlando Magic — the kid with two countries, two passports, two parents who played Division I sports at the same university, and one photograph that told him exactly who he was going to become.

players.name: Kyle Filipowski
players.tagline: Two years in the post-K era.
players.now: Utah Jazz; 4-year/$12M contract; Scheyer era’s first cornerstone
players.stat: 2 Duke seasons • 70 games • Fr: 15.1 PPG/9.0 RPG (16 double-doubles) • ACC ROY • ACC Tourney MVP • 1st-Team All-ACC

bio.road:
Kyle Jarred Filipowski was born November 7, 2003, in Middletown, New York. His family was built on basketball the way some families are built on farming — the language spoken at the dinner table, the reason the car was always pointed toward a gymnasium.

His mother, Rebecca Hagerdon Filipowski, was a 6-4 force at Warwick Valley High School who scored 2,164 career points and was a 1982 Parade All-American. She played at Long Beach State. Knee injuries ended her career; she became a coach and teacher. Her sister Beverly played at Dartmouth. Her brother Randall at Boston College. His father David played at Slippery Rock University. David and Rebecca met at Warwick Valley. Four sons: Daniel, Taylor, and twins Kyle and Matthew.

Rebecca was Kyle’s first coach. She taught fundamentals, drove to AAU tournaments, eventually left teaching to invest in her sons’ development. David retired early to attend games. The family organized around basketball like a military family around deployments.

Kyle and twin Matt were inseparable on the court, playing together at Wilbraham & Monson Academy. Matt, also 6-11, committed to Harvard. Three high schools in five years: Minisink Valley (freshman), Fordham Prep (sophomore), Wilbraham & Monson (junior/senior). Each move elevated competition. In 99 prep games: 1,712 points, 973 rebounds. 2021 Gatorade Massachusetts POY. Naismith All-America first team. Also played baseball and water polo. Magna Cum Laude academic award. Won gold at the 2021 FIBA 3x3 U18 World Cup.

Committed to Duke July 29, 2021 — the first year of Jon Scheyer’s era. Filipowski would be the new coach’s first cornerstone.
bio.duke:
Filipowski arrived for Scheyer’s first season, 2022–23. The legend had retired. The banners hung in Cameron but the man who put them there was gone. Filipowski was asked to bridge the eras.

Freshman: 15.1 PPG, 9.0 RPG. Led all D-I freshmen with 16 double-doubles. 28 points/15 rebounds vs Pittsburgh. 22/13 vs North Carolina. In the ACC Tournament final vs Virginia: 20 and 10 — named Tournament MVP. ACC Rookie of the Year. A throwback big with modern versatility: post scoring, three-point shooting, high-post passing.

Despite being projected first round in the 2023 draft, Filipowski came back. A bet on himself and the program. In an era when the best freshmen almost always leave, he returned. Scheyer’s first cornerstone was also his most loyal.

Sophomore: 16.4 PPG, 8.3 RPG, 2.9 APG. First-Team All-ACC. Consensus second-team All-American. Career-high 30 vs Georgia Tech. Duke went 27–9.

On February 24, 2024, after Wake Forest upset Duke, the crowd stormed the court. Filipowski was struck by a rushing fan and suffered an apparent knee injury. The collision ignited a national conversation about court-storming safety. Scheyer called for a ban. Filipowski played four days later. The toughness was real.
bio.after:
Filipowski declared for the 2024 draft as a projected first-round pick. He received a green room invitation. He sat through the entire first round. His name was never called.

Reports connected the slide to concerns about his personal life — his relationship with his fiancée, Caitlin Hutchison, who is several years older, and his estrangement from the family that had organized its entire life around his basketball career. The personal swallowed the professional on draft night.

The Utah Jazz selected him 32nd overall. They stated they’d done a thorough background check and were excited regardless of the noise. He signed a 4-year, $12M contract on August 12, 2024. Rookie season: splitting time between the Jazz and their G League affiliate, the Salt Lake City Stars. Early returns: 6.3 PPG, 4.9 RPG, 1.9 APG with Utah. The tools are there. The development is ongoing.
bio.now:
Kyle Filipowski is 21 years old. He plays for the Utah Jazz. His twin brother Matt plays basketball at Harvard. His mother Rebecca, who scored 2,164 points at Warwick Valley and was his first coach, is no longer part of his daily life the way she once was. The family that organized itself around his career has fractured. The details belong to the Filipowskis. What is public is the pain.

What is also public: two seasons at Duke. 70 games. Sixteen double-doubles as a freshman — more than any freshman in D-I. ACC ROY. ACC Tournament MVP. Came back when the money said leave. First-Team All-ACC. Consensus All-American. Knocked down by a fan during a court-storming and played four days later. The first cornerstone of Scheyer’s Duke.

He was born in Middletown. His mother was a Parade All-American who taught him the game. His aunt at Dartmouth, his uncle at Boston College. He and his twin played side by side. Three high schools. Magna Cum Laude. Gold medal with USA Basketball. Chose Duke and became the best freshman in the country. Came back when he could have left. Got knocked down and got back up. Sat in the green room and watched thirty-one names called before his. Now he’s in Utah, twenty-one, building the career his family spent two decades preparing him for — navigating the distance between where he ended up and where he started. The cost of ambition is sometimes measured in that distance. Kyle Filipowski is still calculating.

players.name: Dereck Lively II
players.tagline: Born in Philadelphia. Raised in Bellefonte. Coached by his mother. Lost her two months before the Finals.
players.now: Dallas Mavericks; NBA Finals 2024; recovering from foot surgery
players.stat: 1 Duke season • 34 games • 5.2 PPG/5.4 RPG/2.4 BPG • 65.8% FG • 82 blocks (led all D-I freshmen)

bio.road:
Dereck Jerome Lively II was born February 12, 2004, in Philadelphia. His mother, Kathy Phillips Drysdale, was a 6-4 standout at Penn State (1988–92): 1,295 points, 717 rebounds, 89 blocks, part of the Lady Lions team that reached #1 in the AP poll in 1991. She spent 13 years with the Philadelphia 76ers in game operations. His father, Dereck Sr., was a Philadelphia-born chef — boisterous, funny, full of life. But he battled drug addiction.

In 2011, Kathy moved the family to Bellefonte, PA, near State College, for a marketing role at Penn State. On January 5, 2012, five weeks before Dereck’s eighth birthday, his father died of an overdose. The boy walked to the stairs and peered down. He saw his father on the floor, black and blue, lifeless.

Not long after, Kathy told her nine-year-old she had Hodgkin’s lymphoma. She would fight it for eleven years — through chemotherapy, a stem cell transplant, a brief remission — while coaching her son in AAU ball, driving to every tournament, and working full-time at Penn State.

A HoopGroup event gave Dereck his first exposure. Team Final (Nike EYBL) recruited him. Kathy found a documentary called We Town about Westtown School’s basketball program (Mo Bamba, Cam Reddish) and made him watch it. He agreed to attend the boarding school three hours from home. An ankle injury cost him freshman year. COVID sent him back to Bellefonte, where he worked out daily at the local YMCA with his mother, adding muscle and an outside shot. When he returned, Kathy didn’t recognize him.

Senior year: #1 recruit nationally (ESPN 100). 14 PPG/14 RPG/4.5 BPG. PAISAA state championship. McDonald’s All-American. PA Gatorade POY. Chose Duke over Kentucky, Michigan, and his mother’s Penn State.
bio.duke:
Lively arrived as the headliner of a #1 nationally ranked class. The stat line looked nothing like what people expected: 5.2 PPG. But the impact was deafening.

He led all D-I freshmen with 82 blocks. Shot 65.8% from the field. His 7-7 wingspan made him a nightmare at the rim. ACC All-Freshman. ACC All-Defensive. In March Madness: 5.8 PPG, 7.4 RPG, 3.0 BPG on 76.5% shooting. The deeper the tournament went, the bigger he played. Duke won the ACC Tournament championship.

Coach Scheyer later told CBS Sports the summary: Lively was the most impactful defensive player in the country by the end of the season. He declared for the draft after one season.
bio.after:
Dallas selected Lively 12th overall — a pick born from an organizational tank that drew a $750K fine. The payoff was immediate. Rookie year: 55 games, 42 starts, 8.8 PPG/6.9 RPG/1.4 BPG on 74.7% shooting (would have led the NBA). All-Rookie Second Team. Dallas’s defense jumped from 21st to 6th.

His mother moved to Dallas in November 2023. She sat behind the bench. She attended community functions. On April 12, 2024, Kathy Drysdale died at age 53, surrounded by her son, her sisters, and the Mavericks organization. She’d fought cancer for 11 years. Before she died, she told him: ‘Dear Dereck, you made it. Persevered, but you are not done yet.’

Two months later, he was in the NBA Finals. In Game 4, down 3-0, Luka found Lively alone in the corner. He hadn’t attempted a three all postseason. He launched a moonshot that splashed through the net. Dallas won 122-84. He posted back-to-back double-doubles — the first rookie to do so since Magic Johnson in 1980. When asked about the three, he said his mother helped him make it. She was above him, guiding the ball.
bio.now:
Dereck Lively II is 21. In his second season (2024–25), he averaged 9.1 PPG/7.8 RPG/2.6 APG in 32 games before a stress fracture, then foot surgery in December 2025 ruled him out. The injuries test patience. He’s been tested before.

He is an only child. His father died when he was seven. His mother was diagnosed with cancer when he was nine. She coached him in a YMCA gym while undergoing chemo. She moved to Dallas to watch him play. She died two months before the Finals. The Mavericks’ statement said it best: so much of Kathy — her strength, tenacity, and joy — is reflected in her son.

Every game Dereck Lively II plays is a game played for two. His mother told him he wasn’t even close to being done. He’s not. He’s twenty-one years old, and there are so many more three-pointers to guide through the rim.

players.name: Cooper Flagg
players.tagline: Born in Newport, Maine. Raised by headlights. The #1 pick.
players.now: Dallas Mavericks; #1 overall pick 2025 NBA Draft; 2nd-youngest #1 pick in history
players.stat: 1 Duke season • 37 games • 19.2 PPG/7.5 RPG/4.2 APG/1.4 SPG/1.3 BPG • Naismith + Wooden Awards

bio.road:
In a house three miles from the one where she would raise him, Kelly Bowman fell in love with basketball at age seven. Her father Dan affixed a hoop to a shed in their backyard in Newport, Maine — population 3,200. They played so often the grass faded into dirt. When it got dark, he’d park a car and turn on the headlights so they could keep shooting.

Kelly starred at Nokomis Regional High School, then earned a D-I scholarship to the University of Maine (team captain, three-time conference champion). Ralph Flagg also attended Nokomis, played at Eastern Maine Community College. They met at a community center basketball game. He couldn’t stop her up-and-under move. As a wedding present, they received a crabapple tree and planted it next to the driveway hoop.

In 2004, Kelly went into premature labor at 24 weeks. Twins Hunter and Ryder were born at Maine Medical Center. Hunter: 1 lb 10 oz. Ryder: two ounces lighter. Ryder died two days later. Hunter spent 109 days in the NICU. Kelly refused to leave Portland. The Ronald McDonald House became home.

Cooper and Ace Flagg were born premature on December 21, 2006, but healthy enough to go home for Christmas. The household was organized around basketball. Kelly coached Nokomis girls’ varsity; the boys grew up in the gym. Driveway games ended when someone ran inside bleeding. Cooper played in a 4th-grade rec league as a 2nd-grader.

In March 2022, all three Flagg brothers — Hunter a senior, Cooper and Ace freshmen — led Nokomis to its first state championship. Cooper: 22 pts/16 reb in the title game. First freshman named Maine Gatorade POY (20.5/10/6.2/3.7/3.7). Then Montverde Academy. FIBA U17 gold (17 reb vs Spain in the final, USA Basketball Male Athlete of the Year at 15). Reclassified from 2025 to 2024, skipping junior year.

Planned to announce his Duke commitment October 27, 2023. Delayed three days after the Lewiston shootings — 18 killed in his home state. Committed October 30. Signed with New Balance because two factories are near Newport. First men’s college basketball player sponsored by Gatorade. Invited to join USA Select Team at 17, scored up to 17 pts against LeBron James, Curry, and Durant. KD: ‘He’s 17 years old playing like a veteran.’
bio.duke:
College debut November 4, 2024: 18 pts/7 reb/5 ast against the University of Maine — his mother’s alma mater.

19.2 PPG, 7.5 RPG, 4.2 APG, 1.4 SPG, 1.3 BPG. Led Duke in all five major categories — the only freshman in Wooden Award history to do so. 48.3% FG, 37.4% 3PT, 83.6% FT. 709 points (20th in Duke single-season history). ACC Rookie of the Week a record 12 times.

January 11, 2025: 42 points vs Notre Dame — Duke and ACC freshman single-game scoring record (11-14 FG, 4-6 3PT, 16-17 FT, 6 reb, 7 ast). First ACC player in 25 years with 500+ pts, 100+ ast, 30+ blk in a regular season.

Duke 29-4, 19-1 ACC (regular season + tournament champions). NCAA Tournament: 14/18/30/16 pts through four rounds. Final Four vs Houston: 27 pts/7 reb/4 ast/3 blk/2 stl — first player since 1986 to lead his team in all five categories in a Final Four game. Duke lost 70-67.

Naismith Award. Wooden Award. AP POY. USBWA POY. NABC POY. ACC POY + ROY (4th to win both, joining Zion, Bagley, Okafor). Consensus 1st-Team All-American. One of the five best one-and-done seasons in history.
bio.after:
Selected #1 overall by Dallas Mavericks in the 2025 NBA Draft. Age 18 years, 186 days — second-youngest #1 pick in history, 8 days older than LeBron James in 2003.

Before the draft, he didn’t fly to LA for workouts. He went home to Maine. Worked out at a D-III gym at Colby College with his twin brother. Drove to Newport to watch his classmates graduate from Nokomis — the ceremony that should have been his if he hadn’t reclassified.

In December 2024, the Flagg family donated $10,000 to the Ronald McDonald House in Durham. Kelly cried telling the story of Hunter and Ryder. The twins born premature in Portland — one who survived, one who didn’t, 109 days in the NICU — had grown into a young man giving back to the place that gave his family a home during the worst days of their lives.
bio.now:
Cooper Flagg is 18. He plays for the Dallas Mavericks. Twin brother Ace plays at the University of Maine. Hunter is studying sports management. Ryder, who lived for two days, is remembered every time the family walks through the doors of a Ronald McDonald House.

Kelly Bowman Flagg, who shot baskets by headlight in a town of 3,200 people, who captained Maine, who planted a crabapple tree next to the driveway hoop — watched her son become the #1 pick. Ralph watched beside her.

He signed with New Balance because of Maine. He delayed his commitment because of Lewiston. He went home before the draft to watch his classmates graduate. He scored 42 against Notre Dame. He led his team in every category in a Final Four game. He won every major national award. He is eighteen.

The story begins with headlights. A father parks his car and turns on the high beams so his seven-year-old daughter can keep shooting after dark. Forty years later, that girl’s son is the first pick in the NBA Draft. The headlights are still on. Cooper Flagg is just getting started.

players.name: Kon Knueppel
players.tagline: Five brothers. Five trophies. A Nintendo Wii taught him to love basketball. Milwaukee’s finest shooter.
players.now: Charlotte Hornets; 4th overall pick 2025 NBA Draft; Summer League Champion + MVP; ROY frontrunner
players.stat: 1 Duke season • 39 starts • 14.4 PPG/4.0 RPG/2.7 APG • 40.6% 3PT • 91.4% FT • ACC Tournament MVP

bio.road:
Kon Knueppel II was born August 3, 2005, in Milwaukee, Wisconsin, into a house with six basketball hoops scattered around the property. His father, also named Kon, had scored 2,064 career points at Wisconsin Lutheran College — the school’s all-time leading scorer — and was a four-time All-Lake Michigan Conference selection. His mother, Chari Nordgaard Knueppel, had scored 1,964 career points at the University of Wisconsin-Green Bay — the program’s all-time leading scorer. She was an All-Horizon League member every season, played in two NCAA Tournaments, played professionally in Greece, and was inducted into UW-Green Bay’s Hall of Fame in 2004. Her number 33 was retired in January 2025 during a ceremony she flew in for between her son’s Duke games.

The basketball bloodlines ran deeper than even Kon and Chari. Chari’s brother, Jeff Nordgaard, starred at UW-Green Bay before being drafted by the Milwaukee Bucks in the second round in 1996. He played 13 NBA games and spent thirteen years playing professionally overseas. Chari’s maternal grandfather, Arthur “Bud” Rose, played basketball at North Carolina State in 1931–32 and earned Honorable Mention All-American honors — the first All-American in NC State history. The Knueppel-Nordgaard family tree reads like a basketball encyclopedia stretching back nearly a century.

On Kon’s father’s side, there were the uncles: Klint, Klay, and Kole. They competed alongside Kon Sr. in the Gus Macker 3-on-3 Basketball Tournament as a team called the “Flying Knueppel Brothers” — having rejected the alternative name, “Free the Knueppel.” Uncle Kole became a college football referee in the Big Ten.

Kon is the oldest of five boys. His brothers, in order: Kager, Kinston, Kash, and Kidman. All five play basketball. When Kon committed to Duke in November 2023, he posted a photo on Instagram of himself and his four younger brothers standing inside the Duke locker room. Each boy was holding one of Duke’s five national championship trophies. Five brothers. Five trophies.

But here is the part nobody expects. When Kon was five and six, he was not into sports at all. His uncle Jeff Nordgaard, the former NBA player, remembers being surprised. Little Kon was bookish, using vocabulary beyond his age. The family was starting to think he might be a non-athlete — and then his parents bought a Nintendo Wii. It was the family’s first video game console. Kon played Wii Sports basketball obsessively. The virtual game awakened something. His parents enrolled him in a kindergarten league. He didn’t score a single point in his first ten games. He ducked when teammates passed him the ball. The all-time leading scorers at Wisconsin Lutheran College and UW-Green Bay had produced a child who couldn’t catch a bounce pass.

Kon Sr. ran a men’s league in Milwaukee — had been running it for twenty years. In eighth grade, Kon II started playing in it. Among the regulars: Ben Brust, a former Wisconsin Badger and the program’s all-time three-point leader. Brust kept beating Kon on backdoor cuts. By senior year, Kon was the one doing the cutting. The men’s league taught him what no AAU circuit could.

Chari ran a daycare from the family home since 2006. Kon Sr. worked as a school counselor in Milwaukee public schools. Six hoops on the property. Five boys. Two parents who were all-time leading scorers. Blue-collar Milwaukee.

At Wisconsin Lutheran High School: 10.8 PPG as a freshman, 19.7 as a junior, 26.4 as a senior. Perfect 30–0 record, WIAA Division 2 State Championship. Wisconsin Mr. Basketball. Gatorade Wisconsin POY. Also played baritone in the school band. He chose Duke because: “I want to play against the best players every day in practice, because that will make me better.” Enrolled June 2024. Roommate: Cooper Flagg.
bio.duke:
Knueppel started all 39 games as a freshman — tying Duke’s single-season record for starts by a freshman. 14.4 PPG, 4.0 RPG, 2.7 APG. Shot 47.9% from the field, 40.6% from three, and 91.4% from the free-throw line — highest FT% in the ACC, second-highest by a Duke freshman in history (only J.J. Redick shot better). Made 38 consecutive free throws from February through March.

Cooper Flagg was the headliner. Knueppel was the other half. Flagg was impossible to keep out of the lane. Knueppel was a lethal shooter who made the high-IQ plays he’d learned from years in his father’s men’s league. Scheyer: “Their personalities were perfect for each other — competitive, unselfish, and smart.”

One preseason practice, Flagg didn’t have his best stuff. The guy who called him out? Knueppel. He told the future number-one pick to get his head in the game. Scheyer’s staff still points to that moment.

Then the ACC Tournament. Flagg sprained his ankle in the quarterfinal and missed the rest. Knueppel stepped into the spotlight: 21.0 PPG, 5.7 RPG, 4.7 APG across the tournament. 18 points and 8 rebounds in the title game against Louisville. ACC Tournament MVP. Nobody cheered harder for him than Flagg on the sideline.

The same week, back in Wisconsin, brother Kager scored 10 points in the WIAA Division 1 state title game. Wisconsin Lutheran won back-to-back championships in a higher division. Mother Chari flew between Raleigh and Madison, refusing to miss a game from any son. She attended every Duke game in person that season. Every one.

NCAA Tournament: 20.5 PPG in the Sweet 16 and Elite Eight. 16 points in the Final Four against Houston. East Region All-Tournament Team. Season line: 84 three-pointers, 117-of-128 free throws, and the quiet, lethal efficiency that made every scout say: this kid is going to play in the NBA for a long time.
bio.after:
Charlotte selected Knueppel 4th overall in the 2025 NBA Draft. On draft night, Cooper Flagg was mid-interview on NBA TV when the pick was announced. Flagg shot out of his seat — the most animated he’d been all evening, including when his own name was called first. The former roommates, the freshman duo who pushed each other every day, were both top-five picks.

Knueppel led the Hornets to the 2025 NBA Summer League championship, scoring 21 in the title game. Summer League Championship MVP. Regular-season debut October 22: 11 points and 5 rebounds vs Brooklyn. By early November: a 30-point game against Miami, Eastern Conference Player of the Week nomination. On November 14, he scored 32 in an overtime loss against Milwaukee — his hometown team, the franchise his uncle Jeff had once played for — hitting two late threes to force OT. Averaging 19.2 PPG, 5.5 RPG, 3.4 APG. Alongside Flagg, the two former roommates are frontrunners for NBA Rookie of the Year.
bio.now:
Kon Knueppel is 19 years old. He plays for the Charlotte Hornets. His former roommate Cooper Flagg plays for the Dallas Mavericks. The two are the top candidates for NBA Rookie of the Year.

Back in Milwaukee, the Knueppel machine keeps running. Kager, a 6-8 sophomore, has D-I offers and led Wisconsin Lutheran to a second consecutive state title. Kinston is generating recruiting buzz. Kash and Kidman won the national Lutheran grade-school basketball tournament in Fort Wayne, Indiana. Chari is retiring from the daycare to focus full-time on five sons and their schedules. Kon Sr. still runs the men’s league. The six hoops are still on the property.

He was born in Milwaukee into a house with six basketball hoops. His father scored 2,064 points at Wisconsin Lutheran College. His mother scored 1,964 at UW-Green Bay. His uncle was drafted by the Bucks. His great-grandfather was NC State’s first All-American in 1932. His father’s brothers called themselves the Flying Knueppel Brothers. He didn’t score a point in his first ten basketball games because a Nintendo Wii had to teach him to love the sport first. He learned to play off the ball from a former Badger in his father’s men’s league. He played baritone in the high school band. He led Wisconsin Lutheran to a perfect season. He chose Duke because he wanted to practice against the best. He was Cooper Flagg’s roommate. He told the future number-one pick to get his head in the game. He won ACC Tournament MVP when Flagg went down. He was the fourth pick. He scored 32 against his hometown Bucks. And when he committed to Duke, he posted a photo: five brothers, each holding one of the program’s five national championship trophies. Five brothers. Five trophies. One family. The Knueppels don’t just play basketball. They are basketball.

players.name: Jared McCain
players.tagline: One-and-done. Rookie of the Year candidate.

players.name: Cameron Boozer
players.tagline: Born via IVF to save his brother’s life. Four state titles. First since LeBron to win Mr. Basketball USA twice. Carlos Boozer’s son — but now Carlos is known as Cameron’s dad.
players.now: Duke Blue Devils freshman; projected top pick 2026 NBA Draft; 2x Gatorade National POY; 2x Mr. Basketball USA
players.stat: HS: 4 consecutive FL state titles • 2025 Chipotle Nationals Champions • 2x Gatorade National POY • 2x Mr. Basketball USA (first since LeBron) • FIBA U16 + U17 MVP

bio.road:
The story of Cameron Boozer begins before he was born — and it begins with someone else’s survival.

On May 31, 2006, Carlos Boozer and his wife CeCe received the diagnosis that would reshape their family. Their firstborn son, Carmani, had sickle cell disease. Carlos had just signed a six-year, $70 million contract with the Utah Jazz. He was a two-time NBA All-Star, a Duke championship hero, a man who had grown up in Juneau, Alaska. None of it mattered. His son was sick.

Sickle cell disease is a genetic blood disorder in which red blood cells become rigid and crescent-shaped, blocking blood flow and causing episodes of extreme pain, organ damage, and potentially death. Both Carlos and CeCe carried the sickle cell trait. Their best hope was a bone marrow transplant from a sibling’s umbilical cord blood. But they couldn’t simply conceive and hope — if their next child also carried the disease, it would mean two sick children. They needed a child who was both free of sickle cell and a perfect bone marrow match.

Carlos and CeCe chose in-vitro fertilization. Doctors harvested 34 eggs from CeCe. Ten became viable embryos. Only two were both sickle-cell-free and perfect bone marrow matches. Both were implanted. Both survived. On July 18, 2007, in Salt Lake City, Cameron Sikandar Boozer and his fraternal twin brother Cayden were born. They began their lives saving the life of their brother.

Three weeks later, stem cells from the twins’ umbilical cords were transplanted into Carmani at Miami Children’s Hospital. Forty nights in the hospital. It worked. Carmani was cured. ESPN documented the story in 2008 and returned in 2024 for Blood Brothers.

Cameron grew up in Miami. His parents divorced in 2015 but both remained present. CeCe had met Carlos when she was at NC State and Carlos was at Duke. She was 5-10, a D-I volleyball player. Carlos was 6-9, a two-time All-Star, Olympic gold medalist. At fourteen, the twins looked like they’d been built in a basketball lab. Carlos said the light bulb went on in junior high. CeCe said she knew by the eighth grade.

At Christopher Columbus High School: Cameron was the 6-9 power forward, Cayden the 6-4 point guard who knew where his brother wanted the ball before Cameron did. Sophomore year: 21.1 PPG/11.3 RPG/4.2 APG/2.0 BPG, state championship. Four consecutive Florida state titles. 2025 Chipotle Nationals crown. Cameron was named Gatorade National POY as a sophomore (2023) and again as a senior (2025). Mr. Basketball USA in 2023 and 2025 — the first player to win it twice since LeBron James. FIBA U16 Americas MVP (2023, leading USA to gold by 82 over Canada). FIBA U17 World Cup MVP (2024). The most fundamentally sound player in high school basketball.

The commitment came October 11, 2024. Cameron and Cayden chose Duke together, over Miami and Florida. The video featured Carmani — the brother whose life they’d saved. Carlos told ESPN: ‘I’m proud of them. They’ve worked their butts off.’ Then he shared what defines how far the family has come: ‘Hey, Mr. Boozer, you’re Cameron and Cayden’s dad? I get that all the time now.’ The two-time All-Star is now recognized for what his children are becoming. And he loves it.
bio.duke:
Cameron enrolled at Duke for the 2025–26 season alongside Cayden. They are the centerpiece of Scheyer’s next recruiting class — the follow-up to Flagg and Knueppel. Cameron was the #2 overall recruit in the 2025 class.

His freshman season is being written. What he brings to Durham is already clear: a 6-9 power forward with elite scoring touch, a rebounder who attacks the glass like his father, a passer with unusual vision for his size, and a winner who has never known a season without a championship. Four state titles. Two national MVPs. Two FIBA gold medals. He is eighteen, and his resume already reads like a career.

What makes Cameron different from his father at the same age is the modern game. Carlos at Duke was a low-post scorer, a physical rebounder built for the paint. Cameron has the same frame but plays facing the basket. He can shoot from the perimeter, handle in transition, run pick-and-roll as screener or initiator. The NBA he’s preparing for is different from the one his father played in.

Cayden is beside him — the same chemistry they’ve shared since they were toddlers wearing Bulls jerseys at Heat games. The connection is almost telepathic. Cayden doesn’t have to look. He knows where Cameron is.
bio.after:
Cameron Boozer’s Duke career is just beginning. He is projected as a top pick in the 2026 NBA Draft — mock drafts have placed him in the top three since he was a sophomore in high school. His combination of size, skill, basketball intelligence, and pedigree makes him one of the most valuable prospects in amateur basketball.

The NBA story will come. What matters now is the Duke story — and the family story. Cameron will play in Cameron Indoor Stadium, the same court where his father scored, rebounded, and won a national championship in 2001. He will wear Duke blue as the son of a Brotherhood legend, alongside his twin brother, the one conceived through the same act of medical science and parental love, delivered into the world with the same purpose: to save Carmani’s life and then to build lives of their own.
bio.now:
Cameron Boozer is 18 years old. He is a freshman at Duke. His twin Cayden is his teammate. His older brother Carmani — cured of sickle cell disease by stem cells from the twins’ umbilical cords — is 19, a freshman pitcher at the University of Fort Lauderdale, living a full life that exists because of a decision his parents made in 2006. Carlos works as an ACC Network analyst and recently joined the Utah Jazz front office. CeCe remains deeply involved.

The parents who drove a 1985 Dodge van from Washington, D.C., to Juneau, Alaska, because a six-year-old boy was shot on a basketball court — Carlos Sr. and Renee — moved back to Juneau. The family born from fear produced an NBA All-Star, an Olympic gold medalist, a medical miracle, and two of the most celebrated high school basketball players in the country.

Cameron was born in Salt Lake City on July 18, 2007. His umbilical cord blood contained the stem cells that saved his brother’s life. He was three weeks old when they were transplanted into Carmani. He didn’t know. He couldn’t know. He was a newborn. Seventeen years later, he is a Duke Blue Devil, a four-time state champion, a two-time national player of the year, a two-time Mr. Basketball USA, and the son of a man who now gets recognized as ‘Cameron and Cayden’s dad.’ The story of Cameron Boozer is a story about basketball, but it is first and forever a story about blood. The blood that was sick. The blood that healed it. And the brotherhood — the real one, between three brothers in Miami — that made all of it possible.

players.name: Cayden Boozer
players.tagline: Carlos’s son. The legacy continues.
